doc: Early Chicago Sewerage History, 1850s–1900
text: Between 1855 and 1900 Chicago built the first comprehensive, planned underground sewer system of any large American city . It was a combined system : a single network of pipes designed to receive household wastes ("all stagnant water, all liquids from kitchens and manufactories, and the contents of all privies") plus street surface runoff captured through catch basins at block corners . Flow was entirely by gravity — Chesbrough explicitly rejected deep sewers requiring pumps — so the pipes had to sit near or above the natural ground surface of a city that stood only a few feet above lake level . What flowed in: sanitary sewage from house drains (2,194 private house-drain connections existed by January 1861 ), industrial liquid wastes (notably distillery, tannery and packinghouse offal along the river branches ), and stormwater from streets via catch basins . What flowed out, and where: the sewers of all three city divisions discharged primarily into the Chicago River — the South Division draining west into the South Branch and north into the Main Stem, the North Division through mains on Rush, Clark and Franklin Streets into the Main Stem and the North Branch, and the West Division into both branches; only one of the two Michigan Avenue mains had an outlet directly into Lake Michigan , at Twelfth Street (the other Michigan Avenue main discharged to the river) . The river in turn emptied into the lake — the same lake from which the city drew its drinking water, initially at an intake near the river mouth, and after 1867 through a two-mile tunnel to an offshore crib . This supply/disposal collision drove every subsequent decision. Downstream re-routing: from 1871 onward the city tried to send the river's sewage load away from the lake and down the Illinois & Michigan Canal at Bridgeport — first by pumping, then by the gravity "deep cut" of 1871, then by rebuilt pumps in the 1880s — so that diluted sewage flowed to the Des Plaines and Illinois Rivers and ultimately the Mississippi . The permanent solution, the Sanitary District's 28-mile Main Drainage Channel (opened January 1900), replaced the canal as the receiving artery and permanently reversed the Main Stem and South Branch . The 1850s–1890s network described here is the direct ancestor of today's City of Chicago combined-sewer system, whose overflows the MWRD's Deep Tunnel (TARP) now intercepts .

doc: Early Chicago Sewerage History, 1850s–1900
text: The crisis. Chicago in the early 1850s was a boomtown on a wet clay flat barely above the level of Lake Michigan; streets were mud sloughs ("No bottom here" read a famous sign) and drainage was by open gutter into a sluggish river . Waterborne disease was chronic: cholera struck in 1849 and repeatedly thereafter, and in 1854 it killed 1,424 people — with another 210 deaths recorded as "diarrhea" and 242 as "dysentery" that may also have been cholera — while the city's overall death rate hit 53.9 per 1,000 . Six consecutive years of cholera and dysentery epidemics pushed the legislature to act . The institution. Illinois had empowered sewage commissioners for the city as early as 1852 — though the actual June 28, 1852 act, per a Department of Public Works retrospective, created a county drainage commission (Henry Smith, Geo. W. Snow, James H. Reed, George Steele, H. L. Stewart, Isaac Cook and Charles V. Dyer) empowered to build ditches, culverts and roads across roughly 100,000 acres of swamp land in Cook County townships 37–40 N, ranges 12–14 E — a regional land-reclamation body (it spent about $100,000 in its first two years) distinct from the city sewer commission that followed three years later ; on February 14, 1855 the legislature created the Board of Sewerage Commissioners — William B. Ogden (Chicago's first mayor), J. D. Webster and Sylvester Lind — empowered to issue up to $500,000 in bonds . The board recruited Ellis Sylvester Chesbrough (1813–1886), then Boston's first city engineer, as chief engineer, with William H. Clark as principal assistant engineer . The plan. Chesbrough delivered his Report and Plan of Sewerage for the City of Chicago on December 26, 1855; the board adopted it December 31, 1855 . He weighed four disposal schemes: (1) drainage into the river and thence to the lake; (2) directly into the lake; (3) into reservoirs to be pumped and sold as farm manure; (4) into "the proposed steamboat canal" to the Illinois River — an uncanny forecast of the 1900 solution, which he rejected in 1855 only because its cost would exceed "sewers for five times the present population" . He chose plan (1) as cheapest, proposing to keep the South Branch tolerable by driving lake water through it . The system was combined and gravity-flow, with circular brick mains of 5 feet (one 6-foot main on Clark Street), 2-foot brick branches, and 12–15-inch glazed pipe laterals; junctions for every present and future house were to be built in from the start; man

doc: Early Chicago Sewerage History, 1850s–1900
text: The 1855-pattern combined sewers are not a museum piece: the City of Chicago's Department of Water Management still operates a combined sewer system whose oldest brick trunk lines date to the Chesbrough era, and by 1930 that system was described as "the most extensive in the world" . Because combined sewers overflow in storms, the 19th-century design choice led directly to the MWRD's Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP/"Deep Tunnel"), whose Phase I pollution-control tunnels went into service in 1985 to capture the overflows the Victorian system sends riverward . The raised street grades remain a permanent feature of the Loop and near-neighborhood topography (vaulted sidewalks, below-grade original floors) . The I&M Canal, sewage carrier of 1871–1900, ceased navigation in the 1930s and is today a National Heritage Corridor, while the Sanitary District's channel and the reversed river remain the region's operating disposal spine under the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District . The Chicago Public Library's Chicago Sewers Collection (13.5 linear feet, 951 photographs, 121 glass-plate slides, including Chesbrough's 1855 plan) is the principal municipal archive of the early system .

doc: Early Chicago Sewerage History, 1850s–1900
text: - Cholera era (1849–1873): epidemics in 1849, 1850 (420 deaths), 1851 (216), 1852 (630), 1854 (1,424 cholera deaths plus contiguous "diarrhea"/"dysentery" mortality) and 1866 (990 deaths, the next major outbreak after the Civil War) — cholera was killing hundreds of Chicagoans annually even before the sewer system existed ; dysentery killed ~1,600 between 1854 and 1860 . Cholera's last Chicago appearance killed 116 in summer 1873 — after which "Chicago's cholera days were over" . - Mortality turnaround: citywide death rate fell from 53.9 per 1,000 (1854) to 18.8 (1860) as sewers, grade raising and water works advanced — a decline Chesbrough himself documented in April 1861 . - The Great Fire (October 1871) destroyed buildings connected with the works and municipal records but left the buried system essentially intact; the legislature's Oct 20, 1871 appropriation of $2,955,340 relieved the canal lien to help the burned-out city . - The August 1885 storm contaminated the water-supply crib for weeks and galvanized the sanitary-district movement . But the associated "epidemic" is a myth: the oft-repeated claim that ~90,000 Chicagoans (12% of the city) died of cholera/typhoid after the 1885 storm has no basis — no cholera deaths occurred, typhoid deaths never exceeded 1,000 in any 1880s year, and researcher Libby Hill's work prompted a Chicago Tribune retraction on September 29, 2005. The tale traces to a 1941 remark by a sewer superintendent and a 1957 Sanitary District pamphlet . - The real typhoid catastrophe came later: 1891, when typhoid fever killed roughly 2,000 Chicagoans — about 174 per 100,000, the city's worst year — threatening the upcoming World's Columbian Exposition and underscoring that the canal-dilution regime had failed . - Population outran everything: Chicago grew from 29,963 (1850) to 112,172 (1860), 298,977 (1870), 503,185 (1880), 1,099,850 (1890) and 1,698,575 (1900) ; by 1893 roughly 1.5 million people drained to the river system, with 270,000 draining straight to the lake along 32 miles of shore . Each engineering fix (1856 sewers, 1871 deep cut, 1880s pumps) was overwhelmed within a decade .

The book itself is inconsistent. Only the Board of Public Works annual report for 1871 could adjudicate which is the audited figure . - Sanitary District referendum date — most likely November 5, 1889: MWRD and Chicagology both give Nov 5, and so does Brown's (1894) own chronological summary table; but Brown's narrative text, quoting Judge Prendergast's order verbatim, gives "November 6, 1889" for the same event — so Brown's book disagrees with itself. November 5, 1889 was the standard Tuesday general-election day that year, which favors Nov 5 as correct and "November 6" in Brown's narrative passage as a transcription error . - Sewer mileage between 1861 and 1892 — partly closed, one more data point added: the Board of Health's 1873 report (source ) tabulates feet of sewer built annually from 1856 through 1872, giving cumulative totals of 850,563 ft (≈161.1 mi) at year-end 1871 (the Fire year) and 893,330 ft (≈169.2 mi) at year-end 1872. An 1891 Dept. of Public Works retrospective adds a further waypoint: 265.80 miles in place at year-end 1876 . The gap between 1877 and Jan. 1, 1892 (888.3 mi) remains open; Board of Public Works / Dept. of Public Works annual reports on HathiTrust for the intervening years would fill the rest of the curve. - Pre-1855 wooden/plank sewers — resolved: an 1891 Dept. of Public Works retrospective identifies Chicago's actual first sewers: triangular wooden box sewers built in 1850 along Clark, La Salle and Wells Streets, from the main river to the alleys south of Randolph Street, costing $2,871.90, paid entirely by special assessment on the property benefited — almost certainly the "old wooden sewers" the 1860 report says complicated construction . - The 1855 plan's southern boundary street — resolved, not an OCR artifact: an independent 1891 Dept. of Public Works retrospective glosses the same 1855 plan boundaries explicitly — "bounded ... on the west by (Reuben street) Ashland avenue, on the south by (North street) Sixteenth street" — confirming "North Street" (soon renamed Sixteenth Street) real

doc: River Reversal, Canals, Locks & Lakefront Controlling Works
text: The Chicago Area Waterway System (CAWS) is a roughly 78-mile network of natural rivers and man-made canals whose defining feature is that it flows backwards : instead of the Chicago and Calumet rivers draining into Lake Michigan, lake water, treated wastewater effluent, and stormwater are drawn away from the lake and sent southwest across the subcontinental divide into the Des Plaines–Illinois–Mississippi river system . What flows in: (a) Lake Michigan water admitted by gravity at three lakefront control points — the Chicago River Controlling Works/Chicago Harbor Lock at the river mouth, the T.J. O'Brien Lock & Dam on the Calumet River, and the Wilmette Pumping Station at the head of the North Shore Channel ; (b) treated effluent from MWRD's water reclamation plants (O'Brien WRP discharges to the North Shore Channel, Stickney WRP to the Sanitary & Ship Canal, Calumet WRP to the Little Calumet near the Cal-Sag) ; (c) stormwater and combined-sewer overflows from the tributary area . What flows out: essentially everything exits at the system's single downstream throat at Lockport, where the MWRD's Lockport Powerhouse and the adjacent Lockport Controlling Works discharge the entire flow of the CAWS into the Des Plaines River just above Joliet, exploiting a ~40-foot drop in elevation . From there water passes the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers' (USACE) Lockport Lock and continues down the Illinois Waterway to the Illinois and Mississippi rivers . Internal connections: the North Shore Channel (Wilmette → North Branch at Foster/Lawrence Ave) feeds the North Branch of the Chicago River; the Main Stem and South Branch feed the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal (CSSC) at Damen Avenue (historic Robey Street); the Cal-Sag Channel carries the reversed Little Calumet/Calumet river flow 16 miles west to "Sag Junction," where it joins the CSSC at canal mile ~12 above Lockport . The whole system is deliberately sloped to drain "downhill" toward Lockport . The system is also the legal conduit for the Lake Michigan diversion , capped by U.S. Supreme Court decree at 3,200 cubic feet per second (cfs) averaged over 40 years — just over 2 billion gallons per day for all Illinois uses combined (domestic supply, direct diversion, and stormwater runoff from the diverted watershed) .

doc: River Reversal, Canals, Locks & Lakefront Controlling Works
text: Before the big canal. Chicago's first reversal was accomplished with the Illinois & Michigan Canal (opened 1848). Its summit level was fed by steam pumps at Bridgeport that could lift about 100 cfs from the South Branch, and from 1861–1870 the city paid to run the pumps an extra 45 days per year purely to flush sewage away from the lake . In 1871 the I&M's "deep cut" was completed, lowering the summit so lake water flowed by gravity to Lockport — the first true reversal — but the channel was far too small and the river soon relapsed, notably during heavy storms . The catalyzing event was the storm of August 2, 1885 (over 6 inches of rain), which washed the river's filth far out toward the water-supply cribs; the oft-repeated claim that a cholera/typhoid epidemic killed ~12% of the city that year is repeated in some sources but is regarded by historians as a myth — the storm's real effect was political panic . The Sanitary District and the Main Channel, 1892–1900. The Illinois General Assembly created the Sanitary District of Chicago (SDC, today's MWRD) on May 29, 1889; voters approved it that November . The enabling act mandated a channel able to maintain a continuous flow of not less than 80,000 cubic feet per minute at a depth of at least 14 feet and a current no faster than 3 mph — the "dilution solution," sized on Rudolph Hering's standard of 3.3 cfs per 1,000 population . That 80,000 cu ft/min was only the statutory minimum ; the 1907 International Waterways Commission report gives the Main Channel's full designed capacity as 600,000 cu ft/min (10,000 cfs), flowing at 1.25 mph in the earth sections and 1.9 mph in rock — precisely the ceiling the District was later accused of illegally exceeding by the late 1920s (see Notable events). Lyman E. Cooley served as first Chief Engineer (he resigned under board pressure in December 1890 and was succeeded by Benezette Williams; Isham Randolph became Chief Engineer in 1893 and saw the work through) . Ground was broken with ceremony — "Shovel Day" — on September 3, 1892, two miles west of Lemont on the Cook/Will county line . The 28-mile Main Channel from Robey Street (Damen Ave) to Lockport was split into ~one-mile contract sections in three geological classes (earth; earth-and-rock; rock — nearly 15 miles of Niagaran dolomite from Willow Springs to Lockport) . At peak, roughly 8,500 men worked on the channel . Excavation totals: HAER reports slightly over 26 million cu yd of glacial drift plus 12 million cu y

doc: River Reversal, Canals, Locks & Lakefront Controlling Works
text: MWRD operates the system hydraulically; USACE operates the two navigation locks. In normal conditions lake water enters by gravity at the three lakefront structures (the lake typically stands ~2–4 feet above the maintained canal level, about −2 ft Chicago City Datum), flows downhill through the CAWS, and exits at Lockport, spinning the powerhouse turbines . MWRD sets outflow at Lockport with the powerhouse, its sluice gates, and the Controlling Works gates; the USACE Water Control Manual governs coordination with the downstream Lockport Lock . Hydroelectric revenue is real but declining — and briefly collapsed outright: 41.6M kWh ($1.30M) in 2015; 41.1M kWh ($1.09M) in 2019; 21.9M kWh ($836,182) in 2022 ; then generator outages took the powerhouse to zero kWh generated in 2023 (that year's $645,618 in "revenue" came entirely from renewable-energy-credit sales, not power sold), only 343,733 kWh in 2024, with 29.1M kWh ($1.33M) budgeted for 2025 and 32.4M kWh ($1.51M) targeted for 2026 as the units are brought back online . Before forecast storms MWRD pre-draws the canals ("drawdown") by increasing Lockport discharge and closing the lake gates, creating storage; during extreme events, when the canals threaten to overtop, MWRD orders the lakefront sluice gates — and in the worst cases the locks themselves — opened to release flood water to Lake Michigan (a "backflow" or gravity reversal) . The Chicago Harbor Lock's annual traffic figures vary by year and source: an older USACE figure put it at ~40,000 vessels and ~900,000 passengers a year, while USACE reported that the 2025 season alone saw 9,859 lockages serving over 72,000 vessels and nearly 1.3 million passengers . It is commonly cited as the nation's fourth-busiest lock for commercial traffic and second-busiest for recreational traffic — USACE describes it and Seattle's Hiram M. Chittenden Locks as the two busiest locks in the country . Diversion is metered under the 1980 amended decree: Illinois' total take is capped at 3,200 cfs on a 40-year running average, administered by IDNR allocation permits with USACE performing Lake Michigan Diversion Accounting; within that, MWRD holds 305 cfs at the lakefront structures — 270 cfs "discretionary diversion" to keep the canals from going septic (dilution/water-quality flow) plus 35 cfs navigation make-up water, per a 2008 MWRD planning report . text: - Jan 2 & 16–17, 1900 — The stealth opening. Trustees cut the Kedzie needle dam ahead of Missouri's injunction; Lockport gates opened two weeks later . Missouri sued (injunction bill filed January 1900); the Supreme Court allowed the case ( Missouri v. Illinois , 180 U.S. 208 (1901)) but ultimately dismissed it on the merits — Holmes finding Missouri could not prove Chicago's sewage caused St. Louis typhoid — on February 19, 1906 ( Missouri v. Illinois , 200 U.S. 496) . - 1901–1925 diversion battles — The SDC had operated since the reversal under a Secretary of War diversion permit issued May 8, 1899 ; the Secretary then ordered discharge cut to 200,000 cu ft/min (April 9, 1901), then permitted 250,000 cu ft/min (≈4,167 cfs) on December 5, 1901; the District ignored the cap, diverting ~8,500–10,000 cfs by the 1920s (peak ~10,000 cfs in 1928); Canada filed six diplomatic protests 1912–24 . In Sanitary District of Chicago v. United States , 266 U.S. 405 (Jan 5, 1925), a unanimous Court (Holmes) upheld the federal injunction limiting the diversion to the permitted amount ; a new Secretary of War permit (March 3, 1925) allowed 8,500 cfs conditionally while treatment plants were built . - 1929–1930 — Wisconsin v. Illinois : the Court held the diversion unlawful (1929) and Chief Justice Hughes' April 21, 1930 decree stepped it down: 6,500 cfs (+ domestic pumpage) from July 1, 1930; 5,000 cfs from Dec 31, 1935; 1,500 cfs (+ domestic pumpage) after Dec 31, 1938 — the deadline that forced construction of sewage treatment works and the Chicago Harbor Lock . - 1954–1956 — With the 1930 decree's 1,500-cfs floor felt as inadequate for waterway sanitation, Congress twice took up bills to grant a temporary 1,000-cfs diversion increase for a 3-year experimental period under Corps of Engineers supervision; a 1954 version passed but was vetoed by the President, and a modified bill had passed the House and was pending in the Senate as of late 1956 — a preview of the pressure that led to the 1967 decree . - 1967 / 1980 — The June 12, 1967 decree (388 U.S. 426) reset the cap at 3,200 cfs including domestic pumpage; the December 1, 1980 amendment (449 U.S. 48) added the 40-year running-average accounting and flexibility for new suburban allocations. doc: Lake Michigan Water Intake Cribs — Every Crib Ever Built
text: The water intake cribs are offshore structures — essentially armored islands — built to house and protect the mouths of water-supply tunnels bored beneath the bed of Lake Michigan. They are the very first link in Chicago's drinking-water chain: raw lake water enters through intake ports in the crib walls at depths of roughly 20 to 30 feet below the surface, drops down a central well shaft inside the crib, and flows by gravity through brick- or concrete-lined tunnels as much as 200 feet beneath the lakebed to intake basins on shore . From those shore basins the water passes to the city's two purification plants — the James W. Jardine Water Purification Plant just north of Navy Pier (north/central system) and the Eugene Sawyer Water Purification Plant at 79th Street (south system) — and after treatment is lifted by the Department of Water Management's pumping stations into the distribution grid serving Chicago and about 120 suburban communities, roughly 5 million consumers in all, at a rate of almost 1 billion gallons per day . The system exists because of sewage. Nineteenth-century Chicago discharged its wastes into the Chicago River and thence the lake, fouling the shoreline intakes; every heavy rain pushed a plume of filth over the shore inlets, and the city suffered repeated cholera, dysentery and typhoid epidemics . City engineer Ellis S. Chesbrough's solution of 1863 — go two miles offshore, beyond the sewage field, via a tunnel under the lake — created the crib-and-tunnel template that Chicago repeated eight more times over the following seventy years . The cribs are thus the intake-side complement of the same sanitary strategy that later produced the Sanitary & Ship Canal and river reversal: keep the sewage moving away from the spot where the drinking water comes in. Nine permanent cribs were built between 1865 and 1935 (plus at least one temporary "intermediate" construction crib, site of the deadly 1909 fire). Six still stand; exactly two remain in active service — the Edward F. Dunne Crib (paired with the inactive 68th Street Crib, feeding the Sawyer plant) and the William E. Dever Crib (paired with the unusable Carter H. Harrison Crib, feeding the Jardine plant) .

doc: Lake Michigan Water Intake Cribs — Every Crib Ever Built
text: Chicago's very first attempt at a lake-water intake crib predates Chesbrough by more than a decade and ended in failure. Engineer William J. McAlpine surveyed the city's original 1852 waterworks plan (estimated cost $335,500), which specified a timber crib 20 by 40 feet to be sunk 600 feet offshore near Chicago Avenue, feeding a 30-inch wooden inlet main to the pump well; work began in the summer of 1852, but "the boisterous condition of the lake rendered it difficult to secure the crib in place," and the effort was abandoned, with water instead drawn through a pipe close to shore . The template that succeeded was set by Ellis Sylvester Chesbrough (1813–1886), Chicago's chief engineer of the Board of Sewerage Commissioners, who had built Boston's water system before Chicago recruited him in 1855 . With the shore intakes fouled by sewage, Chesbrough's 1863 plan proposed what contemporaries considered folly: a brick-lined tunnel five feet in diameter, driven 10,567 feet (two miles) out under the lake through the blue clay of the lakebed, to a protected intake anchored offshore . The contract went to Dull & Gowan of Harrisburg, Pennsylvania (October 28, 1863) per one lighthouse-history source — though the City's own 1933 Bureau of Engineering retrospective instead names the contractors J. J. Dull and James McGowan of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania , awarded the contract in October 1863 as the second-lowest of seven bidders at a bid price of $315,139 ; the finished project's total cost was $457,845 , of which $380,784 was actually paid to the contractors including extras — work commenced in spring 1864 . The crib — the offshore anchor and armor for the intake shaft — gave the structures their name: a "crib" in Great Lakes practice is a timber box built like ship's hull. The Two-Mile Crib was a pentagonal timber structure 40 feet high with five 58-foot sides, three concentric bolted walls caulked and tarred like a ship, divided into 15 watertight compartments around a 25-foot central well; it consumed 618,325 board-feet of lumber (mostly white pine, with white oak) . It was built on shore, launched on July 24, 1865, towed out by steam tugs, and sunk on station by filling its compartments with stone ballast — a lake storm a few days after the launch disturbed the crib's position before it was finally secured . An iron cylinder was lowered down the well to form the intake shaft, and a lighthouse and keeper's quarters were built on top . Tunneling proceeded simultaneous

doc: Lake Michigan Water Intake Cribs — Every Crib Ever Built
text: Today the crib system runs unmanned. The two active cribs — Dunne (south) and Dever (north) — continuously admit lake water through ports about 20–30 feet down, screened against debris, into their center wells and gravity tunnels; the Dever complex feeds Jardine through a tunnel about 20 feet in diameter, and Dunne feeds the Sawyer plant . During the 1997 crisis, with the Harrison tunnel sealed off for repair and only the Dunne Crib still drawing lake water (feeding the 79th Street/Sawyer plant), Jardine was forced to draw its entire daily supply — about 750 million gallons — directly from the shoreline rather than from the Dever Crib, at higher cost and lower quality; this stopgap, not a normal Dever/Harrison output figure, is the source of the 750 MGD figure sometimes cited for this period . Jardine's rated capacity is 1.4 billion gallons per day — commonly described as the world's largest conventional water filtration plant — and Sawyer's is 720 MGD; combined they actually deliver a bit under 1 BGD to the city and 120 suburbs . Operations staff from the Department of Water Management still travel out "all the time" for inspection and maintenance — in winter historically by icebreaking tug — but no one lives aboard; monitoring is remote, and each crib sits inside a security perimeter of cameras and motion sensors that alert the Chicago Police Department if a boat crosses the line . The four surviving inactive cribs (Four-Mile, 68th Street, Harrison, Wilson Avenue) are mothballed, deteriorating, and — as of the last public record found — still city-owned with no executed demolition or reuse plan . Life aboard in the staffed era is well documented. Crews of roughly two (summer) to four or five men (winter), plus a cook, rotated weekly — one man ashore every fourth or fifth week . Summer work meant clearing fish and debris from the intake screens ("In the summer the trouble is with fish. In the winter, it's ice" ); winter work meant breaking and dynamiting ice off the intake ports . Head keeper Ray Perkins recalled the life as "boring. Fun at times... no TV. Mostly radio, and crossword puzzles" . The Four-Mile Crib's 1898 steam plant made it the comfortable posting at a constant 70 °F . The Encyclopedia of Chicago preserves 1910–1930 Chicago Daily News interior photographs of the Harrison well room and the 68th Street crew kitchen . text: The January 20, 1909 crib fire — Chicago's worst forgotten disaster. During construction of the new southern tunnels to the Dunne Crib, contractor George W. Jackson Inc. housed its tunnel gangs in wooden barracks atop a temporary "Intermediate Crib" on the tunnel route — 7,500 ft from shore per Wikipedia , a figure independently corroborated by a 1912 city history that also gives the same 7,500-ft distance (~2 miles per the 1909 Chicago Examiner ; "about a mile and one-quarter" per a 1929 popular history, which also misdates the fire to January 8 — a likely error given the well-corroborated January 20 date elsewhere ). The crib was serviced by an aerial cableway strung on 26 steel towers; a lake-ice storm on the night of January 30, 1908 — almost exactly a year before the fire — swept away 19 of the towers, which were rebuilt and back in operation by August 1908 . About 108 men were aboard on the morning of January 20, 1909, roughly two-thirds asleep on the second floor, when an explosion — followed about three minutes later by fire — engulfed the barracks. Theories ranged from a match dropped in the powder magazine to gasoline sprinkled against bedbugs; dynamite was kept loosely about the structure, and firefighting gear amounted to "a few axes and some hand fire extinguishers." Men leapt to ice floes; the tug T.T. Morford pulled about 40 survivors off the ice, but many froze, drowned, or burned. Death toll estimates range from 47 (early count) through ~60 (coroner's jury) to 67–70 (press and later accounts) ; two roughly contemporary general histories independently settle on "some seventy lives" and "seventy" . An exact figure is impossible because Jackson's temporary laborers were never fully documented . Most bodies were burned beyond identification; a few days later the largest public burial in South Chicago history took the unidentified dead to a mass grave at Mount Greenwood Cemetery . The fire's origin remains officially unsolved . The 1948 ice siege. A crew fought a 30-hour losing battle against ice choking the intakes, ultimately expending 55 sticks of dynamite . Zebra mussels (late 1980s–90s) colonized and doomed the intake screens, one of the developments that made the resident tenders obsolete . The 1997–98 Harrison tunnel collapse. When the century-old tunnels serving the Harrison Crib were drained for inspection — over warnings from some engineers that dewatering could trigger failure — the tunnel collapsed. doc: Water Purification: Jardine WPP, Sawyer WPP, and Chicago's Drinking-Water Treatment History
text: Chicago's drinking water begins in Lake Michigan, not in the river or sewer system covered elsewhere in this archive — the drinking-water intake system and the wastewater/drainage system (operated by the separate Metropolitan Water Reclamation District) are distinct infrastructures that happen to touch the same lake. Raw lake water enters through offshore intake cribs — armored concrete-and-steel structures two to three miles from shore, in 32–37 feet of water — through screened ports 20–36 feet below the surface . From each crib, water drops through a vertical shaft and travels through a rock tunnel roughly 50–200 feet below the lakebed to shore, arriving at one of the city's two purification plants . Two cribs remain in active service today: the William E. Dever Crib (built 1935), which together with the older Four Mile Crib historically supplied the Central District/Jardine plant, and the Edward F. Dunne Crib (built 1911, per the city's own 1956 engineering account, though some secondary sources give 1909), which supplies the South District/Sawyer plant . At each plant, low-lift pumps raise the raw water 20–25 feet into the treatment train; after coagulation, sedimentation, filtration and disinfection, the finished water flows by gravity through tunnels to a ring of pumping stations, where high-lift pumps force it into the distribution grid of water mains . Downstream , the two plants are the sole source of potable water for the City of Chicago and — via wholesale contracts — more than 120 suburban municipalities and regional water agencies across Cook, DuPage, and Will counties, serving over 5 million people in total . The James W. Jardine Water Purification Plant (north of Navy Pier) supplies the northern, downtown and western parts of the city and northern/western suburbs; the Eugene Sawyer Water Purification Plant (South Shore, near Rainbow Beach) supplies the south side and southern suburbs . One well-documented downstream link is the DuPage Water Commission , which taps Chicago's system at the city's Central Park Avenue Pumping Station, sends water 2.4 miles through a 12-foot-diameter bedrock tunnel to the Lexington Pumping Station, and then 9.3 miles farther west through 90-inch and 72-inch mains to its own DuPage Pumping Station in Elmhurst, from which it redistributes to DuPage County communities . doc: Water Purification: Jardine WPP, Sawyer WPP, and Chicago's Drinking-Water Treatment History
text: Pre-filtration era (1834–1912). Chicago's water system predates any filtration or disinfection by nearly a century. The village's first public well was dug in 1834; a privately owned system went into service in 1842 at a cost of $24,000 , drawing water through a pipe extending 150 feet into the lake off Lake Street, lifted by a 25-horsepower steam pump into a wooden elevated tank, and distributed through wooden mains bored from 10-foot cedar logs . The city obtained legislative authority to build its own works in 1851, laid its first cast-iron distribution pipe on Clark Street in 1852, and put its own municipally owned North Pumping Works into service in February 1854, powered by a walking-beam steam pump nicknamed "Old Sally" — an engine tended, from age 25, by future Chicago mayor DeWitt Clinton Cregier , who went on to serve as the water works' chief engineer (1861–1880), Commissioner of Public Works, and Superintendent of Water Works before his 1889 election as mayor . Rapid population growth (Chicago's population reached 178,500 by the end of the Civil War) and worsening lakefront pollution from the Chicago River drove Ellis Sylvester Chesbrough — born Baltimore County, Maryland, July 6, 1813; formerly chief engineer of the Boston Water Works; appointed chief engineer of Chicago's sewerage commission in 1855 and the city's first Commissioner of Public Works in 1861 — to design an offshore intake . Construction of the first lake tunnel began March 17, 1864 (some secondary accounts say the crib work began May 1864), and water first reached the North Pumping Works through the completed 2-mile, 5-foot-diameter brick-lined tunnel and its timber crib on March 25, 1867, at a reported construction cost of $380,784 . The city's own 1933 retrospective resolves what could otherwise read as a discrepancy in "completion" dates: the tunnel was filled with water and pumped out for final inspection on March 8, 1867 , then formally dedicated — with a cornerstone laid in the new water tower and water first pumped from the new lake tunnel — on March 25, 1867 ; the same source's separate historical-dates table gives early 1869 for completion of the new buildings and water tower at the rebuilt North (Chicago Avenue) pumping station — matching the 1933 retrospective's statement that the station 'was completed in 1869' — a later milestone than either the tunnel test or the dedication . doc: Water Purification: Jardine WPP, Sawyer WPP, and Chicago's Drinking-Water Treatment History
text: Today, the Bureau of Water Supply (one of three bureaus within the Chicago Department of Water Management, alongside the Bureau of Engineering and the Bureau of Administrative Support) operates both plants around the clock, drawing raw water through the Dever and Dunne cribs, treating it, and delivering it by gravity and pumping through roughly 65 miles of tunnel and 4,300–4,400 miles of water main to 12 pumping stations serving Chicago and approximately 118–125 suburban customers — the count varies slightly by source and year: 118 (older trade/journalism sources), 120 (chicago.gov, current), 123 (2022–2026 CIP), and 125 (Chicago Public Library's archival description) — and onward to customers . DWM as a whole employs approximately 2,100 full-time staff on an annual operating budget of about $1 billion, with administrative offices located at the Jardine plant, 1000 E. Ohio Street . The treatment train at both plants follows the conventional sequence: coarse screening (eight traveling screens per plant catch fish and debris), chemical coagulation with aluminum sulfate (alum) and, at times, polymer, flocculation in paddle-agitated basins, sedimentation (removing roughly 90% of remaining particulates), rapid sand filtration through gravel-and-sand beds for a "polishing" effect, and finally disinfection with chlorine, plus post-treatment dosing of fluoride (for dental health) and phosphate (to coat aging pipe interiors and inhibit lead and copper leaching) . Water quality staff reportedly run on the order of 900 tests a day at Jardine alone, and DWM chemists and technicians conduct more than 600,000 water-quality analyses annually system-wide (secondary aggregation; see Open Questions). Both plants reportedly operate on an isolated internal control network without direct internet connectivity, coordinate daily cybersecurity diagnostics with federal partners, and permit public access only by appointment — a posture tightened after the September 11, 2001 attacks (secondary aggregation; see Open Questions). The plants are also themselves permitted point-source dischargers, not just treatment facilities: the Sawyer plant operates under NPDES Permit No. IL0002429 (for filter-backwash/residuals discharge), and in August 2020 the Illinois Pollution Control Board approved an $8,100 settlement against the City for late renewal of that permit and for failing to submit discharge monitoring reports in 2018–2020 — a minor compliance lapse, since resolved . text: - January 20, 1909 — Fire aboard a wooden workers' barracks on the crib under construction for the Dunne intake tunnel system killed an estimated 70 tunnel workers, by fire or drowning while attempting to escape — one of the deadliest incidents in the system's construction history . - October 8–9, 1871 — The Great Chicago Fire damaged the North Pumping Works (Chicago Avenue), taking the pumps out of service for eight days even as the adjacent Water Tower survived largely intact . - 1954 — Bowes v. City of Chicago reached the Illinois Supreme Court after opponents won a lower-court injunction against building the Central District plant in Chicago's harbor; the Supreme Court's reversal on May 24, 1954, was necessary before construction of what became the Jardine plant could proceed . - 1955 — Record system pumpage: a peak day of 1,513,140,000 gallons on July 27, 1955, and a peak hour of 1,783,000,000 gallons (annualized rate) on July 23 at 3 p.m. . - 1955 — Chicago Health Department reported zero deaths from waterborne disease citywide; eight typhoid cases and 49 amoebic dysentery cases were recorded but all were traced to infection outside Chicago (foreign travel or rural areas), reflecting the city's transition from a historically high typhoid rate to one of the lowest among major U.S. cities . - December 1931 — The U.S. Lighthouse Service completed a nine-month, $92,000 project installing 240,000-candlepower rotating navigation beacons and fog-signal equipment on two of Chicago's water cribs, along with fourteen miles of submarine cable for power and remote control from a shore breakwater light station — the city permitted the federal navigation infrastructure on its cribs in exchange for the protective and navigational benefit, at no separate construction cost to the government . - 1997–1998 — The Carter H. Harrison Crib tunnel (by then supplemented/superseded by the Dever Crib) was taken out of service in 1997; when drained for inspection in 1998 portions of the tunnel had collapsed, prompting the city to spend $5.3 million filling sections beneath Lake Shore Drive and triggering litigation against engineering and construction firms involved in the original work . - 2016 — Renaming ceremony for the Eugene Sawyer Water Purification Plant, led by Mayor Rahm Emanuel with the Sawyer family present, marking the first time a Chicago water plant was named for a former mayor . - 2024 — AFSCME Council 31 publicly commended Chicago DWM staff at the Jardine plant

doc: Water Purification: Jardine WPP, Sawyer WPP, and Chicago's Drinking-Water Treatment History
text: - Exact chlorination start date — resolved. Primary/agency sources initially appeared to conflict on whether 1912 (Chicago Dept. of Water & Sewers, 1956, and City Engineer John Ericson's own account of experimental trials) or 1916 (Encyclopedia of Chicago, describing sustained/systematic disinfection) is the correct "start" date. The city's own 1933 Century of Progress retrospective now bridges the two explicitly: hypochlorite trials began March 12, 1912, at single cribs, applied intermittently and only in warm weather; liquid chlorine replaced hypochlorite at the remaining treated cribs in 1915; and liquid-chlorine plants were installed at all pumping stations during 1915–1916, which is almost certainly the "systematic, sustained" milestone the Encyclopedia of Chicago dates to 1916. Both figures are correct for what they describe, and this report now has a single primary source stating the reconciliation directly . - Jardine completion date: 1964 vs. 1968. Multiple credible sources give different "completion" years — full operation in fall 1964 (Wastewater Digest trade article; Encyclopedia of Chicago) versus construction completion/"began functioning" in 1968 (Structurae's structural database, Engineering News-Record, Wikipedia, and Gizmodo — each independently re-verified by direct fetch in July 2026 to state 1968, not 1964; an earlier draft of this report had miscited Wikipedia and Gizmodo as supporting the 1964 date, which they do not). This report presents both and offers a plausible reconciliation (operational by 1964, fully finished/expanded by 1968) but could not locate an unambiguous primary-source dedication record confirming either date definitively. New evidence tilts further toward 1964: a March 1967 Illinois state water-resources report already describes the plant, in the past tense, as complete and "the largest water purification plant in the world," with its full 96-filter/16-basin/8-clear-well complement built out — a full year before the "1968" date given elsewhere — though this still does not explain what specifically happened in 1968 to generate that date in other sources (possibly a final cost close-out, dedication ceremony, or last contract completion not captured in any source located here) . - Jardine total construction cost. doc: Chicago's Water Pumping Stations and Distribution Tunnels
text: This is the delivery half of Chicago's drinking-water chain — the machinery that takes purified Lake Michigan water and pushes it out to taps across the city and the region. (The offshore intake cribs and lake tunnels that bring raw water in are covered in depth in the companion document on intake cribs; this document begins where that one leaves off, at the two purification plants, and follows the water outward.) Raw lake water enters through the offshore cribs and flows by gravity through tunnels roughly 200 feet under the lakebed to shore-side intake basins at Chicago's two purification plants: the James W. Jardine Water Purification Plant (1000 E. Ohio St., serving the north, central and downtown areas) and the Eugene Sawyer Water Purification Plant (3300 E. Cheltenham Pl., serving the south side), formerly named the Central District and South District Filtration Plants respectively . After filtration and chlorination, gravity carries the treated water onward — Chicago has essentially no elevated storage tanks — to a citywide network of 12 pumping stations , each responsible for a geographic "pumping district," where high-lift pumps raise the water's pressure and push it into the street-level grid of cast-iron and ductile-iron mains . From the grid mains, service pipes carry water to roughly 2.7 million Chicago residents and, on a wholesale basis, to more than 5 million people combined in surrounding suburban communities — about 43% of Illinois's population. Sources disagree on the precise current count of those suburban customers: the FY2022–2026 Capital Improvement Program, the most recent official figure available, states 123; the 2011 DWM transition report states 125; a 2021 University of Chicago Chicago Studies paper states 126; and chicago.gov's own current suburban-customers page gives no specific count at all . Return flow — sanitary sewage and stormwater — is an entirely separate system operated by the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago (MWRD), not the Department of Water Management (DWM); DWM's sewer mains simply convey wastewater to MWRD's treatment plants, a boundary that is a frequent point of public confusion . The tunnels described in this document are therefore of two kinds: the long offshore lake tunnels (crib to shore — treated at length in the companion crib document) and the shorter inland land or "crosstown" tunnels that link the shore intake basins to each pumping station and, in several cases, link pumping

doc: Chicago's Water Pumping Stations and Distribution Tunnels
text: Chicago's pumping-and-tunnel system is the direct engineering legacy of Ellis Sylvester Chesbrough (1813–1886), chief engineer of the Board of Public Works, who in 1863 proposed running a tunnel two miles out under Lake Michigan to intercept clean water beyond the sewage plume fouling the shoreline . The tunnel and the accompanying Chicago Avenue Pumping Station were built simultaneously and "piecemeal," so as not to interrupt the existing water supply from the 1854 works on the same site . The pumping station's original engine, nicknamed "Old Sally," was an 8-million-gallon-per-day (MGD) vertical condensing steam engine with a single-acting pump, installed under the direction of a young engineer named DeWitt Clinton Cregier in 1853; it served the city faithfully for more than 50 years . A third pump of 18 MGD was added on Cregier's recommendation as the present Chicago Avenue building rose in 1867–69 . The architect of both the Chicago Avenue Pumping Station and the adjoining Chicago Water Tower was William W. Boyington (1818–1898), one of the most prolific Midwestern architects of the era, who designed the pair in "castellated Gothic" style using rough-faced, cream-yellow Joliet limestone . The pumping station's engine room measured 142 by 60 feet with a 36-foot ceiling and an operating gallery; a 131-foot smokestack rose from twin boiler rooms at the rear . Because 1860s pumping engines delivered water in surges rather than a steady stream, Chesbrough specified a standpipe — an 138-foot, 3-foot-diameter vertical iron pipe — to absorb the pressure spikes; rather than leave the standpipe exposed, the city built the ornamental tower around it, with 168 foundation piles and a 154-foot limestone shaft (237 interior steps, no elevator) . Both buildings were dedicated March 25, 1867 (tower cornerstone) and opened for service in 1869; they are the only public structures in the burned district to survive the Great Chicago Fire of October 8–9, 1871 substantially intact — although the fire's wood-shingle roof over the pumping station burned, gutting the machine shop and putting the pumps out of service for eight days — from the afternoon of October 9 until Cregier's crews restored them roughly a week later — leaving the city without water in the aftermath . doc: Chicago's Water Pumping Stations and Distribution Tunnels
section: Which plant feeds which station: the three pre-filtration tunnel districts
text: Before either purification plant existed, the city's own engineering staff organized the tunnel network — and the 12 (then 11) pumping stations it fed — into three self-contained "Districts," each with its own crib(s) and dedicated land-tunnel system: a North District (Lake View and Wilson Avenue tunnels, serving the Lake View, Mayfair and Thomas Jefferson stations), a Central District (Four Mile Lake, Chicago Avenue, Blue Island Avenue, Polk Street and Desplaines Street tunnels, serving Chicago Avenue, 22nd Street/Cermak, Central Park Avenue and Springfield Avenue), and a South District (Southwest Lake and Land, Western Avenue and Stewart Avenue tunnels, serving 68th Street, Roseland and Western Avenue) — a grouping laid out explicitly, station by station and tunnel by tunnel, in the city's own 1956 technical report . That report predates Jardine (whose predecessor, the Central District Filtration Plant, was then still under construction) and Sawyer had only just reached full capacity, so the North and Central Districts were still running on chlorination-only lake water piped in from their old cribs, while the South District's tunnels had already been physically spliced into the new South District (Sawyer) plant via a dedicated 2.80-mile, 1945 "Connection to Filtration Plant" tunnel — the direct ancestor of every finished-water feeder described below . The same 1956 report describes the mechanism at the Sawyer end concretely: the South District plant's low-lift pumping station used eight large motor-driven pumps with a combined 750 MGD capacity to lift raw water roughly 20–25 feet from the intake basin into the treatment train, after which filtered water flowed by gravity to two main filtered-water reservoirs connected "by a tunnel through solid rock" directly to the three South District pumping stations (68th Street, Roseland, Western Avenue) — i.e., the 2.80-mile connector is that tunnel . Once Jardine came online in the 1960s, its combined "north, central and downtown" service territory absorbed both the old North and Central District tunnel networks, meaning 8 of the 12 modern stations — Chicago Avenue, Central Park, Springfield, Mayfair, Thomas Jefferson, Cermak, Lakeview and (indirectly, via Central Park) Lexington — draw finished water that originates at Jardine , while the other 4 — 68th Street, Roseland, Western Avenue and Southwest — draw from Sawyer , continuing the old South District network Sawyer was built to feed . doc: Chicago's Water Pumping Stations and Distribution Tunnels
text: Chicago's Department of Water Management delivers on the order of 750 MGD to just under 1 billion gallons per day on average — against a combined rated treatment capacity at Jardine and Sawyer of roughly 2.1 billion gallons per day — to Chicago's approximately 2.7 million residents and, per the most recent official count (the FY2022–2026 CIP), 123 suburban wholesale customers . That count is not stable across sources: the 2011 DWM transition report gives 125 and a 2021 academic paper gives 126, so the precise current figure should be treated as approximate. Together these customers represent more than 5 million people and roughly 43% of Illinois's population . Water leaves the two plants by gravity, is lifted and pressurized at the 12 pumping stations, and is pushed into roughly 4,300–4,400 miles of water mains via 47,000-plus hydrants and 48,000 valves . Notably, this average delivery figure is lower than the system pumped seven decades ago: a 1956 city report puts 1955's average daily pumpage at 1,037,074,000 gallons — a legacy of substantially higher mid-century per-capita and industrial water use — even though the system now also serves a larger combined city-plus-suburban population . DWM's own laboratories monitor water quality around the clock, and the department reports that its water consistently meets or exceeds US EPA, Illinois EPA and Illinois Department of Public Health standards ; its 2024 Consumer Confidence Report shows a 90th-percentile tap-water lead result of 7.1 ppb, well under the EPA action level of 15 ppb . Organizationally, DWM in 2011 comprised roughly 2,135 budgeted full-time positions under a Commissioner, a 1st Deputy/Chief Engineer, and three Managing Deputy Commissioners overseeing Engineering Services/Water Supply, Administrative Support/Meter Services, and Operations & Distribution ; the department's Water Fund and Sewer Fund together carried more than $250 million in annual revenue at that time, funded almost entirely by water and sewer rates rather than general tax dollars, with roughly 48% of water revenue historically generated by suburban wholesale sales . DWM is legally and organizationally distinct from the MWRD: DWM supplies drinking water and conveys sewage/stormwater only as far as MWRD's interceptors, while MWRD owns and treats the wastewater side and the Deep Tunnel/TARP reservoir system . doc: Chicago's Water Pumping Stations and Distribution Tunnels
text: - October 8–9, 1871 — Great Chicago Fire. The wood-shingle roof of the original Chicago Avenue pumping station caught fire; the pumps were knocked out of commission on the afternoon of the second day of the fire and stayed out of service for eight days (the city's own 1933 statistics table records the city as "without water for eight days"), even as the adjacent Water Tower and standpipe survived essentially undamaged — one of very few structures left standing in the burned district . An estimated 15,000 lead service connections citywide melted in the blaze . - 1908 — Waste investigation. A city investigation found roughly 75% of pumped water was being lost to leakage or unmetered/unnecessary use, spurring the early-20th-century push toward universal metering . - 1909 — Dunne/Intermediate Crib fire (see companion crib document for full account): killed an estimated 47–70 construction workers building the tunnel system that would feed the Roseland pumping station via the Southwest Land Tunnel . - 1932 — Reconstruction Finance Corporation loan. A $2,327,000 federal RFC loan — one of the era's signature federal infrastructure interventions — financed the new Central Pumping Station (later Cermak), consolidating three obsolete 19th-century stations into one modern, all-electric plant during the depths of the Depression . - 1964–65 / 1978–79 — Lake View station phase-out and demolition. Chicago's oldest annexed pumping-station complex (in service on that site since 1875) was placed on standby in 1964, formally retired in 1965, and physically demolished in 1978–79 — a rare fully documented "cradle to grave" record for a Chicago pumping station thanks to its HAER survey . - 1974 — Lake View chimney partial demolition. The station's 215-foot chimney, built on four 5-foot-diameter caisson piers, was judged unsafe and had its top section removed in 1974, five years before the rest of the plant came down . - 2012–2017 — Springfield Avenue steam-to-electric conversion , announced 2012 and unveiled 2017: Chicago's first completed pumping-station power-source conversion under the modern modernization program, publicly framed around a ~70% carbon-emissions cut . - July 9–11, 2024 — Roseland Pumping Station mechanical failure and boil order. doc: The City of Chicago Combined Sewer Network
text: The City of Chicago's combined sewer network is the local collection layer of a three-tier regional wastewater and stormwater system. It is owned and operated by the Chicago Department of Water Management (DWM), a city agency, and is legally and physically distinct from the much larger interceptor and tunnel system owned by the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago (MWRD), a separate unit of government . What flows in. Roof downspouts, yard and area drains, sump pumps, and street catch basins feed stormwater into the system; toilets, sinks, and industrial process drains feed sanitary sewage into the same pipes through private "house drains" (building sewers) that connect to the public main in the street or parkway . Because more than 95 percent of the city's sewer system is combined — that is, it carries sanitary sewage and stormwater runoff in the same pipe — nearly all of this flow mixes immediately downstream of the property line . A small minority of the city's drainage area (mainly Lake Shore Drive and non-industrial portions of O'Hare International Airport) is served by separate storm sewers regulated as a Municipal Separate Storm Sewer System (MS4) under NPDES Permit No. IL0002283 for O'Hare, rather than the combined-sewer NPDES permit that governs the rest of the city . What flows out, and to whom. City-owned local sewers do not go to a treatment plant directly. They discharge into MWRD-owned intercepting sewers — described by MWRD as the "superhighway of sewers" — which are typically much larger, run along or under major streets and waterways, and are fed by roughly 10,000 connections from local municipal systems across the region . From there, dry-weather flow is pumped to one of MWRD's water reclamation plants (the Chicago combined-sewer area is served principally by the Stickney, Calumet, and O'Brien/North Side plants) . In wet weather, when the combined flow exceeds what the interceptors and treatment plants can carry, MWRD's Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP, "Deep Tunnel") intercepts the excess through vertical drop shafts and routes it into deep rock tunnels and reservoirs for temporary storage until treatment capacity is available . Only when TARP itself is full does the system discharge untreated combined sewage directly to the Chicago River, the Calumet River, or the Des Plaines River through a combined sewer overflow (CSO) . doc: The City of Chicago Combined Sewer Network
text: Chicago's sewer system began as a direct response to repeated public-health catastrophes. Six consecutive years of typhoid and dysentery outbreaks, capped by a cholera epidemic in 1854 that (by contemporary Chicago Tribune reporting) killed roughly six percent of the city's population, pushed the Illinois legislature to create a municipal Board of Sewerage Commissioners on February 14, 1855 . The Board hired Ellis Sylvester Chesbrough — previously chief engineer of the Boston Water Works — as its engineer in December 1855; he delivered his "Report and Plan of Sewerage for the City of Chicago" to the Board on December 31, 1855, and it was formally adopted . This plan is generally credited as the first comprehensive, engineered sewer system built for an American city . Chesbrough's plan faced a fundamental problem: Chicago's terrain was so flat, and so close to the level of Lake Michigan, that sewers could not be laid at a depth sufficient for gravity flow without pipes literally sitting below the level of the lake into which they had to drain . His solution — debated and revised through 1856 and formally approved by the Common Council as the "High Grade" plan on May 4, 1857 — was to build the sewers largely above the existing ground surface and then raise the grade of the streets around them by roughly six to fourteen feet (accounts vary: contemporary Tribune coverage and modern secondary sources cite figures from "6 to 10 feet" up to "as much as 14 feet" in places), filling in around the new pipes and re-paving at the new elevation . Existing masonry buildings were jacked up in place on hundreds to thousands of jackscrews — the Tremont House, a six-story hotel, was raised six feet on roughly 1,000 jackscrews operated by about 500 men while remaining open for business; the 27,000-ton, iron-framed Robbins Building was raised 27.5 inches on 1,584 jackscrews over three days in November 1865 — while smaller wood-frame buildings were more often moved bodily on rollers to new lots . This "Raising of Chicago" continued in a piecemeal fashion into the 1860s and beyond, and construction on the sewer system itself began in earnest in 1856 (roughly six miles built by the end of that year), with the City's own modern engineering materials describing sewer-system construction as "beginning in 1857" . doc: The City of Chicago Combined Sewer Network
text: DWM's Bureau of Operations and Distribution is responsible for day-to-day maintenance of the sewer system, alongside water mains, hydrants, and valves, and fields roughly 145,000 customer calls a year on issues including water/sewage in basements, water on the street, and broken mains . Routine sewer maintenance includes cleaning approximately 25,000 catch basins annually and responding to 311-generated service requests for sewer cleaning, inspection, and cave-ins . Capital rehabilitation runs on a rolling multi-year cycle combining cured-in-place-pipe (CIPP) and geopolymer trenchless lining (chosen because it is cheaper and less disruptive than open-cut replacement), targeted replacement of hydraulically undersized or structurally deficient mains, and structure (manhole/catch basin) lining and rehabilitation; DWM's FY2022–2026 program alone represents roughly $1.2 billion, and multi-year trenchless-lining contracts awarded to outside contractors (e.g., a reported $148 million package covering roughly 575,000 linear feet of 10-to-60-inch sewer lining, awarded to Granite Inliner) illustrate the scale of this ongoing work . Development review and permitting for any new sewer connection or drain is governed by the City's Regulations for Sewer Construction and Stormwater Management (most recently reissued August 2024) and by Municipal Code Chapters 11-16 (Public Sewers and Drains) and 11-18 (Stormwater Management); regulated developments must submit stormwater management plans, meet volume- and rate-control requirements (commonly implemented with 3-inch and 4-inch "vortex restrictor" outlet devices calibrated to a maximum release rate, typically 0.15 cfs per half-acre), and, where feasible, disconnect roof downspouts from the sewer system entirely . Sewer connections and repairs on public property require a permit from the Department of Buildings; minor drain-layer work and short in-kind repairs are exempted subject to registration . The City manages its own combined sewer overflow program under NPDES Permit IL0045012, including a Nine Minimum Controls Enhancement Plan, a Pollution Prevention Plan, and public CSO notification coordinated through MWRD's online and text/email alert systems; members of the public can report a dry-weather CSO discharge by calling 311 . text: - 1937 Fullerton–Milwaukee Avenue manhole explosions (May 29, 1937). Naphtha or gasoline vapor that had accumulated in the sewer ignited, blowing 17 manhole covers out of the Fullerton Avenue sewer along roughly a mile of pipe between Kedzie and Western avenues; one of the 155-pound manhole covers was hurled onto and through the roof of a five-story building, falling down an elevator shaft and killing the elevator operator. Residents had reported a gas odor in the area in the days before the blast . - September 12–16, 2008 flood. Remnants of Hurricane Ike combined with a slow-moving low-pressure system to drop a then-all-time-record 6.64 inches of rain in a single calendar day (Sept. 13) at O'Hare, with storm totals ranging from about 2.4 to 10.5 inches across northeastern Illinois; MWRD diverted roughly 11.2 billion gallons into Lake Michigan and about 50 billion gallons into the Des Plaines River to relieve the system, roughly 10,000 homes were evacuated, and insured property losses were estimated at $155 million . - April 18, 2013 sinkhole. A severe rainstorm ruptured a water main in Chicago's South Deering neighborhood on the Southeast Side; the resulting sinkhole on Houston Street grew from about 20 to about 40 feet across and swallowed three cars, injuring one driver [not directly a sewer failure, but illustrative of combined subsurface-utility risk in older infrastructure corridors]. - 2019 Greater Grand Crossing sinkhole. A cave-in on the side of an aging brick sewer main near 73rd Street and Woodlawn Avenue opened a street sinkhole, illustrating the continued in-service presence — and periodic failure risk — of 19th/early-20th-century brick trunk sewers within the modern system. - July 2, 2023 flash flood. One of the heaviest rainfall events in Chicago's recorded history (per the National Weather Service), with localized totals up to 9.0 inches over 18 hours and a new daily rainfall record at O'Hare (3.35 inches, surpassing the prior record of 2.06 inches set July 2, 1982); rainfall exceeded the combined capacity of the local sewers and MWRD's Deep Tunnel/McCook Reservoir system, at least 70,000 basements flooded, the Chicago River rose above flood stage near the Loop, and 311 logged flooding complaints from roughly 7,000 addresses in the first week of July alone, disproportionately concentrated in majority-Black West Side neighborhoods such as Austin; the event was declared an Illinois disaster area July 11, 2023 and a federal disaster area Augus

doc: MWRD Intercepting Sewers & Sewage Pumping Stations
text: The Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago (MWRD, originally the Sanitary District of Chicago, chartered May 29, 1889) does not own the pipes under most Chicago-area streets. Those "local sewers" — combined sewers carrying both sanitary sewage and stormwater in a single pipe — are owned and maintained by the City of Chicago and 128 suburban municipalities, and they drain by gravity into a second, larger tier of pipe that the District does own: roughly 560 miles of intercepting sewers and force mains , ranging from 6 inches to 27 feet in diameter, fed by about 10,000 local-sewer connection points across an 882-square-mile service area . Functionally, the intercepting sewers are "the superhighway of sewers" — they gather what the municipal system collects and convey it, largely by gravity along waterway corridors and beneath major streets, to one of the MWRD's seven water reclamation plants (WRPs) for treatment . In dry weather this system works by gravity alone. In wet weather, when combined flow exceeds what the intercepting sewers and WRPs can carry, two backstops exist. First, since the 1970s–2000s, excess flow is diverted into the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) — deep rock tunnels 150–300 feet underground that store combined sewage and stormwater until treatment capacity is available, then release it to three surface reservoirs and ultimately to the WRPs via TARP's own pumping stations . Second, at several points where the interceptor system itself needs to lift flow to a higher elevation — or where a CSO (combined sewer overflow) has to be discharged to a waterway because both the interceptors and TARP are full — the District operates dedicated sewage pumping stations . These are a distinct engineering layer from TARP's tunnel-dewatering pumps, built decades earlier to solve a simpler problem: intercepting sewers running along the lakefront and river branches are often below the elevation of the receiving river, so gravity cannot finish the job. Downstream, treated effluent from the WRPs (Stickney, Calumet, O'Brien/North Side, and four smaller plants) discharges to the Chicago Area Waterway System — the North Shore Channel, North Branch and main stem of the Chicago River, the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal, the Cal-Sag Channel, and the Calumet River — which flows away from Lake Michigan toward the Des Plaines and Illinois Rivers, a legacy of the 1900 reversal of the Chicago River . doc: MWRD Intercepting Sewers & Sewage Pumping Stations
text: Intercepting sewers (1906 onward). Before the Sanitary District built intercepting sewers, local sewers drained straight into the Chicago River and Lake Michigan. Reversing the Chicago River's flow via the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal (construction completed 1898, opened January 1900; chief engineer Isham Randolph) solved the problem for sewage that reached the South Branch, but it did nothing for sewage that drained directly into the lake along the northern lakefront . The District's response, beginning in 1906, was to build a dedicated network of intercepting sewers — masonry, brick, cast-in-place concrete (pre-1950) and later precast concrete pipe, up to 27 feet in diameter — running along the lakefront and river corridors to catch that flow and redirect it inland . The first completed segment, built 1900–1907 along the lakefront, fed a predecessor pumping station at Lawrence Avenue (just east of Broadway) that lifted the intercepted sewage into the North Branch of the Chicago River instead of the lake . Per the Sanitary District's own 1928 engineering report, that original Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station — with its crib ¼ mile offshore in Lake Michigan at the foot of Lawrence Avenue supplying dilution water — was actually built and initially owned by the City of Chicago , completed in 1908, and only transferred to Sanitary District operation on December 6, 1910; it drew a 6-sq-mi area and pumped 40–300 cfs via one screw flushing pump and three small centrifugal sewage pumps . (A separate 1927 Sanitary District-commissioned engineering report locates this same predecessor station instead at "Lawrence and Racine Avenue" rather than Lawrence and Broadway — a minor discrepancy between two Sanitary District-linked primary sources that this brief has not been able to resolve further .) In parallel, the District cut the 7.7-mile North Shore Channel from Lake Michigan at Wilmette south to the North Branch (construction began September 1907, channel completed November 1910) specifically to bring diluting lake water down to flush the newly intercepted sewage away from the city, with the Wilmette Pumping Station (built 1908–1910, lock added 1912) serving as the controlling structure regulating that lake diversion . doc: MWRD Intercepting Sewers & Sewage Pumping Stations
text: The MWRD today operates 23 sewage pumping stations alongside seven water reclamation plants, moving an average of roughly 1.2–1.5 billion gallons of wastewater per day through a combined treatment capacity exceeding 2.0–2.4 billion gallons per day . The 560-mile intercepting sewer network is inspected and maintained jointly by the District's Maintenance & Operations and Engineering Departments; under the Collection Asset Management Plan (CAMP) — which replaced the 1991 Interceptor Inspection and Rehabilitation Program (IIRP) in 2013 — segments are prioritized for CCTV inspection, lining, or replacement using NASSCO-standard, risk-based scoring, and the District tracks asset age by decade (roughly 37% of interceptor mileage was installed 1961–1980, versus 6.9% before 1920) . Each of the five major CSO pumping stations — North Branch, Racine Avenue, 95th Street, 122nd Street, and 125th Street — is subject to NPDES permit sampling requirements: whenever a station's first storm pump activates, MWRD Industrial Waste Division staff are dispatched to pull composite and grab samples (BOD5/TOC, suspended solids, cadmium, hardness, pH, fecal coliform, depending on the receiving water) from the discharge for regulatory reporting . All are unmanned, remotely alarmed facilities inspected monthly, with level sensors triggering pump operation from wet-well elevation, debris screens (some with automatic raking) protecting the pump intakes, and backup generators and dehumidification equipment on site . A sixth, much smaller CSO pumping station, Westchester (WCPS), is separately monitored under MWRD's CSO Representative Monitoring and Reporting Plan for its single Addison Creek outfall (No. 150), reporting quarterly to IEPA via the same telemetry-based program that covers the five larger stations . On the TARP side, the system now consists of four connected tunnel systems (Mainstream, Calumet, Des Plaines, and Upper Des Plaines) feeding three reservoirs (Majewski, Thornton, and the still-expanding McCook) with 15.15 billion gallons of design storage, of which all but McCook Stage 2 (6.5 of the 10 billion gallons planned for McCook) is complete . The Mainstream and Calumet TARP pumping stations are the only points where stored combined sewage is lifted back out of the tunnel/reservoir system to the WRPs (Stickney and Calumet, respectively) for treatment; MWRD's own 50th-anniversary retrospective states the full system captured more than 153 billion gallons of combined sewer

text: October 3, 1986 — Deep Tunnel geyser event. A heavy thunderstorm dumped several inches of rain in a short period over the newly built (and not yet fully interconnected) tunnel system. Water surged unevenly through the tunnels, producing geysers reported at over 65 feet near Wilmette and the Bahá'í Temple, and a roughly 30-foot geyser erupted downtown at Jefferson and Monroe streets, filling a woman's car with water and trapping her inside. The MWRD subsequently installed a system of watertight bulkheads throughout TARP to prevent similar pressure events . 2015–2016 — green infrastructure and manufacturing recognition. The Racine Avenue Pumping Station's green-roof retrofit (2015) and the Calumet TARP Pump Station's award-winning custom steel pump fitting (2016) both reflect a period of MWRD investment in retrofitting Depression- and Cold War-era pumping infrastructure with modern equipment and sustainability features . 2018–2021 — structural rehabilitation of the two oldest major pumping stations. Both the ~1930 North Branch Pumping Station and the ~1985 Mainstream TARP pumps required major rehabilitation contracts within a few years of each other — the former because decades of 300-cfs discharge had eroded structural concrete columns by up to 50%, the latter because the original 1985 pump/motor sets were nearing the end of their design life . July 2–4, 2026 — record rainfall and near-capacity reservoirs. A multi-day rain event over the Independence Day weekend brought locally heavy rainfall — up to roughly 8.5 inches in Aurora and nearly 8 inches in Naperville over 48 hours, well above the Chicago area's 3.7-inch average for the entire month of July — pushing the McCook Reservoir to full capacity (100%, more than 3.6 billion gallons; different outlets reporting at different points over the weekend put it as low as 96–98% full) — its sixth time reaching capacity in 2026 alone, versus five times in the preceding five years combined — and the Thornton Reservoir to about 94% full (more than 7.4 billion gallons), the fullest it had been since opening in 2015. MWRD officials acknowledged that if reservoirs filled completely, untreated water might have to be released to Lake Michigan or allowed to overflow into local waterways, risking beach closures; as of the storm's immediate aftermath, officials reported the system had contained the rainfall without requiring such releases. doc: North Branch (Lawrence Avenue) Pumping Station
text: The North Branch Pumping Station (NBPS), also widely known by its informal name the Lawrence Avenue Pumping Station , sits on the east bank of the North Branch of the Chicago River at 4860 N. Francisco Avenue (building footprint 4840–4860 N.), immediately north of the Lawrence Avenue bridge and roughly across the river from Ronan Park's natural area, in the Lincoln Square/Ravenswood area of Chicago's North Side . It is owned and operated by the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago (MWRD), successor to the Sanitary District of Chicago, which built it . In the District's own river-mile referencing system it sits at USGS river mile 333.1 upstream of Lockport — about 0.2 mile downstream of the site of the former North Branch Dam (river mile 333.3, south of Foster Avenue at River Park), where the North Shore Channel met the natural North Branch until the dam was removed in 2018 . (Correction, July 2026: an earlier version of this document inverted this relationship, placing the station "just upstream" of the dam; the cited MWRD table lists Lawrence Avenue at mile 332.9, the station at 333.1, and the dam at 333.3 — miles increase moving upstream.) (Second correction, July 2026: this pass re-verified the station's position relative to the Lawrence Avenue bridge, which an earlier version of this document also had backwards. The building's Chicago street-grid address (4840–4860 N.) is entirely north of the 4800 N baseline that Lawrence Avenue itself occupies, and direct measurement of the mapped building footprint against the mapped Lawrence Avenue bridge crossing confirms the station's near edge sits roughly 230 feet, and its far edge roughly 480 feet, north of the bridge — well under a city block, but north, not south, as previously stated. The station's east-bank position and its rough alignment across the river from Ronan Park's natural area — which runs along the west bank from Lawrence Avenue north to Argyle Street — are both independently confirmed and unchanged from the earlier text .) NBPS performs two distinct hydraulic functions that correspond to its two eras of construction: Dry-weather / sanitary lift function (original, 1928–1930). Before the riverside station existed, the original inland Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station (1907, see below) lifted local North Side sewage about 10 feet into the head of the westward 16-foot Lawrence Avenue conduit, which then ran the rest of the way by gravity to a straight, untreated outfall

section: The North Branch Dam next door
text: Two-tenths of a mile upstream of NBPS, at the confluence where the North Shore Channel meets the natural North Branch of the Chicago River (river mile 333.3, immediately south of Foster Avenue at River Park), the Sanitary District of Chicago — MWRD's predecessor — built a low dam in 1910 as part of the North Shore Channel project, the same construction campaign that tied the lakefront intercepting-sewer system into the North Branch corridor NBPS would later serve . The North Shore Channel's own bed, and the lower North Branch it fed into, were engineered to a lower, canal-controlled water elevation than the natural, un-channelized North Branch immediately upstream; multiple accounts put the resulting difference at roughly four feet, and the dam was built to hold the upper river at its own separate elevation and keep that four-foot head differential from eroding the riverbed and banks working upstream from the confluence . A U.S. Army Corps of Engineers spokesman quoted around the time of removal gave a complementary account of the same function, describing the dam as preventing water from draining out of the upper river too rapidly and guarding against reversed flow into the system's northern reaches . Physically the dam was modest — a low-head concrete structure with a single four-foot vertical drop — but for the century it stood it was hydraulically and ecologically significant out of proportion to its size: it was the only waterfall on the entire Chicago River system, a complete barrier to upstream fish passage, and an obstacle canoeists and kayakers had to portage around . A fisheries survey using electrofishing above the dam before removal found a single green sunfish in the North Branch upstream of it, against 35 native species already documented in the North Shore Channel just downstream . Friends of the Chicago River began campaigning for the dam's removal in 2000, opening an 18-year effort that combined feasibility studies, fisheries surveys, and sustained advocacy with the Army Corps, MWRD, and the Chicago Park District . The Corps' Chicago District ultimately executed the physical removal under its Section 506 Great Lakes Fishery and Ecosystem Restoration (GLFER) Program authority — a 65 percent federal / 35 percent local cost-share program — with MWRD and the Chicago Park District as the local cost-share partners . Reported figures for the dam-removal phase itself range from roughly $2.1 million (the Phase 1 estimate approved in 2017) to $4.5–4
