doc: North Branch (Lawrence Avenue) Pumping Station
text: The Sanitary District of Chicago built the North Branch Pumping Station between 1928 and 1930 to solve a specific problem left over from the district's turn-of-the-century sanitation work. Between 1900 and 1907 the district had built large intercepting sewers along the lakefront to stop wastewater from draining directly into Lake Michigan; that captured flow needed a place to go, and — until a treatment plant existed — the intercepting sewer discharged into the North Branch of the Chicago River at this point on Lawrence Avenue . Construction of the permanent riverside pumping station is documented in a long run of MWRD's own dated construction photographs: excavation is visible by April 9, 1928 (photographed from the roof of Lawrence Hall, 4833 N. Francisco Avenue, across the street); further excavation for the connection to the "Lawrence Avenue conduit" is dated April 20, 1928; superstructure progress is documented on May 25, August 8, September 4, September 15, and October 26, 1928; the building appears "nearly complete" in a photograph dated October 26, 1929; and a photograph captioned "newly constructed" is dated April 26, 1930 . MWRD's own captions state plainly that "the station was completed in 1929 and went into service in 1930" . On October 26, 1929, MWRD also photographed miners working in a tunnel under the North Shore Channel connecting to the North Side Intercepting Sewer near the pumping station — evidence of the tunneled connection that ties the lakefront intercepting-sewer system into the new station . A commenter on the historical-photo blog that documents this construction (an anonymous, unverified source, not an MWRD record) states that an earlier "Lawrence Avenue Pumping Station" had existed since 1907 on the north side of Lawrence Avenue between Broadway and the Red Line embankment, and that operations were moved to the new riverside building in 1930 . That predecessor is now corroborated by primary sources on both its existence and its precise date, location, and capacity — see "The original Lawrence Avenue Pumping Station, 1907–1930" below. The Sanitary District's own 1928 publications, obtained for this archive as page-image harvests, separately caption a construction photograph of the new riverside station as replacing "the Lawrence Avenue station" at four times its capacity , and the companion North Side Sewage Treatment Project volume captions an excavation-phase photograph — showing the six intake wells under construction — as "

doc: North Branch (Lawrence Avenue) Pumping Station
section: The original Lawrence Avenue Pumping Station, 1907–1930
text: NBPS's own construction record starts in 1928, but the pumping function it took over did not — and the true predecessor was not on the river at all. Two primary sources newly reviewed for this archive, previously unavailable, turn what had been only an anonymous blog comment into a well-documented station in its own right. C. D. Hill, in a paper on "The Sewerage System of Chicago" read to the Western Society of Engineers and published in its Journal in September 1911, describes the city's north-side lakefront intercepting sewer converging at "a pumping station at Lawrence Avenue east of Evanston Avenue" — Evanston Avenue was renamed Broadway in 1913, so "east of Evanston Avenue" is equivalent to "east of Broadway." The Sanitary District's own July 1921 Engineering Data on the Sanitary District of Chicago tabulates the same plant outright as the "Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station," giving its location as "Lawrence Ave. and C. M. & St. P. R. R. (N. W. El.)" — the crossing of Lawrence Avenue with the railroad/elevated embankment immediately east of Broadway . Both descriptions match the 2024 blog commenter's account of a site "on the north side of Lawrence between Broadway and the Red Line" almost exactly, from sources the commenter could not have seen . (Discrepant outlier, July 2026.) One primary source bucks this consensus: a 1927 sewage-disposal survey of the Sanitary District by the consulting firm Alvord, Burdick & Howson — reproduced independently the same year as Illinois State Water Survey Bulletin No. 23 — instead locates the predecessor station "at Lawrence and Racine Avenue" . Racine Avenue is roughly three miles west of the Lawrence/Broadway site every other source (including Engineering Works , the same firm's contemporaries at the Sanitary District) agrees on, and the same 1927 report elsewhere correctly describes the station's westward discharge into the North Branch, so this is treated here as a likely transcription error in the 1927 original rather than a competing location. The date is also now independently corroborated, and resolves in favor of 1907 rather than 1909. The Sanitary District's 1919 History of its Growth and Development states that the north-side and south-side (39th Street) intercepting-sewer systems, "with their respective pumping stations, ... were completed about the same time in the year 1907 and placed in operation" — an institutional account from the District's own law department, written a little over a decade af

section: The 1928 hydraulic reconfiguration: from mid-route lift to river-end lift
text: This report set out to test a specific hypothesis: that the 1928–1930 project moved the system's single lift from mid-route (the inland station) to the river end (NBPS), so that the conduit now flows to NBPS's wet well by gravity and NBPS lifts once into a gravity interceptor running toward the treatment works — versus the alternative that flow direction reversed entirely. Two Sanitary District publications from 1928, newly OCR'd for this research pass — the promotional North Side Sewage Treatment Project: An Outline of Its Construction, Operation and Effect and the more technical Engineering Works — describe, in the District's own contemporary language, exactly what changed, and the evidence supports the hypothesis. Before 1930: gravity conduit, mid-route lift. As established above, the inland station lifted local sewage about 10 feet into the head of the westward 16-foot Lawrence Avenue conduit, which then ran by gravity the rest of the way to a straight outfall on the North Branch at the river . After 1930: gravity to the river end, one lift there. The Outline states plainly that the new station "will receive the sewage and storm-water flow by gravity through the 16-foot Lawrence Avenue conduit," now serving an enlarged area — "not only... the area now served by the present Lawrence Avenue Station but also... the area served by the Broadway relief sewer and sewers reaching the conduit west of Broadway" — and that "[t]he Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station at Lawrence Avenue and Broadway will be abandoned upon the completion of the North Branch Station" . Engineering Works confirms the same outcome independently: the new station is "replacing the Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station with a new electrically operated station of four times the present capacity" . The inland lift was not relocated or duplicated — it was eliminated once the conduit could deliver flow all the way to the river by gravity, and the system's sole remaining lift point moved to NBPS itself. The route beyond NBPS. Engineering Works documents the North Side Intercepting Sewer's actual alignment, resolving how NBPS connects onward to the treatment works. The main interceptor runs along the west bank of the North Branch and North Shore Channel — a southern branch beginning at Fullerton Avenue and following Elston Avenue, Washtenaw Avenue, Melrose Street, California Avenue, and Manner Avenue to the west side of the North Shore Channel, and a northern branch running south along the c

text: - 1898–1902 — new detail (July 2026). The City and Sanitary District's governing ordinances and agreements (Jan. 31 & Feb. 4, 1898; Nov. 18, 1899) fix the Lawrence Avenue conduit's design as a 16-foot-diameter conduit running "from Lake Michigan...to the Chicago River," but construction — contracted May 2, 1898 for a promised May 1900 completion — falls far behind (under 33% done by March 1901, under 40% by December 1901) and then stalls completely through December 1902 amid "the failure of the original contractors and...resulting legal complications"; the Sanitary District separately relocates the pumping station's site by land exchange in December 1902 after its own ordinance rerouting the North Branch channel renders the originally acquired parcel unusable . - 1900–1907 — Sanitary District builds lakefront intercepting sewers, eliminating direct lakefront sewage outfalls and creating the need for a pumping point on the North Branch . - 1907 — Resolved (July 2026). The City of Chicago completes the original, inland Lawrence Avenue Sewage Pumping Station (Lawrence Ave. at the C.M.&St.P.R.R./Northwestern Elevated crossing, east of old Evanston Avenue/Broadway), which pumps intercepted North Side sewage onward and forces lake water through to flush the North Branch; confirmed by two independent 1911/1919/1921-era Sanitary District primary sources, resolving both the date (1907, not 1909) and the site previously known only from an anonymous blog comment . The Sanitary District's own 1921 tabulation separately dates the related North Branch channel-straightening work between Belmont and Lawrence Avenues to May 10, 1904 – December 2, 1907, consistent with the conduit, pumping station, and river work in this reach finishing together after the earlier delays . - 1910 — The Sanitary District builds the North Branch Dam at River Park, where the newly dug North Shore Channel meets the natural North Branch, to hold the two waterways' differing elevations apart; it stands as the only "waterfall" on the Chicago River system until removed in 2018 (see "The North Branch Dam next door," above) . - 1921–1930 — resolved (July 2026), see "The 1928 hydraulic reconfiguration" above. doc: North Branch (Lawrence Avenue) Pumping Station
text: NBPS is operated as an outstation of the Terrence J. O'Brien WRP; an "OWRP Operating Engineer" at O'Brien WRP monitors the station remotely and is the point of contact (via extensions x88380/x88381) for entry, a silent intrusion alarm, and coordination of NPDES sampling . Field Services/Industrial Waste Division (IWD) North Office personnel hold the Medeco key needed for physical entry . Upon activation of the station's first storm pump, the OWRP Operating Engineer notifies the District's Systems Dispatcher, who assigns IWD-North staff to sample the discharge from an automatic composite sampler mounted on the south wall of the building's lower level . Sampling requirements under the O'Brien WRP NPDES permit include a one-gallon composite for general chemistry, a composite for trace metals, and a grab sample for pH (taken 30 minutes to 2 hours after the first pump activates) for every 24-hour period of discharge; results for BOD5/TOC, suspended solids, cadmium, and hardness are reported by MWRD's Monitoring & Research laboratory at the Lue-Hing R&D Complex . Operationally, NBPS is treated by MWRD as one of a short list of "major pumping stations" (alongside Racine Avenue Pumping Station on the South Fork of the South Branch of the Chicago River, and the 95th Street and 122nd Street Pumping Stations on the Calumet River) whose discharges into local waterways are separately, specifically monitored under the WRPs' NPDES permits . Every CSO event at NBPS also triggers a standing fish-kill response protocol: two days after a pumping event (or the following Monday if it falls on a weekend), MWRD's Channel Maintenance Unit dispatches boat crews to inspect the river downstream for fish kills, cleans up if warranted, and files both an oral and written report to the Illinois EPA . Water-quality context for the surrounding stretch of river is also actively managed: an instream aeration station at Webster Avenue adds oxygen to the North Branch (a companion station at Devon Avenue serves the North Shore Channel), controlled using a dissolved-oxygen probe read remotely at O'Brien WRP from the Ohio Street bridgehouse on the main Chicago River . The five storm-discharge pumps are activated on an as-needed basis during large storms specifically to draw down local combined-sewer elevations before they surcharge — a proactive, powered response rather than a passive weir overflow — which MWRD describes as distinguishing NBPS (along with Racine Avenue Pumping Station) from most

text: - July 2–3, 2023 storm. The National Weather Service recorded rainfall totals of roughly 3 to 7 inches across greater Chicago, with some localized areas exceeding 8 inches; the worst flooding hit the west and southwest sides and near-west/southwest suburbs . Even though the McCook Reservoir was only 17 percent full as of 2 a.m. on July 2, CSOs began by 8:19 a.m., and NBPS began actively releasing sewage and stormwater into the North Branch at 9:44 a.m. — before the region's primary TARP reservoir was anywhere near capacity. MWRD, responding publicly to the Chicago Tribune, used this event to explain that NBPS and Racine Avenue Pumping Station pump proactively to control local sewer elevations, rather than waiting for TARP itself to fill and spill by gravity . - March 11, 2026. Residents across several North Side neighborhoods along the North Branch of the Chicago River and North Shore Channel — including Lincoln Square, Albany Park, North Center, Irving Park, Horner Park, and Roscoe Village — reported a strong sewage-like ("skunk"-like, per some residents) odor. MWRD spokesperson Allison Fore confirmed District staff investigated and also detected the smell; search-indexed excerpts of the WTTW report state that "many pointed blame in the direction of a nearby pumping station" and that a Deep Tunnel drop shaft located near the North Branch Pumping Station was linked to the problem, and MWRD invited residents to report odors via its hotline (800-332-3867) or online system . Because NBPS sits directly on this stretch of river and adjoins one of the area's TARP drop shafts (Ronan Park), it is very likely implicated in or adjacent to this incident, but the full WTTW article text remained access-blocked (HTTP 403) on repeated retrieval attempts, so this is sourced only to search-engine-indexed excerpts rather than the primary text and is still flagged as an open question below. - 2018–2020 concrete erosion repair. MWRD's own contract documents describe the storm pumps' high-velocity discharge as having eroded the below-waterline concrete building columns by 20–50 percent of their original cross-section, necessitating the $4.6 million Contract 16-079-3D repair — itself a notable operational consequence of the station's storm-relief role . - 2024–2027 major structural rehabilitation (MWRD Contract 22-094-3D, awarded to Path Construction Company, Inc., March 7, 2024, for $22,593,795.00; contracted substantial completion August 2027), described above, undertaken bec

doc: North Branch (Lawrence Avenue) Pumping Station
section: Disambiguation: this is NOT a City of Chicago drinking-water pumping station
text: Because Lincoln Square has more than one historic "pumping station" from roughly the same era, it is worth stating plainly what NBPS is not. The Thomas Jefferson Pumping Station , at 2250 W. Eastwood Avenue — about a mile from NBPS — is a City of Chicago Department of Water Management facility that pressurizes purified Lake Michigan drinking water for distribution through the city's water mains; it was designed by city architect Argyle Robinson (city architect 1926–1929) and became operational in August 1928, almost exactly contemporaneous with NBPS's construction, but it has nothing to do with sewage or stormwater . Similarly, the Mayfair Pumping Station , at 4850 W. Wilson Avenue, is another City drinking-water pumping station (opened 1913 per some sources, 1915–1918 per city records — see the water-pumping document in this archive; originally steam-driven, later upgraded) supplying the northwest side and suburbs . Both are operated by the City of Chicago; NBPS is operated by MWRD, a separate unit of government, and moves wastewater and stormwater, not drinking water. General web searches for "North Branch Pumping Station" or "Lawrence Avenue Pumping Station" occasionally surface unrelated facilities of the same generic name elsewhere in the country (for example, a "North Pumping Station" in South Bend, Indiana, and a "North Branch" commuter-rail station in New Jersey turned up as false-positive image matches during this research and are not related in any way) [confirmed by direct verification during this research].

doc: TARP Phase I: The Deep Tunnel System
text: TARP Phase I — universally nicknamed "Deep Tunnel" — is the conveyance-and-interception half of the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago's (MWRD, formerly the Metropolitan Sanitary District, MSD) Tunnel and Reservoir Plan. It is a separate, later-built system from the 19th-century intercepting sewers described elsewhere in this archive: those shallow interceptors (built after 1907) still do the first-line job of carrying dry-weather sewage to the water reclamation plants (WRPs) . TARP Phase I exists for the moments when a storm overwhelms that shallow system. Combined sewers — carrying stormwater and sanitary sewage in the same pipe across roughly 360 square miles of Chicago and 51 suburbs — surcharge past the capacity of the intercepting sewers and the WRPs; historically this backed up into basements or spilled untreated, via combined sewer overflows (CSOs), into the Chicago River, the Calumet River, the Des Plaines River, and ultimately Lake Michigan . TARP intercepts that overflow before it reaches the waterways: local sewers drain into MWRD connecting structures, many with regulating gates or weirs, which under normal flow route water to the shallow interceptors but under storm flow divert the excess down vertical drop shafts into 109.4–110.4 miles of rock tunnels 8 to 33 feet in diameter, lying 150 to 300 feet below the waterways they parallel . From the tunnels, flow moves by gravity toward pumping stations and, since the 1980s–2010s, into the three Phase II reservoirs (Majewski, Thornton, McCook — covered in a companion document in this archive) for temporary storage until it can be pumped back up to a WRP for treatment . Upstream to downstream, Phase I is four separate tunnel systems that converge on shared low points: - The Upper Des Plaines (O'Hare) System , the smallest, drains an 11-square-mile combined-sewer area around O'Hare Airport (Des Plaines, Mount Prospect, Arlington Heights) and discharges toward the James C. Kirie Water Reclamation Plant in unincorporated Des Plaines Township, from 1998 onward via the Gloria Alitto Majewski Reservoir . - The Des Plaines System — named the "Lower Des Plaines Tunnel System" in MSDGC's original 1970s EPA environmental impact statements, distinguishing it from the separate Upper Des Plaines (O'Hare) system — runs along the Des Plaines River and its North Branch tributary, draining a wider swath of the northwest suburbs, and — together with Mainstream — feeds toward the McCook area .

doc: TARP Phase I: The Deep Tunnel System
text: Origins. Planning traces to 1967, when officials of the newly named Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago, the State of Illinois, Cook County, and the City of Chicago formed a Flood Control Coordinating Committee and, that November, a Technical Advisory Committee to evaluate the region's flood and pollution options; after 1967–68 discussions stalled, the effort was reactivated in November 1970 . The specific "deep tunnel" concept, however, predates that committee: Vinton W. Bacon , general superintendent of the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago from 1962 to 1970, is widely credited as the idea's originator, and Engineering News-Record profiled him as the sanitary engineer who "cleaned house" at the District during that tenure . The technical proposal that gave the idea real shape was the Harza-Bauer Report of May 1968 (Harza Engineering Company), which outlined a series of tunnels in the Niagaran and Galena-Platteville rock strata converging on a single mined/surface storage complex in the Calumet area — labeled "Alternate A, Original Deep Tunnel Plan" in the 1972 planning document . By the time the plan was finalized in 1972, Bacon was gone and the roster had changed: Ben Sosewitz was MSD General Superintendent, Forrest Neil was Chief Engineer, and Frank E. Dalton — later Dalton's own MWRDGC obituary credits him with having "conceived and led" the "$3.2 billion" Deep Tunnel project — held the title of Assistant Chief Engineer . In practice, both narratives are defensible and not mutually exclusive: contemporary reporting in 1979 also names Dalton as the MSD's "deputy chief engineer" who steered TARP "into construction and through the public arena," while Bacon is the earlier-tenure name credited with first floating a deep-tunnel concept for Chicago . Dalton went on to serve roughly a decade as MWRDGC General Superintendent later in his career, overseeing Deep Tunnel's continued build-out . The 1972 Chicago Underflow Plan. The Flood Control Coordinating Committee's consulting team — engineers from the City, MSD, County, State and federal agencies, plus outside firms Consoer Townsend and Associates, Greeley and Hansen, Harza Engineering, Metcalf and Eddy, and Warren and Van Praag — evaluated 23 distinct alternatives (labeled A through T, with several "pumped-storage" sub-variants), including full sewer separation (rejected at an estimated cost "upwards of four billion dollars" with no flood-control benefit), a Leffler Plan of Lak

doc: TARP Phase I: The Deep Tunnel System
text: In dry weather and light rain, local combined sewers drain through MWRD connecting structures into the shallow intercepting-sewer system exactly as they have since the early 20th century, bypassing TARP entirely . When flow exceeds interceptor or treatment-plant capacity, regulating gates, weirs, or orifices at the connecting structures divert the excess to nearby drop shafts, which carry it down 150–300 feet into the rock tunnels below the waterways . Air displaced by the rising water escapes through the drop shafts' separate air-side channel and rooftop vent chamber (with louvers added in 1985–86 to manage this more precisely), while water races through the tunnel network by gravity toward the pumping stations and Phase II reservoirs . Once tunnel storage and, since the 2010s, reservoir storage are both exhausted in a severe event, remaining overflow spills at the connecting structures directly to the waterways as a CSO — the residual roughly 15% of pollution load the tunnels alone do not capture . After a storm subsides, the Mainstream Pumping Station (dewatering Mainstream/Des Plaines tunnels and McCook Reservoir to Stickney WRP), the Calumet TARP Pumping Station (dewatering the Calumet tunnels, and since 2015 Thornton Reservoir, to Calumet WRP), and the O'Hare/Upper Des Plaines pumping arrangement (dewatering to Kirie WRP via Majewski Reservoir) empty the system back down at whatever rate the receiving WRP's treatment capacity allows . The system's original design target, per EPA's 1988 evaluation, was to dewater a full Mainstream tunnel within 60 hours, though MSDGC found that running pumps mainly during off-peak electricity hours (nights, weekends, and around holidays) saved several hundred thousand dollars a year in operating costs at some cost to that dewatering speed; the same evaluation flagged hydrogen sulfide released from stored sewage during pumpback as an ongoing corrosion and worker-safety concern that MSDGC attempted to manage by injecting oxygen into the pumped flow . MWRD's Collection Asset Management Plan (2022 update) formalizes annual inspection of drop manholes and connecting structures, condition assessment of TARP tunnel liners (noted as "relatively good" where limited manned entries have occurred, though the oldest tunnel segments — now 40-plus years old — are flagged for a more systematic asset-management approach going forward), and maintenance of the roughly 45 miles of intercepting sewer already rehabilitated under the Distri

doc: TARP Phase I: The Deep Tunnel System
text: - October 3, 1986 — the Jefferson/Monroe geyser. A heavy thunderstorm produced air-water geysers exceeding 65 feet at several points along the operating (but not yet complete) tunnel system, including a roughly 30-foot geyser at the downtown intersection of Jefferson and Monroe that lifted a 61-year-old motorist's Pontiac Bonneville and nearly drowned her in the ejected water; the tunnel system itself "performed satisfactorily," absorbing the storm's volume, but the air-entrapment/transient-pressure dynamics of rapid filling became a lasting cautionary example cited in wastewater-engineering literature on deep-tunnel design generally . MSDGC's documented response was not a physical retrofit but a modeling-driven operations program: the University of Minnesota's St. Anthony Falls Hydraulic Laboratory began hydraulic-transient modeling of the Mainstream system in 1988 (the first of a SAFL series that ran through at least 2002), recommending closure of tunnel sluice gates as the system approached full and informing subsequent fill-management procedures . - 1979–1985 — the cost-and-schedule controversy. A series of GAO reports dating from May 1978 — the first, PSAD-78-94 (May 24, 1978), priced the total program at roughly $8 billion and found no single federal agency had oversight responsibility — tracked the full-program cost estimate (tunnels, reservoirs, associated treatment-plant work, and financing/escalation combined — the same basket as the 1972 report's own $2.65 billion total-program figure) climbing to $8.5 billion in GAO's May 1979 report and to $10.2 billion (1980 dollars) in GAO's CED-81-51, dated January 21, 1981 — a letter report to Sen. Charles Percy that this pass obtained and read in full. Using a 10-percent annual inflation assumption, that same report projected the total would reach $12.5 billion "by 1983," almost certainly the basis for the $12.5 billion figure the Washington Post reported as a current GAO estimate in November 1982 — more, contemporary reporting noted, than the Trans-Alaska Pipeline had cost . The same GAO letter separately notes the Army Corps of Engineers' own estimate to complete TARP rose from $6.4 billion (1975) to over $10 billion (1980), while the Sanitary District's own estimate climbed from $2.6 billion (1972) to over $4 billion (1977) . CED-81-51's own Enclosure I itemizes that $10.15 billion 1980 estimate: $1.257B for funded Phase I tunnels/shafts/structures (47.0 mi.), $1.074B for unfunded Phase I (62.6 mi.), $

text: - Exact drop-shaft and connecting-structure counts remain unreconciled across sources — now with a third and fourth data point rather than a resolution. One peer-reviewed conference paper (Schmidt/Fitzpatrick et al., 2009) cites "256 drop shafts" and "over 600 connecting structures, pumping stations, and other appurtenances" for the TARP system as a whole (Phase I tunnels plus the reservoirs then existing); a University of Illinois hydrosystems-lab summary instead cites "over 250 drop shafts" and "over 400 connecting structures." MWRD's own March 2023 internal 50th-anniversary seminar states a fourth count, 264 drop shafts, for Phase I . A 1987 USACE report separately gives 132 drop shafts (4–17 ft diameter) for the Mainstream/Lower Des Plaines subsystem alone — not directly comparable to the system-wide figures above, but a reminder that even MWRD's own internal materials disagree . Neither MWRD status report reviewed here gives an authoritative Phase-I-only count; MWRD's Collection Asset Management Plan references a complete inventory in "Appendix 8" that was not accessible in this pass . - The precise 1985 "first Mainstream segment" mileage/endpoints could not be independently confirmed. Secondary sources describe "the first tunnel" coming online in 1985 and the Christian Science Monitor's 1985 dedication article puts 47 of 110 miles complete by that date, but no primary source located here states the specific length or exact endpoints (e.g., "Wilmette to Hodgkins") of that first operational Mainstream segment with certainty. - The Bacon-versus-Dalton "who conceived it" question is genuinely contested in the sources , not merely a research gap: Bacon (MSD general superintendent 1962–1970) is credited in several secondary accounts as originator of the deep-tunnel concept, while Dalton's own MWRDGC obituary claims he "conceived and led" the project; the 1968 Harza-Bauer engineering report is the earliest concrete technical proposal located. This document presents both attributions with their respective sourcing rather than resolving the dispute. - Individual pumping-station design capacities in MGD or cfs — partially resolved for Calumet and for Mainstream's dewatering target, still open for Mainstream's MGD/hp figures. doc: TARP Phase II: The Reservoirs (Majewski, Thornton, McCook)
text: TARP Phase II is the storage half of the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago's (MWRD) Tunnel and Reservoir Plan ("Deep Tunnel"). Phase I — 110.4 miles of concrete-lined rock tunnels, 8–33 ft in diameter, 150–300 ft underground, built 1975–2006 at a construction cost of $2.332 billion — intercepts combined-sewer overflows (CSOs) from a ~360-square-mile combined-sewer area covering Chicago and 51 suburbs, but the tunnels themselves store only 2.32 billion gallons (BG) . Phase II/CUP adds three terminal reservoirs that receive the tunnels' overflow and are "intended primarily for flood control… but also considerably enhance pollution control benefits being provided under Phase I" . The federal partner interest was defined by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers' (USACE) Chicagoland Underflow Plan (CUP), Final Phase I General Design Memorandum of 1986 . When McCook Stage 2 is finished the whole system will store 17.5 BG — reservoirs 15.15 BG (TARP share) plus tunnels 2.32 BG . Upstream-to-downstream, the four tunnel systems map onto the three reservoirs as follows : - Upper Des Plaines Tunnel System (6.6 mi, 70 million gallons (MG), 9–20 ft diameter; first TARP segment online, 1981) serves an 11-sq-mi combined-sewer area in Des Plaines, Mount Prospect and Arlington Heights near O'Hare, and discharges to the Gloria Alitto Majewski Reservoir (0.35 BG), an open basin adjacent to the James C. Kirie Water Reclamation Plant (WRP). Stored sewage is pumped back to Kirie WRP for treatment . - Mainstream Tunnel System (40.5 mi, 1.2 BG, 8–33 ft diameter, Wilmette to McCook; completed in stages 1985–2006) and Des Plaines Tunnel System (25.6–26.6 mi, 0.4 BG) together drain a 258-sq-mi service area — central Chicago plus 36 suburbs, 3.1 million people — and both feed the McCook Reservoir (3.5 BG operating; 10 BG at completion) in Bedford Park, wedged between the Chicago Sanitary & Ship Canal and the Des Plaines River. McCook is dewatered through distribution tunnels to the TARP Mainstream Pumping Station at 6100 River Road, Hodgkins, which lifts flow to the Stickney WRP . - Calumet Tunnel System (36.7 mi, 0.63 BG, 9–30 ft diameter; built 1986–2006) drains a 91-sq-mi area of the Far South Side and south suburbs and feeds the Thornton Composite Reservoir (7.9 BG) in the north lobe of the Thornton Quarry. Thornton's stored sewage is pumped ~5 miles north to the Calumet WRP in Riverdale for treatment and release to the Little Calumet River . Thornton addition

doc: TARP Phase II: The Reservoirs (Majewski, Thornton, McCook)
text: Majewski (the pilot reservoir). Under the CUP cost-share, MWRD as local sponsor acquired land rights and USACE Chicago District designed and built the reservoir next to Kirie WRP; it was completed in 1998 for about $45 million ($40.8M USACE contract, 75% federally funded, plus $4.0M in MWRD "betterments" — a control building, reservoir outflow control gates, and a monitoring system; nominal late-1990s dollars) . Originally the "O'Hare Reservoir" (the O'Hare CUP unit), it was renamed for Gloria Alitto Majewski, the MWRD commissioner (1984–2010) who was the District's first woman finance chair . It is the only one of the three that is a conventional excavated open basin rather than a converted quarry. USACE's July 1989 Phase II General Design Memorandum/EIS for the "O'Hare Reservoir" — first cost estimated at $20.52M in October 1989 prices, against a 1.15 benefit-cost ratio — documents the design in detail: a 105-acre parcel bounded by the Northwest Tollway and Elmhurst Road, of which the reservoir itself occupies roughly 30 acres at ground elevation; storage of 1,050 acre-feet (342.1 MG, the design figure later rounded to the "350 MG" cited everywhere else) between elevations −2.0 and +58.0 ft CCD, a working pool 22 ft below the average +80 ft CCD ground surface; excavation with a 15-ft bench at +46 ft CCD and 1-on-2 side slopes; and a 30-in. recompacted-clay bottom liner (minimum permeability 1×10⁻⁷ cm/sec) topped with 10 in. of roller-compacted concrete . This resolves most of the "Majewski physical dimensions" open question via a primary USACE source — including independently corroborating the 1,050-acre-foot figure that a low-confidence web lead had put forward — though the exact date/ordinance renaming the reservoir for Commissioner Majewski still was not located. Thornton (quarry conversion, world's largest CSO reservoir). The Thornton Quarry was first worked in the 1830s–40s (Wikipedia dates the earliest pits there to 1836, 1846 and 1850) and owned by Material Service Corp. from 1938 ; MWRD's own Thornton fact sheet instead says the quarry "dates to the 1860s" — the two cited sources disagree and neither is reconciled here . It exposes a Silurian reef dolomite prized for aggregate; the pit complex is about 1.5 miles long, 0.5 miles wide and up to 450 ft deep . MWRD chose it because bedrock rises nearly to the surface there . Under a 1998 mining agreement, operator Hanson Material Service (now Heidelberg Materials) mined roughly 76 million tons of sto

doc: TARP Phase II: The Reservoirs (Majewski, Thornton, McCook)
text: TARP is complete and fully operational except McCook Stage 2 . MWRD publishes live reservoir levels (a McCook live stream and a TARP reservoir-levels page) and NOAA maintains a gauge on the Mainstream Deep Tunnel at McCook . Combined sewage enters the reservoirs only after tunnel storage is exhausted; afterwards the water is pumped out — McCook via distribution tunnels to the Mainstream Pumping Station in Hodgkins and on to Stickney WRP; Thornton to Calumet WRP; Majewski to Kirie WRP — at rates limited by plant treatment capacity . Drawdown of a full Thornton takes on the order of a month; the blog-documented pumping pace in July 2026 was roughly 2 BG per 10 days . Water quality in storage is managed with the McCook coarse-bubble aeration system , floating solar-powered surface aerators that oxygenate the top layer to control odors at the reservoirs , sprayers/misting, and (from 2026) carbon filtration at Thornton . Groundwater protection is verified through an IEPA-approved monitoring program: 111 wells along the tunnels plus reservoir-specific networks — four wells at Majewski (QK-1 through QK-4, at the reservoir's four corners, plus nine privately owned water-supply wells within 1,000 ft tracked for reference) sampled for six consecutive weeks after each fill event, seven wells plus the Main Quarry Sump at Thornton (fill threshold −280 ft CCD), and five to seven wells at McCook (high-stage threshold −220 ft CCD) . Performance to date: CSO frequency system-wide has fallen from ~100 days per year pre-TARP to ~50 with the tunnels alone ; Thornton has captured over 99% of the volume entering its system and eliminated CSOs in its service area since 2020, protecting 556,000 people in 14 communities and roughly 182,000 structures ; Majewski has captured ~8.0 BG lifetime ; McCook Stage 1 ~137.9 BG through September 2025 . Benefit estimates (nominal-dollar figures from different years, tracking successive MWRD/USACE re-evaluations as the project grew): Thornton ~$40M/yr in flood-reduction benefits , with $713.7M in cumulative flood-damage-reduction benefits to its 14 communities reported through December 2025 ; McCook was projected at $90M/yr in an April 2008 early-CUP-planning snapshot , stated as "more than $143 million per year" in MWRD's TARP Status Report as of June 30, 2020 , and "more than $175 million per year" in the 2025 fact sheet, status report and 2026 budget-in-brief ; MWRD's engineering chief has cited a benefit-cost ratio above 3 for McCook . doc: TARP Phase II: The Reservoirs (Majewski, Thornton, McCook)
text: - May 2020 storms. With the system overwhelmed, MWRD reversed the waterways into Lake Michigan on May 15 and May 17–18, 2020 — the backstop action TARP is designed to avoid [17 (context)]. (Reservoir-era reversals remain rare.) - June 2019. Thornton's previous fill record, roughly 55% of capacity . - July 2–4, 2026 storm (record fill). After 8+ inches of rain in 30 days including 3+ inches on July 3–4, McCook hit 100% on July 3 — its sixth time at capacity in 2026 alone, versus five times in all of 2021–2025 — and Thornton reached 94% (~7.4 BG), by far its record; the system as a whole held over 13 BG, "nearly 20,000 Olympic-sized swimming pools," while Majewski stayed comparatively empty . CSOs still reached the Chicago River over three days, and the city logged ~400 basement-flooding and ~600 street-flooding complaints . Illinois state climatologist Trent Ford warned that saturated soils and nearly full reservoirs raise regionwide flood risk even from moderate rain . - July 2026 odor crisis at Thornton. A nearly full, slowly draining reservoir of raw combined sewage produced a "rotten eggs" hydrogen-sulfide stench that residents of Thornton/South Holland called "horrific" and "putrid"; MWRD deployed large aerators, acknowledged its aerator-and-sprayer systems only partially work at such fill levels, and announced the $5M carbon-filter and misting projects (due Dec 2026, $8.6M cumulative odor spend) . Drawdown was expected to take at least a month (Thornton was down to 82% and McCook to 54% by July 11) . - Stage 2 schedule slip. The consent-decree McCook completion (mining by Dec 31 2028, operational Dec 31 2029) has been pushed to an estimated Dec 31, 2032 because commercial demand for Vulcan's stone — not construction funding — sets the excavation pace . - A useful contrast: Thornton was excavated for free in rock-sale terms (Hanson bought the right to mine 76M tons) , while McCook cost MWRD ~$81.6M (updated from ~$79.4M as of the June 2025 report) in "Vulcan rock mining hard costs less royalty," still rising because Stage 2 mining continues, plus the entire mining plant, because there MWRD owns the hole and pays for extraction net of stone royalties .

doc: Stickney Water Reclamation Plant
text: The Stickney Water Reclamation Plant (WRP), owned and operated by the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago (MWRD), sits on 413 acres at 6001 West Pershing Road in the suburb of Cicero, Illinois, immediately adjacent to the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal (CSSC) . It is the flagship — and by acreage and flow the largest — of the MWRD's seven water reclamation plants, and is widely cited as the largest wastewater treatment plant in the world by treatment volume . Inflow. Stickney receives sanitary and combined sewage from central Chicago and 46 suburban communities across a 260-square-mile service area, serving roughly 2.2–2.3 million people (sources differ; see Open Questions) . Wastewater and stormwater collected in local sewers is carried to Stickney through the MWRD's network of intercepting sewers and pumping stations. Major intercepting sewers historically feeding the plant include the Salt Creek intercepting sewer (7 ft diameter), the Argo sewer (10 ft), the West Side 1 sewer (17 ft), and the double-barreled Southwest 1 sewer (two 16 ft barrels) . On the south side, the Racine Avenue Pumping Station — built in 1939 and substantially rebuilt/expanded in 1964 — collects sewage from a roughly 36-square-mile area stretching from Western Avenue east to the lakefront and from 87th Street north to Taylor Street, and lifts it into the interceptor system that feeds Stickney . Solids and sludge generated at four smaller MWRD plants — the John E. Egan, James C. Kirie, Terrence J. O'Brien, and Lemont WRPs — are also piped or hauled to Stickney, where they are combined with Stickney's own solids and anaerobically digested . During major storms, combined sewage and stormwater captured in the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan's (TARP) McCook Reservoir — situated a few miles from Stickney in Hodgkins/Bedford Park — is pumped to Stickney for treatment when the reservoir is dewatered . Outflow. After treatment, Stickney discharges its cleaned effluent directly to the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal, a segment of the Chicago Area Waterway System (CAWS) that carries the flow (via the Des Plaines and Illinois Rivers) toward the Mississippi River and ultimately the Gulf of Mexico . Unlike two of MWRD's other large plants (Calumet and O'Brien), Stickney does not disinfect its effluent before discharge; this is discussed under Current Operations below. doc: Stickney Water Reclamation Plant
text: Stickney's origin lies in the Sanitary District of Chicago's response to a legal and environmental crisis. The Sanitary District (chartered May 29, 1889, and renamed the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago in 1955 and the MWRD in 1989) had spent its first three decades relying almost entirely on dilution — reversing the Chicago River in 1900 via the 28-mile Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal, and later the North Shore Channel (1910) and Cal-Sag Channel (1922) — to carry Chicago's raw sewage away from Lake Michigan and toward the Illinois and Mississippi Rivers . That approach ran into its limit when a series of U.S. Supreme Court decisions in the 1920s and a final 1930 ruling forced a sharp reduction in the volume of Lake Michigan water the District was permitted to divert for dilution . With less water available to dilute sewage, the District was compelled to build actual treatment plants. The 1920s accordingly saw a first wave of Sanitary District treatment works: the Calumet works (1922, the District's first treatment plant), the North Side works (1928), and — completing the shift — the West Side works, which entered service on June 2, 1930 (a few secondary sources, including the Encyclopedia of Chicago, date "West Side works" to 1931; MWRD's own fact sheet gives 1930) . The West Side plant used the German-designed Imhoff tank as its core treatment technology: two-story concrete chambers combining sedimentation in an upper flow-through chamber with anaerobic sludge digestion in a lower chamber, a design associated with engineer Karl Imhoff. Over time the plant's Imhoff tanks were organized into three batteries, lettered A through C, ultimately totaling roughly 108 individual tanks [6-analog]. Batteries A and B entered service with the rest of the West Side plant in 1930; construction of Battery C began in 1931 but was halted in 1932 for lack of funds, and only resumed after new federal financing was secured in late 1933 (below). That same reorganization folded in the flow of a separate "Des Plaines River plant," which was abandoned in 1931 . As the West Side works filled to capacity treating growing volumes from the South and West Sides — including roughly half the waste of the Union Stock Yards — the District built a second, larger facility immediately to the south: the Southwest Sewage Treatment Works. text: - 1930 — West Side Sewage Treatment Works enters service (June 2), using Imhoff tanks for combined settling/digestion . - 1939 — Southwest Sewage Treatment Works enters service (September 7), adding activated-sludge secondary treatment capacity and a new intercepting sewer system draining 12.5 miles of the city, including half the stockyards' waste . - 1939 — Racine Avenue Pumping Station (RAPS) built to lift South Side sewage into the interceptor system feeding the combined West-Southwest plant; per a 2020 USACE report, RAPS began operation with six pumps discharging raw sewage directly to Bubbly Creek . - 1940s — Secondary (activated-sludge) aeration tanks progressively added across the site to supplement primary Imhoff-tank treatment, following broader adoption of activated-sludge technology by Sanitary District engineers . - 1949 — MWRD's own historical timeline dates the operational combination of the West and Southwest plants to this year; per USACE, this is also when RAPS stopped discharging dry-weather sewage to Bubbly Creek and began diverting it instead to the West-Southwest plant . - 1954 — RAPS expanded from 6 to 14 centrifugal pumps (West Side: 3 units at 375 cfs, 2 at 400 cfs, 2 at 500 cfs; East Side: 3 units at 375 cfs, 4 at 500 cfs), per a 2020 USACE report — a different expansion date than the "1964 rebuild" reported elsewhere; not reconciled . - 1964 — Racine Avenue Pumping Station rebuilt/expanded on Pershing Road to replace the smaller original structure, increasing its South Side interceptor capacity . - 1971 — MWRD's Fulton County "Prairie Plan" land-reclamation program begins (per MWRD's own historical timeline, superseding this document's earlier "1970" figure); it wins the ASCE's "Special Civil Engineering Achievement Award" in 1974 . - 1972 — MWRD (then Metropolitan Sanitary District) adopts the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP), the multibillion-dollar "Deep Tunnel" program that would eventually include the McCook Reservoir feeding into Stickney . - 1976–1979 — A federal Environmental Impact Statement for the TARP Calumet Tunnel System documents a parallel "West-Southwest treatment plant expansion project" estimated at $666.3 million, targeted for Step 3 federal (FWPCA) construction-grant funding by December 1979, within a combined roughly $1.02–1.13 billion Calumet/West-Southwest treatment-plant-improvement program — a 1970s capital-expansion episode not otherwise documented in MWRD's own retrospective materials . - 1984 (March 2

doc: Stickney Water Reclamation Plant
text: Stickney today operates as a conventional primary/secondary activated-sludge plant without a tertiary filtration step, treating a design average flow of 1,200 MGD (peak/permitted hydraulic capacity 1.44 BGD, i.e., roughly 1 million gallons per minute at max) and an actual 2024 annual average flow of 685 MGD . Roughly 397 employees staff the 413-acre site . Incoming wastewater passes coarse screens, aerated grit removal, and primary settling (removing an estimated 60–80% of solids), then flows by gravity into roughly 36 acres of activated-sludge aeration tanks where a cultivated community of microorganisms consumes dissolved and suspended organic matter, followed by final settling before discharge . Solids removed at each stage — plus digested/dewatered solids piped or hauled in from the Egan, Kirie, O'Brien, and Lemont WRPs — are thickened, anaerobically digested (generating digester gas used to help heat plant buildings and digesters), dewatered by centrifuge, and then either air-dried in lagoons at the adjacent Lawndale Avenue Solids Management Area, composted at the Harlem Avenue Solids Management Area in Forest View, applied directly as Class B biosolids to farmland by contractors (Synagro Midwest and Stewart Environmental in 2024), or converted to Class A "Exceptional Quality" heat-dried pellets by the on-site, privately run Metropolitan Biosolids Management facility . In 2024, Stickney's combined process train produced 119,234 dry tons of biosolids, of which 105,144 dry tons were beneficially reused (59,234 DT applied as Class B biosolids to farmland; 16,043 DT of Exceptional Quality biosolids — 14,321 DT air-dried, 1,721 DT composted — applied to golf courses, parks, and other urban land; and 29,867 DT dewatered and conveyed to the on-site MBM facility, where it was pelletized and sold as fertilizer) . None of Stickney's biosolids went to landfill in 2024 . Notably, Stickney does not disinfect its effluent before discharge to the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal. MWRD's large plants stopped routine chlorination in 1985, both because chlorine residuals were toxic to fish and because the CSSC — an artificial, industrial waterway — was not then designated for primary recreational contact . That regulatory picture has partly shifted: since 2011, MWRD has installed disinfection facilities at its Calumet and O'Brien WRPs to protect increasing recreational use of the North Branch Chicago River and Calumet-area waterways (operational for the 2016 recreationa

multiple fire departments responded and contained the blaze; no injuries, no evacuations, no damage to other buildings; cause was under investigation . - July 2023 — More than eight inches of rain (National Weather Service data cited in Illinois EPA's 2024 triennial-review response; doc: Calumet Water Reclamation Plant & Terrence J. O'Brien Water Reclamation Plant
text: The Calumet Water Reclamation Plant (WRP) , 400 E. 130th Street, Chicago, is the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago's (MWRD) oldest continuously operating treatment works, in service since 1922 . It sits on 275–470 acres (sources differ; see Facilities table) on the Little Calumet River in the Riverdale/Hegewisch area of the Far South Side . It collects domestic and industrial sewage, plus combined stormwater, from Chicago's South Side and 48 southern Cook County suburbs — an area MWRD describes as roughly 300 square miles serving "over one million people" (MWRD's 2025 fact sheet) or, per an earlier engineering document, a design population of 1,053,160 . Two emergency high-level combined-sewer-overflow (CSO) outfalls (numbered 151 and 752 in its NPDES permit, at the 95th Street and 122nd Street pump stations) discharge to the Little Calumet River only when the plant and its Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) storage are overwhelmed . Since 1986–2015, Calumet has been the discharge point for the Calumet TARP tunnel system and the Thornton Composite Reservoir : a 36.7-mile ring of deep rock tunnels (0.63 billion gallons/BG capacity) culminating in the 7.9-BG Thornton Composite Reservoir in South Holland, about five miles south of the plant; when storms fill the reservoir, its contents are pumped north to Calumet WRP for full treatment before release to the Little Calumet River . Treated effluent leaves Calumet WRP into the Little Calumet River , which flows west to the Cal-Sag Channel and eventually the Illinois Waterway system, never returning to Lake Michigan . The Terrence J. O'Brien Water Reclamation Plant — built as the North Side Sewage Treatment Works and renamed in 2012 for longtime MWRD Board President Terrence J. O'Brien — sits at 3500 W. Howard Street, Skokie, on the city/suburban boundary at the North Shore Channel . It receives sewage and combined stormwater from Chicago north of Fullerton Avenue and 17 northern suburbs (Evanston, Skokie, Wilmette, Winnetka, Glencoe, Northbrook, Morton Grove, Niles, Park Ridge, Des Plaines and others), an area of 143 square miles serving roughly 1.3 million people . Its main sanitary inflow arrives via the intercepting-sewer network and the North Branch Pumping Station , which also functions as a TARP dropshaft/CSO outfall (Discharge No. 107) tied into the Mainstream Tunnel System, which in turn drains to the McCook Reservoir under construction in Hodgkins . doc: Calumet Water Reclamation Plant & Terrence J. O'Brien Water Reclamation Plant
text: Both plants were built by the Sanitary District of Chicago (renamed MWRD in 1988) as the second wave of its sanitation infrastructure, after the 1900 reversal of the Chicago River and completion of the North Shore Channel (1910) and Cal-Sag Channel (1922) had diluted sewage but not treated it . Facing mounting local and Illinois-Missouri litigation and public-health pressure to treat effluent before discharge, the District began building dedicated sewage treatment works in the 1910s–1920s: Calumet (1922), North Side (1928), West Side/Southwest (West Side opened June 2, 1930 per MWRD's own fact sheet, 1931 per the Encyclopedia of Chicago and other secondary sources; Southwest 1939 — later combined into Stickney) . Imhoff-tank technology, long assumed by this document to have been rejected for both Calumet and North Side in favor of pure activated-sludge design, was in fact used at Calumet as well as at West Side: MWRD's own historical-photo archive documents "Imhoff tank construction at the West Side Sewage Treatment Works, one of two treatment plants which were combined and eventually became the Stickney Water Reclamation Plant, on August 5, 1927" , while independent primary sources — the District's own consulting engineers' 1927 report and its 1928 Engineering Works volume — separately confirm Calumet ran Imhoff tanks (55 MGD capacity) alongside a smaller experimental activated-sludge component well into the late 1920s (see Calumet paragraph and Open Questions, below) . North Side, by contrast, remains confirmed as activated-sludge from the outset. Calumet. A construction contract was let September 6, 1920, and MWRD dates the completed plant's opening to September 11, 1922 — the same month the Cal-Sag Channel first went into operation (gates opened Aug. 18, 1922; fully operational Aug. 26, 1922) . A federal court's later factual findings in a 1946 patent-infringement case state that the Calumet plant "was placed in operation June 5, 1923," after the same 1920 contract; a contemporary December 1923 Sanitary District memorandum independently confirms and refines this, stating "The Calumet Treatment Plant was begun in 1920, the sedimentation tanks completed and put in operation in September, 1922, and the tanks for the Activated Sludge Process put in service in May, 1923" — primary-source proof (within about a month of the court's later-cited date) that primary/sedimentation treatment opened in 1922 while activated-sludge secondary treatment followed in 1923

text: - 1922–1928 — Calumet (dual Imhoff-tank/activated-sludge process, 55-MGD Imhoff capacity per an independent 1927 engineering report) and North Side/O'Brien (activated sludge from the outset) works built; West Side works (opened June 2, 1930 per MWRD, 1931 per the Encyclopedia of Chicago) and Southwest works (1939) follow, later merged into Stickney . - 1924 — Activated Sludge, Inc. files suit against the Sanitary District of Chicago in federal court over its patented activated-sludge process (a parallel suit was also filed against Milwaukee's sewerage commission), opening a legal dispute that would not reach final judgment for 22 years . - 1925 (March 3) — U.S. War Department issues the Sanitary District's Lake Michigan diversion permit (8,500 cfs), Condition 4 of which requires the District to complete sewage-treatment works providing the equivalent of 100% treatment for a population of at least 1,200,000 by December 31, 1929 — a concrete federal deadline driving the North Side Works' completion timeline . - 1932–1935 — Activated Sludge, Inc. patent-infringement trial against the Sanitary District is heard (May 1932 – June 1933) and argued (March 1934); liability is found in a 1935 decree, with damages not finally set until 1946 . - 1934 — A federal review of District plans records Calumet's capacity at 186 MGD and North Side's at 200 MGD, reflecting post-1920s expansion of activated-sludge capacity at both plants . - 1946 — Federal court (N.D. Ill.) rules in Activated Sludge, Inc. v. Sanitary District of Chicago , 64 F. Supp. 25, setting infringement-era damages for the District's use of patented activated-sludge process at the Calumet, Des Plaines, and North Side plants; the court's opinion is a rare primary source fixing exact contract and start-up dates for all three plants . - 1975–2006 — TARP "Deep Tunnel" tunnel construction proceeds regionwide; the O'Brien-tributary Mainstream Tunnel System (40.5 mi, 1.2 BG) and the Calumet-tributary tunnel system are built in phases; full tunnel system operational 2006 . - 1986 — First leg of the Calumet Tunnel System (Cal-Sag Channel Leg, 186 million gallons) completed, beginning TARP service to Calumet WRP's drainage area . - 1988 — Sanitary District of Chicago is renamed the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago . - 1996–2006 — Remaining legs of the Calumet Tunnel System completed: 140th Street/Indiana Leg (236 MG) in 1996; Torrence Avenue Leg (141 MG) and Little Calumet Leg (59 MG) in 200

doc: Calumet Water Reclamation Plant & Terrence J. O'Brien Water Reclamation Plant
text: Calumet WRP employs 268 people on what MWRD's own materials describe inconsistently as either "275.4 acres" (2025 fact sheet) or "470 acres" (2025 locations webpage) and treats a design average of 354 MGD (design maximum 430 MGD), discharging to the Little Calumet River . It serves Chicago and 48 suburbs including Blue Island, Oak Lawn, Tinley Park, Orland Park, Calumet City, Harvey, and South Holland . Treatment is conventional: coarse screening, aerated grit removal, primary settling (60–80% solids removal), secondary activated-sludge treatment with mechanical aeration, final settling, and — since 2015 — chlorination/dechlorination disinfection before release . MWRD states wastewater takes roughly 4.5 hours (dry weather) to reach the plant from Blue Island and about 8 hours to pass through treatment . Solids (about 100 dry tons/day) are digested, centrifuge-dewatered, and air-dried at the adjacent Calumet Solids Management Area, producing biosolids used on golf courses, parks, farmland, and reclamation sites, including the Chicago Park District's Ping Tom Park . The plant also converts fats, oils and grease into methane to offset natural-gas purchases and is evaluating a digester-gas energy-generation system whose design work is expected to inform similar planning at Stickney . Since February 2025, a ferric-chloride Chemical Phosphorus Removal Facility (awarded August 2022) has supplemented treatment to meet NPDES phosphorus limits, while MWRD's Monitoring & Research Department continues experimental work on a fully biological phosphorus-removal alternative for Calumet's influent stream . O'Brien WRP employs 187 people across 14 buildings on 97 acres (a 2019-vintage MWRD fact sheet said 12; the current locations page and Oct. 2025 fact sheet both say 14), with a design average flow of 333 MGD (design maximum 450 MGD) but a current average flow near 230 MGD . It serves Chicago north of Fullerton Avenue plus Evanston, Skokie, Wilmette, Winnetka, Glencoe, Northbrook, Northfield, Glenview, Golf, Morton Grove, Niles, Lincolnwood, Park Ridge, Harwood Heights, Des Plaines, and Kenilworth . Treatment mirrors Calumet through the secondary stage, then passes through the 896-lamp UV disinfection facility before discharge to the North Shore Channel . MWRD states wastewater from Lincolnwood can reach the plant in under 30 minutes and takes about 12 hours to complete treatment . All solids are pumped to Stickney WRP for digestion rather than processed on-site . The pl

Ill.) ran from the suit's 1924 filing through trial (May 1932 – June 1933), argument (March 1934), a 1935 liability decree, and finally the 1946 compensatory-damages ruling — a 22-year case tied to the District's use of the patented activated-sludge process at the Calumet, Des Plaines, and North Side plants; one of relatively few disputes in U.S. water-infrastructure history litigated over sewage-treatment process patents, and a rare primary source for exact plant start-up dates . - May 17 – October 30, 2015 — Construction activity for O'Brien's new UV disinfection facility triggered the collapse of an adjacent CTA Yellow Line (Skokie Swift) embankment, closing the line for over five months at an estimated $3.5 million cost to CTA — an unusual case of a wastewater-plant capital project directly damaging adjacent transit infrastructure . - July 4th weekend, 2023 — Record stormwater inflows during Independence Day flooding sent about 3.5 billion gallons into Calumet's system without exceeding half of its ~8-billion-gallon emergency quarry storage, illustrating the plant's built-in flood-resilience margin as extreme-rainfall frequency increases . - 2015–2016 disinfection rollout — Completion of chlorination/dechlorination at Calumet (2015) and UV at O'Brien (2016) ended Chicago's status, per MWRD and EPA officials at the time, as the only major U.S. city releasing undisinfected treated sewage effluent into its waterways, directly enabling expanded recreational contact (kayaking, canoeing) on the Chicago Area Waterway System . - 2026 (June 18) — MWRD Board authorized a $409.4 million sludge-removal contract to Michels Construction as part of the Battery E project at O'Brien — described by observers as the largest single contract in MWRD's history — signaling a new multi-decade capital-investment cycle at the 98-year-old plant .

doc: The Suburban Satellite Plants: Kirie, Egan, Hanover Park, and Lemont Water Reclamation Plants
text: The Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago (MWRD) operates seven water reclamation plants (WRPs) across its roughly 882–883-square-mile service area covering Chicago and 125–128 Cook County suburbs (the exact community count varies slightly by fact-sheet vintage) . Two of the seven — Stickney and Calumet — are the enormous, century-old plants that anchor the system's Deep Tunnel-fed core. The other five are smaller "satellite" plants built or expanded in the mid-to-late 20th century to serve suburban areas too far from the core interceptors to be economical to pipe downtown. This document covers four of those satellites, all in the northwest and southwest suburbs: James C. Kirie WRP (Des Plaines), John E. Egan WRP (Schaumburg), Hanover Park WRP , and Lemont WRP . - Kirie WRP (701 West Oakton Street, Des Plaines) receives sanitary flow from local sewers in Des Plaines, Elk Grove Village, Mount Prospect, Arlington Heights, Rolling Meadows, Wheeling, Prospect Heights, Buffalo Grove, Elk Grove Township, and the Forest River and Northfield Wood Sanitary Districts, treats it, and discharges the cleaned effluent to Higgins Creek , a small tributary of the Des Plaines River near O'Hare International Airport . Kirie is a "satellite" plant in the specific engineering sense that it performs no on-site solids digestion: raw sludge pumped off during primary and secondary treatment is piped to the Egan WRP for digestion, then on to O'Brien WRP , and finally to Stickney WRP for final dewatering and drying . Combined-sewer overflow (CSO) from Kirie's service area is captured by the Upper Des Plaines Tunnel System, a small, self-contained component of the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP) that stores flow in the Gloria Alitto Majewski Reservoir near O'Hare — 0.35 BG (350 million gallons) per MWRD's TARP fact sheet, though MWRD's own Kirie-specific fact sheet states 343 million gallons — before pumping it back to Kirie for treatment. Kirie's own 1975 planning-era environmental impact statement (filed when the plant was still called the "O'Hare WRP") describes the interceptor system feeding it in street-level detail: a tunnel running along Elmhurst Road between the Northwest Tollway and Weller Creek, then along Weller Creek itself between Elmhurst Road and Central Road, with a branch extending from Elmhurst Road/Lonnquist Boulevard east to William Street, then north on William Street to a point north of Rand Road and Isabella Street, and a further 9-foot-d

doc: The Suburban Satellite Plants: Kirie, Egan, Hanover Park, and Lemont Water Reclamation Plants
text: None of these four plants was part of the original Sanitary District of Chicago's 1889–1928 canal-and-dilution system; all were built decades later by the district under its 1955–1988 name, the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago , as suburban Cook County urbanized after World War II and Illinois (then federal) water-quality law increasingly required treatment rather than dilution . (The district was renamed the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago effective January 1, 1989 .) Hanover Park was the earliest of the four, entering service July 19, 1963, in the midst of Chicago's postwar suburban building boom in northwestern Cook County . Roughly a decade later it became nationally notable: a secondary source describes Hanover Park's sand-filtration tertiary system, installed around 1971, as "the first of their kind to go into service" and states the plant was "the nation's first major tertiary wastewater treatment facility" ; a second, independent local-history source corroborates the general idea but in softer language, describing the 1970 plant Nixon visited only as "one of the first of its kind in the nation" . That claim is corroborated by an extraordinary documented event: on February 6, 1970 , President Richard Nixon personally toured the "Hanover Sewage Treatment Works" as part of a Chicago-area environmental-policy trip, accompanied by the governors of Illinois (Richard Ogilvie), Indiana (Edgar Whitcomb), Michigan (William Milliken), and Wisconsin (Warren Knowles), White House aide John Ehrlichman, and Benjamin Sosewitz, then Acting General Manager of the Metropolitan Sanitary District . H. R. Haldeman's diary records that area schools let out for the visit, crowds were "very enthusiastic," Nixon "did not bring his overcoat" despite freezing conditions, and the sound system failed, forcing Nixon to address the crowd with a bullhorn . The visit came the same day as a meeting of the Cabinet Committee on the Environment and the Council on Environmental Quality held not at the White House but at the Field Museum of Natural History in Chicago — attended by CEQ Chairman Russell Train, the same four governors, and other Cabinet members — situating Hanover Park's advanced treatment technology as a showcase for the administration's emerging clean-water agenda a few months before the EPA's creation later that year . Egan WRP followed, entering service December 16, 1975, and receiving a formal dedication ceremony on June 4, 1

doc: The Suburban Satellite Plants: Kirie, Egan, Hanover Park, and Lemont Water Reclamation Plants
text: - 1966 — MSDGC purchases the 104-acre "Site 1" at Oakton Street and Elmhurst Road for what becomes Kirie WRP, after the City of Chicago rejects MSDGC's preferred remote site on O'Hare Airport land; the purchase, made the day after a meeting with Des Plaines officials, triggers roughly nine years of litigation and public opposition (see Original construction and Notable events) . - 1967 — MSDGC contracts with Brown and Caldwell (agreement dated July 27, 1967) to design the O'Hare (Kirie) plant around the Site 1 location; design reports follow in February and June 1968 . - 1969 — MWRD purchases the 200-acre Fischer Farm adjoining Hanover Park WRP and constructs the Upper DuPage Reservoir (≈75 million gallons of stormwater storage capacity), establishing what remains an active biosolids land-application program to this day . - ca. 1971 — Hanover Park WRP installs sand-media tertiary filters, described by a secondary source as the first such filters in the nation to enter service; the installation prompts President Nixon's February 1970 visit and subsequent dedication-era attention (visit predates filter completion, consistent with a groundbreaking/preview visit) . - June 1972 — Lemont's original 1961 works (pump station, grit chamber, two Imhoff tanks, trickling filter, final settling tank) is entirely demolished and replaced by a new activated-sludge plant built on the same foundations . - 1973 — MSDGC publicly floats, then does not pursue, building the O'Hare (Kirie) plant underground in the Swedish style for an estimated $5–10 million savings, roughly six years into final design of the above-ground plant that was ultimately built . - June 4, 1976 — Formal dedication of Egan WRP, one year after it entered service; ceremony guest register, a same-day Chicago Tribune, a photo of the sitting Board of Commissioners, a Bicentennial coin proof/set, and 1976 postage stamps are sealed into a plant time capsule . - 1982 — U.S. EPA Region V names Egan WRP the "best operated and maintained" large treatment plant in the region, seven years after opening . - August 1987 — Severe storm-driven sewer back-up flooding across the O'Hare TARP System's combined-sewer area damages an estimated 6,000 homes in Mount Prospect alone; the flood is later cited in USACE's Phase II design memorandum as evidence of the O'Hare System's ongoing basement-flooding problem, part of the case for the terminal reservoir that becomes the Majewski Reservoir . - 1989 — Congress's Water Resources D

doc: The Suburban Satellite Plants: Kirie, Egan, Hanover Park, and Lemont Water Reclamation Plants
text: All four plants remain in continuous operation as of 2026, treating a combined average of roughly 96 MGD against a combined design-average capacity of roughly 96 MGD and combined peak capacity above 186 MGD (Kirie 52/110, Egan 30/50, Hanover Park 12/22, Lemont 2.3/4) . All employ the standard MWRD three-stage process — primary screening and grit/solids removal, secondary biological (activated-sludge) treatment, and tertiary polishing — with tertiary treatment tailored to each plant's receiving stream: dual-media sand/anthracite filtration at Egan, sand filtration at Hanover Park, and filtration plus chlorination/dechlorination at Kirie and Lemont . Treatment time from raw sewage to discharge is cited by MWRD as roughly 24 hours at Kirie, 7.8 hours at Egan (for design average flow), 12 hours at Hanover Park, and 10 hours at Lemont . A 2012 Illinois Institute of Technology master's thesis that built a process-simulation model of Kirie found the plant functions in practice as "a nitrification plant with no separate distinct denitrification process," with ammonia oxidation occurring incidentally in the aeration basins rather than in a dedicated nitrification unit, and that simulated wet-weather swings in flow and temperature — rather than any single design flaw — were the dominant drivers of effluent-quality variability . Kirie's current average annual day flow is about 35.8 MGD — well below its 52 MGD design average — and its treatment configuration is in fact a two-stage plant (see Original construction) whose 2nd-stage aeration/clarifier train is presently run as a pass-through rather than an active nitrification stage . An earlier, independent before/after assessment — a 1994 USGS study using MWRD/IEPA monitoring data from 1978–88 — compared Higgins Creek and Salt Creek water quality before and after Kirie (May 1980) and Egan came online, and found no uniform improvement or decline: downstream of Kirie's outfall, statistically significant increases in chloride, ammonia-nitrogen, phosphorus, and cyanide concentrations were measured after 1980, while numerous other constituents (chemical oxygen demand, fecal coliform, suspended solids) fell significantly — a net effect the study attributed mainly to TARP's elimination of combined-sewer overflows rather than to the treatment process itself. The same study found no major changes to Kirie's treatment process between 1978 and 1988, and confirmed the plant has chlorinated its effluent since first operating in 198

doc: The Suburban Satellite Plants: Kirie, Egan, Hanover Park, and Lemont Water Reclamation Plants
text: - 1966–1975 — Kirie's construction site (the "Oakton-Elmhurst" parcel at Elmhurst Road and Oakton Street) was the subject of a near-decade-long fight between the City of Des Plaines and MSDGC — arguably the most contentious pre-opening history of any of the four plants, rivaling the Nixon visit for historical color even though it drew far less attention at the time: two zoning lawsuits (the first ultimately reversed in MSDGC's favor by the Illinois Supreme Court, January 25, 1971), USEPA hearing testimony that former MSDGC superintendent Vinton Bacon had turned against the site over concerns for his own family's health, a public meeting where more than 300 residents confronted MSD Superintendent Benjamin Sosewitz (August 8, 1972), a 906-signature resident petition (February 8, 1973), and a since-abandoned 1973 proposal to build the plant underground in the Swedish style to save an estimated $5–10 million . - February 6, 1970 — President Nixon's inspection tour of Hanover Park's sewage treatment works, detailed above, remains the single most historically notable opening-era event associated with any of the four plants: a sitting U.S. president touring a suburban Chicago sewage plant, accompanied by four state governors, amid freezing weather and a failed sound system . - August 1987 — A severe storm caused sewer back-up flooding across the O'Hare TARP System's combined-sewer area, damaging an estimated 6,000 homes in the Village of Mount Prospect alone (Village crews hauled away more than 287,000 pounds of flood-damaged household goods, at an estimated $285,000 cost to the Village); USACE's 1989 Phase II design memorandum for what became the Gloria Alitto Majewski Reservoir cites the flood as evidence of the O'Hare System's ongoing basement-flooding problem that the reservoir was built to address . - September 2008 — Heavy regional rainfall required staff to sandbag Hanover Park WRP buildings against flooding, illustrating the plant's location on the flood-prone West Branch DuPage River corridor even with the adjacent Upper DuPage Reservoir in place . - 2012–2015 — Kirie's sewerthermal pilot project drew an international technical delegation: in August 2012, Brazilian wastewater-treatment students and operators toured Kirie specifically to study the heat-recovery system, an early instance of the plant serving as a showcase for the district's energy-innovation program . - June 4, 1976 / September 3, 2025 — The sealing and, fifty years later, reopening of Ega

text: - ~~Original construction contractors/architects and nominal construction costs for Kirie~~ — substantially resolved for Kirie : 1975-era MSDGC/USEPA documents name Brown and Caldwell (design engineer, contracted July 1967), DeLeuw, Cather and Company (preliminary collection-facility plans, November 1972), and Consoer, Townsend and Associates (Environmental Assessment Statement, November 1974), and give a combined 1974-era cost estimate of $197,010,000 for the O'Hare WRP plus its associated tunnels and reservoir (not broken out by component) — see Original construction . Still open: the general contractor and an isolated, plant-only bid cost for Kirie's actual 1977–1980 construction, and any equivalent contractor/architect/cost information for Egan (1975–76) and Hanover Park (1963), were not located in the sources consulted. MWRD's institutional archives, Metropolitan Sanitary District annual reports from the relevant years (likely held at the Chicago Public Library Municipal Reference Collection or in MWRD's own records center), or period Chicago Tribune coverage would be the logical next sources to check for those two plants. - The claim that Hanover Park was "the nation's first major tertiary wastewater treatment facility" rests on a local-history blog rather than a primary MWRD, EPA, or academic citation; a second, independent local-history source (a Hanover Park community-history blog) corroborates the general claim but in softer language — describing the plant only as "one of the first of its kind in the nation" rather than definitively "the first" — and adds an otherwise-uncorroborated anecdote that Nixon refused to drink a glass of the plant's treated water offered to him during the visit . Neither source is a primary MWRD, EPA, or academic citation, so the superlative claim remains unverified at that level. - A precise identification of the TARP tunnel/reservoir infrastructure serving Egan's Upper Salt Creek service area (analogous to Kirie's well-documented link to the Upper Des Plaines Tunnel System and Majewski Reservoir) was not pinned down; Upper Salt Creek is described by MWRD as within Egan's service territory and subject to ongoing stormwater/TARP watershed planning, but no dedicated "Upper Salt Creek Tunnel System" analogous to the Upper Des Plaines system was confirmed in the TARP fact sheet consulted. - A discrepancy in Kirie's design maximum flow appears in the record: MWRD's own fact sheets and the Illinois EPA NPDES public notice bot

doc: CSOs, Outfalls, Discharges, Flooding & the Modern Waterway
text: The Chicago Area Waterway System (CAWS) — the Chicago River (North, South and Main branches), the North Shore Channel, the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal (CSSC), the Cal-Sag Channel and the Calumet River — is the receiving water for two separate but interlocked discharge systems: treated effluent from the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago's (MWRD) seven water reclamation plants (WRPs), and combined sewer overflows (CSOs) that spill untreated when storms overwhelm the combined sewers of Chicago and 51 suburban satellite communities . Upstream inputs. Roughly 99.5% of the City of Chicago's sewer system is "combined" — a single pipe network carrying sanitary sewage and stormwater together — and it feeds MWRD's intercepting sewers, which in turn feed the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP, "Deep Tunnel") and the seven WRPs . In dry weather essentially all flow reaches a plant for treatment. In wet weather, once TARP's tunnels and reservoirs and the WRPs are full, excess combined flow spills through fixed-elevation weirs at combined sewer outfalls directly into the CAWS, bypassing treatment entirely . Downstream outputs. Each WRP discharges its treated effluent to a specific CAWS reach: Stickney WRP (the world's largest wastewater plant) to the Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal; the Terrence J. O'Brien WRP (formerly "North Side") to the North Shore Channel; Calumet WRP to the Cal-Sag Channel/Calumet River system; and the smaller Kirie, Egan, Hanover Park and Lemont plants to their local receiving streams in the Des Plaines and Salt Creek watersheds . From the CSSC, flow continues southwest through the Des Plaines and Illinois Rivers to the Mississippi — the entire point of the 1900 reversal was to send Chicago's waste away from Lake Michigan, the region's drinking-water source, rather than into it . The lake connection. Because the reversed river system is gravity- and pump-controlled at three lakefront control points — the Wilmette Pumping Station (North Shore Channel), the Chicago River Controlling Works (CRCW, downtown), and the O'Brien Lock and Dam (Calumet River) — extreme storms can force MWRD to open locks and sluice gates and let the swollen waterway drain back into Lake Michigan, "re-reversing" the flow and sending a mix of stormwater and diluted combined sewage into the lake that supplies the region's drinking water . This tension — protect the lake vs. protect the streets and basements — is the central engineering trade-off docum
