doc: CSOs, Outfalls, Discharges, Flooding & the Modern Waterway
text: Not every wet-weather discharge into the CAWS is an MWRD-permitted CSO outfall. Where a satellite community's sewers are fully separated — storm drains independent of the sanitary system, rather than Chicago's combined design — that community typically owns, operates and NPDES-permits (Illinois EPA general MS4 permit) its own local stormwater pumping station, outside the CSO-outfall counts tabulated above. Village of Wilmette Storm Water Pumping Station (SWPS). West Wilmette — about 2.8 sq. mi. west of Ridge Road, built out 1930–1950, before modern stormwater practice — drains through a fully separated storm/sanitary sewer system rather than combined sewers; all of its storm and street runoff collects at a single Village-owned pumping station at 4000 Lake Avenue, on Wilmette's western border, which lifts the water into a discharge channel flowing by gravity to the North Branch of the Chicago River near the Lake Avenue overpass . Built in 1936, the station runs five pumps automatically on sump-level signals from its intake chamber; a 2020 Village hydrologic/hydraulic engineering study rates its combined capacity at 585 cfs, comfortably above the roughly 290–295 cfs 10-year/100-year storm flows the same study modeled for the drainage area — though the separate storm-sewer network feeding the station has only about 2-year capacity, so street flooding (2+ ft. deep over 310 structures, per the study's 10-year-storm model) still occurs in larger events . The station is monitored remotely from the Village's C.P. Dubbs Water Treatment Plant, with Public Works staff dispatched during significant rainfall . In October 2022 Village trustees approved a $1.93 million contract (Connelly Electric) to replace the station's switchgear — last replaced in 1991, roughly a decade past its typical 30-year service life — and the variable-frequency drive on one of its five pumps, installed in 2001 and already past its rated 15–20-year life; the project ran about 28% ($432,000) over its 2022 budget on equipment and labor-cost escalation, with switchgear work completed in late 2023 . This Village-owned facility is unrelated to MWRD's own Wilmette Pumping Station, the lakefront lock/control structure at the head of the North Shore Channel discussed above (see also docs 02 and 07 in this archive) — the two share only a village name, one district-owned and lakefront, the other Village-owned and on the North Branch .

doc: CSOs, Outfalls, Discharges, Flooding & the Modern Waterway
text: The discharge/flooding architecture described here is the second great re-engineering of the Chicago waterway, layered on top of the 1900 Sanitary and Ship Canal reversal (covered in depth elsewhere in this archive). By the 1960s the combined-sewer system built around that reversal was itself failing: Chicago-area sewers were overflowing to the rivers more than 100 days a year, and flooding had become chronic as paved development sent ever more stormwater into pipes sized for a smaller, less-developed city . In 1967 the MWRD, the State of Illinois, Cook County and the City of Chicago formed the Flood Control Coordinating Committee, which evaluated 50 alternative solutions — including simply separating storm and sanitary sewers, judged too costly and disruptive — before settling on TARP as the most cost-effective approach . The MWRD formally adopted TARP on October 20, 1972, as the region's plan for complying with new federal and state water-quality standards . Tunnel construction began in 1975. Because completed segments could be brought online while work continued elsewhere, the Upper Des Plaines Tunnel System near O'Hare went into service first, in 1981 . The tunnels — driven 150 to 300 feet down through dolomite bedrock (up to 350 feet in some segments, per secondary accounts) — were bored, not blasted, using newly developed tunnel-boring machines to limit vibration and surface disruption; cracks in the limestone were sealed with grout curtains to protect groundwater, and the finished bores were concrete-lined . Phase I (the full 110.4-mile tunnel network, 2.3 billion gallons of conveyance/storage) was declared complete in 2006 . The USACE's 1986 Chicagolands Underflow Plan (CUP) defined the federal interest in Phase II, the three large flood-control reservoirs: Majewski (built by USACE, completed 1998), Thornton (District-managed after USACE funding proved inadequate; permanent reservoir completed 2015) and McCook (under a 1999 Project Cooperation Agreement with USACE, mined out in partnership with a commercial limestone quarry operator, Stage 1 completed 2017, Stage 2 consent-decree target 2029, since revised to Dec. 31, 2032) . As of the December 31, 2024 TARP status report, cumulative construction cost stood at $3.856 billion — $2.332 billion for Phase I tunnels and appurtenant facilities, $1.524 billion for the three Phase II reservoirs ($45 million Majewski, $450 million Thornton, $1.029 billion McCook) ; the December 31, 2025 status report puts t

MWRD reports CSO frequency thereafter falls from a pre-TARP average of ~100 days/year to ~50 days/year . - 2007 — MWRD Board adopts the Cook County Stormwater Management Plan (CCSMP), establishing Watershed Planning Councils, Detailed Watershed Plans for six major watersheds, a Stormwater Management Capital Improvement Program, the Small Streams Maintenance Program and the Watershed Management Ordinance . - 2011 (June) — MWRD Board votes to add disinfection at the Calumet and North Side (O'Brien) WRPs — chlorination/dechlorination at Calumet, UV irradiation at North Side — after decades without effluent disinfection . - 2011 (Dec. 14) — DOJ/EPA announce a Clean Water Act consent decree with MWRD over untreated CSO discharges, mandating disinfection, floatables controls and a Long-Term Control Plan (TARP completion) by December 31, 2029, at an estimated cost of more than $3 billion, plus a $25–50 million Green Infrastructure Program and a $675,000 civil penalty ($350,000 federal / $325,000 state) . - 2013 (April 18–19) — Record-setting reversal event: 10,719.5 million gallons (≈10.7 BG) released to Lake Michigan across all three control points, the second-largest event in the 1985–2021 MWRD record . - 2014 (Jan. 6) — After two years of litigation, U.S. District Judge George M. doc: CSOs, Outfalls, Discharges, Flooding & the Modern Waterway
text: Day to day, MWRD balances the CAWS through "downstream control" (metering outflow at the Lockport Powerhouse, the canal's terminus) and "upstream control" (sluice gates at the three lakefront structures) . Ahead of forecast storms, operators draw down canal levels to create in-channel storage capacity, and, during a storm, route flow into TARP — whose tunnels (2.32 BG) and on-line reservoirs (8.65 BG as of December 2024) together hold on the order of 11 billion gallons — before treatment plants and reservoirs are exhausted . (An earlier revision of this document put the pre-storm in-channel storage at "roughly 5 billion gallons"; that figure could not be traced to any source during verification and has been removed pending a direct MWRD operational statement.) Only when waterway levels exceed Lake Michigan's does MWRD open locks/gates to reverse flow back to the lake, a step system operators describe as rare and used only when water threatens to overtop banks into homes and businesses . Combined sewage that cannot be captured discharges through the ≈370 CSO outfalls across the district. Chicago's own 184 outfalls are inspected at least annually, with monthly spot monitoring by the Chicago Department of Water Management and tide-gate alarm monitoring by MWRD at most City outfalls; illegal dry-weather discharges can be reported via 311 or MWRD's 1-800-332-DUMP hotline . Consent-decree "floatables control" is a standing waterborne operation, not just a permit clause: MWRD's Channel Maintenance Unit runs a 36-ft. debris boat with barge and crane, two 23-ft. skimmer boats, a utility work boat and a pontoon boat across the CAWS's 76 navigable miles; under the decree's Floatables Control Plan, two skimmer boats (two-person crews) patrol daily on weekdays from mid-April through mid-October collecting smaller floating debris, while larger items are flagged for the debris boat, and the decree separately requires a containment boom at one specific CSO outfall . Public notification runs through two channels: MWRD's CSO email/text alert system (re-platformed June 17, 2025, requiring re-registration) and the CSO Events/Quick View map on MWRD's GeoHub, which plots confirmed and suspected CSO events by CAWS reach . H2NOW Chicago supplements this with continuous fluorescence-sensor E. coli estimates, refreshed every 15 minutes at monitoring points on the North, South and Main branches, validated monthly against Shedd Aquarium lab cultures . text: - April 13, 1992 — Great Chicago (Loop) Flood. A contractor's piling, driven near the Kinzie Street bridge in September 1991, punctured the roof of a disused turn-of-the-century freight-tunnel network beneath the Chicago River. The breach went undiscovered for months; when it failed, an estimated 250 million gallons of river water flooded Loop basements and the underground Pedway, shutting down the financial district for days. Crews eventually drained the flooded freight tunnels into the still-unfinished Deep Tunnel network below. Remediation and business-interruption costs have been estimated at roughly $2 billion in 1992 dollars . - 1985–2025 reversal record (MWRD's own tally, updated). A January 2026 update to MWRD's official "Reversals to Lake Michigan" table extends the ledger three years past the archive's original 1985–2021 source and shows zero reversal events in 2022, 2024 and 2025 — only the July 2023 storm broke that streak. Recomputed across the full 1985–2025 series, the CAWS reversed to the lake at least once in 23 of 41 years, across 36 discrete dated events summing to ≈52.5 billion gallons; the largest single event remains September 13–16, 2008 (11,049.1 million gallons combined) and the second-largest April 18–19, 2013 (10,719.5 million gallons) . Other cited totals in secondary press coverage still don't reconcile — MWRD's then-executive director told reporters in 2014 that reversal frequency had "increased in the past five years" versus a historical average of about once a year , and separate news accounts describe "about 50" reversal events since 1985 versus a "27 reversals... 44 billion gallons" figure attributed to MWRD elsewhere — neither matches the direct count/sum of the source-12/72 tables even with the 2022–2025 update, so the discrepancies likely reflect different counting rules (gate reversals vs. lock reversals, per-event vs. per-date tallies) rather than missing data; this file reports the MWRD-published table as the primary figure. - July 2–3, 2023. A storm dropping 3–8+ inches regionally left an estimated 70,000-plus homes flooded across Chicago's West Side, South Side and suburbs; $426 million in federal disaster aid was later awarded to Chicago and $244 million to Cook County, though a 2026 investigative report found essentially none of it had yet been spent . doc: CSOs, Outfalls, Discharges, Flooding & the Modern Waterway
text: - Does the total reversal count/volume reconcile? (Substantially resolved — 2022–2025 data now located.) A January 2026 MWRD update (source 72) extends the reversal ledger through 2025 and closes the archive's earlier data gap: 2022, 2024 and 2025 each had zero reversal events, and the sole event in that window — July 2–3, 2023 — totaled a precisely quantified 1,142.6 million gallons (911.8 MG CRCW + 230.8 MG Wilmette; no O'Brien Lock volume). Recomputed across the full 1985–2025 series this yields 23 years with at least one event (not 27) and a cumulative volume of roughly 52.5 billion gallons — still reconciling with neither the press citation of "~50 reversals since 1985" nor the separately reported "27 reversals / 44 billion gallons" figure attributed to MWRD , which confirms the discrepancy is a counting-method difference (calendar dates vs. discrete events vs. lock-only vs. gate-plus-lock) rather than missing 2022–2026 data. No reversal volume for the July 2026 storm was located — the source-72 table's cutoff is January 2026, before that event . - What is the true current fish-species count? Sources range from "more than 60" (Wikipedia, citing Chicago Tribune/WTTW) to "more than 70" (MWRD, Friends of the Chicago River) to "77" (one Shedd/CBS-linked figure), with a distinct 2025 Shedd Aquarium finding of 24 species successfully spawning to the larval stage. These appear to be different metrics (species observed vs. species reproducing) rather than a contradiction, but the underlying methodology/dataset for each number was not confirmed . - Bubbly Creek restoration status (updated). USACE's 2020 feasibility study recommended a roughly 40–44-acre ecological restoration (30.7 ac. substrate capping with rounded river rock/quarried stone/sand, plus riparian, emergent and submergent plantings), but the project stalled over liability concerns tied to sediment contamination, and a $11.6 million congressional authorization has sat unspent pending resolution . As of a February 2025 update, EPA's Great Lakes National Program Office, USACE and the City of Chicago began a joint pre-design investigation — sediment characterization across roughly 22 acres plus a sediment-capping pilot — intended to help unblock the liability impasse and inform USACE's restoration design; EPA states final pre-design investigation reports are expected in fall 2027, meaning the restoration itself had not begun construction as of this research pass (July 2026). doc: The Calumet-Region Lakes: How Lake Calumet, Wolf Lake, and Hyde Lake Were Re-Engineered
text: Every other document in this archive follows engineered infrastructure — canals, cribs, plants, tunnels — built around Chicago's natural waterways. This document covers something different: three natural lakes at the far southeast corner of the system that were themselves reshaped into infrastructure. Lake Calumet, Wolf Lake, and the vanished Hyde Lake sat at the historic headwaters of the Little Calumet/Calumet River system that doc 02 describes being reversed by the Cal-Sag Channel in 1922. That reversal did not simply redirect water — it also supplied the legal and economic rationale for a 60-year campaign of dredging and filling that cut Lake Calumet's open-water area roughly in half, eliminated Hyde Lake entirely, and left Wolf Lake divided into diked pools by causeways built for reasons that had nothing to do with the lake itself . Geologically, all three lakes occupy the same shallow, poorly drained plain: a former arm of glacial Lake Michigan left behind sandy beach ridges (the Tolleston and Calumet ridges) enclosing broad marshy flats after the lake receded roughly 4,000–2,000 years ago . Surveyors in 1834 found Lake Calumet at the center of an "impassable" wetland roughly three miles long and a mile and a half wide, six to ten feet deep, ringed by cattail marsh that extended east to the Calumet River and west to the Blue Island moraine [2 p.14-17]. Wolf Lake sat in a similar depression hemmed in by beach ridges straddling what became the Illinois-Indiana state line, its basin floored with twenty to thirty inches of peat over sandy soil [2 p.15]. Hyde Lake did not even exist on the earliest surveys — it emerged sometime between 1872 and 1881 as the water stage of Wolf Lake and neighboring Berry Lake dropped, exposing sand ridges that separated a shallow remnant pool from the rest of Wolf Lake and gave it a name of its own [1 p.14-15]. That pool then had one of the shortest lifespans of any named lake in the Chicago region: formed in the 1870s, it was "almost completely filled by 1902" and gone for good by 1927 [1 p.15,24-25]. This document tells the physical-transformation story that the rest of the archive assumes but does not narrate: the dredging and diking that created Calumet Harbor and the Port of Chicago; the slag, dredge spoil, and municipal refuse that did the filling; the causeways that split Wolf Lake into managed pools; and the disappearance of Hyde Lake as documented on USGS topographic maps. doc: The Calumet-Region Lakes: How Lake Calumet, Wolf Lake, and Hyde Lake Were Re-Engineered
section: Lake Calumet: from wild-rice-and-cattail marsh to harbor
text: Congress authorized the first federal money for a harbor at the mouth of the Calumet River in 1869 — $50,000 to cut through the sandbar and build two piers into Lake Michigan [3 p.204][2 p.19]. For the next two decades, improvement of the river upstream toward Lake Calumet was blocked less by engineering difficulty than by property law: the Corps of Engineers refused to dredge until riparian landowners surrendered their rights, and a handful of speculators held out for higher prices, delaying real work until 1888 [2 p.19]. Once underway, dredging moved quickly: a 16-foot-deep, 20-foot-wide channel reached 108th Street by 1891, and by 1896 a shallower channel reached Lake Calumet itself [2 p.19-20]. By 1912 a 21-foot federal channel extended all the way to the lake [3 p.207]. Industry did not wait for the Corps. Landowners along the river dredged their own frontage and used the spoil to raise building sites — the Calumet and Chicago Canal and Dock Company reported spending $4,800 on filling/grading and $12,000 on dredging in 1879 alone [2 p.19-20]. When George Pullman built his factory town starting in spring 1880, his engineers filled a two-by-half-mile section of the western shore of Lake Calumet, raising it five feet above the surrounding marsh — while simultaneously mining the exposed lake bed for brick clay at a rate described in Corps correspondence as "100,000 [bricks] per day," with a company agent suggesting the excavation could "go far towards excavating the lake" for dock frontage [2 p.20-21]. At the river mouth, North Chicago Rolling Mill began "systematically" dumping slag and mill refuse into the lake and shoreline starting in 1878-79; by 1882 the practice had created over 30 acres of new land, and it eventually built out roughly 300 acres there, with Iroquois Iron and American Smelting & Refining creating another 400 acres just south of the river mouth using the same method [2 p.21-22]. This pattern — slag and dredge spoil dumped directly into the water to manufacture buildable land — became, in Colten's words, "the norm throughout the Calumet region" and is why the 1911 Sanborn insurance map for the Sherwin-Williams plant on Lake Calumet's shore carries the frank label "Land in Making" [2 p.22]. By the early 1900s, momentum built for a formal harbor plan rather than piecemeal industrial filling. section: Hyde Lake: formed, mapped, and erased inside 55 years
text: Hyde Lake's entire documented existence — appearance, industrial use, and disappearance — fits within about five decades, and it happened almost entirely within the fill zone this document has already described. It was not an ancient feature: USGS geologists, working from an 1882 congressional survey document, place its formation "sometime between 1872 and 1881," when falling water levels in Wolf Lake exposed a sand ridge that separated off a shallow western remnant pool and gave it a name [1 p.14-15]. A U.S. Army Corps of Engineers map of "Calumet River Improvements, 1894," reproduced in Colten's history, labels "HYDE LAKE" clearly at this location, immediately southwest of Wolf Lake and northeast of the Calumet River's turning basins — and the earliest USGS topographic coverage of the area, the 1892 Calumet 15-minute quadrangle, shows the same small lake between Calumet Lake and Wolf Lake, already squeezed by the railroad and industrial grid pressing in from South Chicago [2 fig. 2-3]. That map is the clearest surviving cartographic record of Hyde Lake as a distinct, named water body. Its filling followed the same playbook as the rest of the region: steel-industry slag and river dredge spoil, deposited to "create land for steel manufacturing, petroleum refining, and storage" — the USGS's fill-characterization study groups Hyde Lake with two nearby Indiana-side lakes (Deer Lake and Berry Lake) that were filled by the identical process in the same window [1 p.24]. The USGS's map-comparison methodology is unusually precise about the timing: "Hyde Lake was almost completely filled by 1902," the year the agency treats as the boundary of its earliest reliable topographic coverage, and by 1927 Hyde Lake (along with Deer and Berry Lakes) "had been filled" outright [1 p.15, 24]. A small residual wet spot apparently lingered for decades — the same study notes that "small amounts of fill were dumped... at the northern tip of what was once Hyde Lake" as late as the 1978-1993 period, suggesting a marshy remnant persisted long after the main lake was gone [1 p.28]. This document previously could not identify a single named company responsible for the final filling; a 2021 IDNR-published guide to neighboring Wolf Lake now supplies the most direct attribution located in any source consulted, stating in its own history section that "Wolf Lake's sister Hyde Lake to the west was filled in to create land for what would become the nearby Republic Steel plant" . section: Wolf Lake: shared, diked, and slagged
text: Wolf Lake's transformation ran on a different track from Lake Calumet's, because it was never dredged into a shipping harbor — it was instead progressively diked, filled at the margins, and pressed into service for projects that had nothing to do with the lake itself. Colten's history notes that peat deposits up to thirty inches deep "are most common in the Wolf Lake basin," reflecting its origin as a shallow, marsh-fringed depression among beach ridges — described by naturalist Donald Culross Peattie as "a famous hunting ground for aquatic wildlife... where the plants form one of the most remarkable assemblages of aquatics in the country" [2 p.15]. Industrial filling reached Wolf Lake later and less completely than it reached Lake Calumet: the USGS's fill-deposit mapping finds "the northern and southeastern parts of Wolf Lake" had been filled by the early 1990s, and identifies slag as "the principal fill material" around the lake, with especially heavy ash-and-cinder deposits — flue dust from steel manufacturing — on its west side [1 p.24, 27]. On the Indiana side of the state line, that filling had explicit legislative sanction: a 1906 Indiana General Assembly joint resolution allowed industries to create land by infilling Lake Michigan and connected lakes with the Secretary and Chief of Engineers' permit approval, and 1907 state law I.C. 4-18-13 went further, authorizing riparian owners outright "to fill submerged land adjacent to and within the width of their shoreline property" [1 p.15] — a lakeshore-filling counterpart to the riparian-rights fight that had already stalled Calumet River dredging on the Illinois side (see above). The single largest filling episode came during the Cold War: several hundred acres of wetland on the lake's northwest side were filled with ferrous slag to build a Nike Ajax anti-aircraft missile site; the missiles themselves were removed in the 1970s, and that land is now the Cook County Forest Preserve District's Eggers Woods . The lake's present-day division into separate diked pools dates specifically to the late 1950s and had nothing to do with water management — it was fill logistics for a highway project. section: Cal-Sag Channel: deepening the construction story
text: Doc 02 already covers the Cal-Sag Channel's dates and dimensions; the additional construction detail relevant here concerns how it was built and where the spoil went — because much of that spoil reshaped the lakes described above. The Sanitary District divided the 16.2-mile channel into fourteen sections, each roughly 1.125 miles long, let out to separate contractors [3 p.206]. Construction proceeded from the main channel above Lemont eastward toward Blue Island and the Little Calumet River; the ceremonial start of excavation came on July 4, 1913, when the contracting firm of Green & Sons broke ground with a steam shovel near Worth . Photographs from the build (reproduced by local historians from Sanitary District/MWRD archives) show steam shovels repeatedly mired in the region's soft clay, dredges being assembled on-site as early as December 1911, and — in the channel's rock-cut sections through the Sag Valley — a traveling bridge crane that dredged material and sorted it to either side of the cut, stone to one side and earth to the other, so it could be re-used or trucked away . Contractor A. Guthrie & Co. cast the channel's original concrete retaining walls, later removed during the 1955-1971 widening . Neither the Colten history nor the Corps' own district history gives a comprehensive workforce headcount for the 1911-1922 build; that remains an open question below. Disposal of the material excavated from both the original 1911-1922 Cal-Sag dig and the subsequent decades of Calumet River/Harbor maintenance dredging followed an evolving, and increasingly contested, path. Colten's history states that prior to the 1920s, river-dredging spoil was "typically dumped indiscriminately along the banks of the rivers from which the spoil was removed" — exactly the practice already described above as building "made land" along the Calumet River shore [1 p.28][2 p.19-22]. Beginning in the 1920s new regulations required open-lake disposal roughly 12 miles offshore instead, a practice that continued (with berms occasionally built to contain material left on riverbanks) until the late 1960s [1 p.28]. The Corps' own history confirms the scale of this open-lake dumping: from 1924 onward, Chicago, Calumet, and Indiana Harbors shared "one common underwater dumpground" nine miles east of Calumet Harbor's breakwater entrance, a rectangular tract roughly 5 by 17 miles and 69 feet deep on average [3 p.271]. Rising environmental concern in the mid-1960s ended open-lake dumping

text: Lake Calumet today functions as a working harbor rather than a natural lake. The Illinois International Port District — successor since 1985 to the 1951 Chicago Regional Port District — controls roughly 1,600 acres of land around Senator Dan Dougherty Harbor (the Lake Calumet terminal) plus the separate Iroquois Landing Terminal at the Calumet River's mouth on Lake Michigan; combined, the Port of Chicago handled about 894,832 tons of cargo annually as of the most recent figures located, moving grain (two elevators totaling 14 million bushels of capacity), bulk liquids (800,000 barrels of storage), petcoke, and cement through roughly 3,000 linear feet of ship/barge berthing . The lake remains connected to Lake Michigan by the dredged Calumet River channel, deep enough that 600-foot lake freighters still back up the river into the harbor . Immediately south and east of the open water, the industrial land created by a century of slag and dredge-spoil filling now carries a very different designation: the 87-acre Lake Calumet Cluster Superfund site (US Drum, Alburn Incinerator, an unnamed parcel, and part of the old Paxton Lagoons) is in active EPA remediation, with a partial landfill cap already built and groundwater investigation ongoing through the mid-2020s . The CID Landfill site (EPA ID ILD010284248) is located near 138th Street and the Calumet Expressway/I-94 in Calumet City — not at 122nd Street, which is actually the southern boundary of the separately-described Lake Calumet Cluster site above . EPA's own site profile lists CID Landfill's status as "Not on the NPL" with an NFRAP ("No Further Remedial Action Planned") designation, meaning it is not an actively managed federal cleanup. Moreover, EPA's RCRA facility database lists that same EPA ID under the name "CID Recycling and Disposal Facility" — the still-operating Waste Management facility that also appears at 13707 South Jeffery Avenue on the MWRD's July 2025 significant-industrial-users list — indicating the "CID Landfill" and the active recycling/disposal facility are almost certainly the same EPA-regulated facility rather than the separate legal entities this document previously concluded . The Army Corps' Confined Disposal Facility at 95th Street continues to receive Calumet River and Harbor maintenance-dredging material under an Illinois EPA water-pollution-control permit, most recently renewed in 2016 with a temporary extension through 2021 while a vertical-expansion permit is reviewed . text: - 1912-1916 — Discovery and disappearance of Thismia americana . Botanist Norma Etta Pfeiffer discovered this chlorophyll-lacking, mycoheterotrophic flowering plant in 1912 in a wet-mesic sand prairie at 119th Street and Torrence Avenue, in the wetlands then still surrounding Lake Calumet — the only location it has ever been recorded anywhere in the world. It was last seen in 1916 and is presumed extinct, its habitat destroyed by the same filling and industrial development described throughout this document . - 1924 — Leopold and Loeb hide Bobby Franks' body in a Wolf Lake culvert. A culvert leading into Wolf Lake was used by Nathan Leopold and Richard Loeb to conceal the body of their murder victim, Bobby Franks, in the crime that became known as the "Trial of the Century" — one of the lake's better-documented brushes with Chicago criminal history during the same interwar decades it was being progressively diked and filled . - 1933-1936 — A racetrack built inside Wolf Lake. Promoted as "The Most Unique Racetrack in the World," a dirt oval with no guardrails or fencing was constructed in the middle of the lake, with water on both sides of the track; it hosted four years of races involving both cars on the track and speedboats in the surrounding water before being discontinued . - 1945 — Landfill dike failure. Heavy rain washed out a section of the 1940 municipal landfill's ash-and-slag dike across Lake Calumet at 110th Street; the Sanitary District/city rebuilt it with slag [2 p.59]. - 1958 — St. Lawrence Seaway bet that didn't pay off. Lake Calumet was deepened and large new slips built specifically to accommodate ocean-going freighters expected after the Seaway's opening; that traffic "never truly materialized," leaving Lake Calumet's harbor infrastructure oversized relative to the shipping it actually attracted for decades . - 1965 — Industrial water use documented at its peak. A U.S. Department of Health, Education and Welfare pollution survey that year found eight Calumet-area industrial dischargers — Commonwealth Edison's since-vanished Calumet Station chief among them, at 220 million gallons of cooling water drawn from the Calumet River daily — contributing roughly 376 million gallons of liquid waste a day to the region's waterways, with the area's steel mills alone adding more than 900 lb of phenols and 800 lb of ammonia nitrogen daily on top of that cooling-water volume [2 p.63-66]. - 1965-1966 — The Corps' pivot away from open-lake dumping. text: - Lake Calumet's original acreage cannot be reconciled with its current acreage using the sources consulted. Colten's "3 mi × 1.5 mi" description, the frequently cited 442-acre current figure, and the "about half filled" USGS estimate do not add up to consistent numbers under any straightforward reading. Two more dated figures, added in this pass, widen rather than close the gap: a 1988 field study measured the lake's surface area at 782 acres , while a 2000 IDNR/ISWS regional assessment measured it at 1,410 acres — the later figure is larger than the earlier one, which is physically implausible for a lake that was otherwise still being filled during those same twelve years, and points toward the different studies drawing their lake boundary at different places (open water only vs. open water plus adjoining wetland) rather than toward any single figure being simply wrong. A primary Chicago Regional Port District or Illinois International Port District survey document, or the underlying GIS boundary files behind the 1988/2000/2022 figures (none located in this pass), would likely resolve this. - No source consulted previously named a specific company, contractor, or precise final-fill date for Hyde Lake; this pass located a direct (if uncorroborated) attribution. The USGS fill-deposit study groups Hyde Lake with Deer Lake and Berry Lake and dates its filling to the 1902-1927 window generally, attributing the fill type to "steel manufacturing, petroleum refining, and storage" development without naming individual operators [1 p.24]. EPA's community history of the adjacent South Deering neighborhood offers one circumstantial, geographic/corporate link: Joseph H. Brown Iron & Steel Co. began operating on the Calumet River in 1875, was bought by International Harvester in 1902 (becoming the plant popularly known as Wisconsin Steel), and was joined soon after by Gold Medal Flour Co., Illinois Slag and Ballast Co., and the Federal Furnace Co. — an industrial cluster whose 1875-1902 growth window brackets Hyde Lake's own 1902-1927 disappearance . More directly, a 2021 IDNR-published guide to Wolf Lake states outright that "Hyde Lake to the west was filled in to create land for what would become the nearby Republic Steel plant" ; Republic Steel's own documented Southeast Side corporate history (predecessor land acquisition at 118th and the Calumet River in 1901, reorganization as Interstate Iron and Steel in 1916, renamed Republic Steel in 1930) overlaps Hyde Lake'

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
text: Every other document in this archive narrates something essentially finished — a canal dug in 1900, a tunnel bored between 1975 and 2006, a reservoir completed in 2015. This document narrates the opposite: the system as a live construction site, captured in a single snapshot dated July 2026 . Every dollar figure, percent-complete, and schedule date below is true as of that month and will drift — some quickly. A contract "under construction" in July 2026 may be finished by the time this is read; a project budgeted at one figure may be awarded at a materially different one (the O'Brien WRP Battery E project below moved from a $325.0 million engineer's estimate to a $409.4 million actual low bid within the same budget cycle — a live illustration of why this document is dated rather than definitive) . The value of pinning this snapshot into the archive is threefold. First, most of the system documented elsewhere here — the interceptors of doc 07, the TARP tunnels of doc 09, the reservoirs of doc 10, the treatment plants of docs 11, 12, and 13 — is not actually finished; it is mid-rehabilitation, mid-expansion, or (in McCook Reservoir's case) still being excavated four decades after the federal design memorandum that authorized it. A "completed" 1930s pumping station or a "completed" 2015 reservoir is, this document shows, still generating tens of millions of dollars in active contract work every year. Second, a capital-projects snapshot is the cleanest way to see money and governance move across agency lines in real time: a Chicago Transit Authority transit-line extension is currently reshaping a Calumet WRP solids-handling contract; a U.S. Army Corps of Engineers pilot funding program is underwriting part of McCook Reservoir's federal share; a regional water commission's pipeline groundbreaking runs through the same Southwest Pumping Station this archive's water-distribution document already covers. Third, this document is a control on the rest of the archive: several facts recovered here — the exact contract number and value for the North Branch Pumping Station's 2023–2027 rehabilitation (open in doc 08 as of its last revision), for instance — resolve open questions elsewhere by drawing on MWRD's and the City's own 2026 capital-budget documents rather than secondary reporting. doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: McCook Reservoir Stage 2 — the last piece of TARP
text: McCook Reservoir Stage 2 is the single largest piece of unfinished work anywhere in the system this archive documents, and it is unusual among the projects here in being gated not by money or design but by a commercial rock market. As doc 10 covers in detail, MWRD's 2003 mining agreement with Vulcan Materials Co. lets Vulcan crush and sell the reservoir's excavated stone, which is what makes MWRD's own out-of-pocket excavation cost (contract 73-161-EH, "Vulcan Rock Mining Hard Costs Less Royalty," $81,585,000 and still climbing as mining continues) far below what a conventional dig would cost — but it also means the pace of excavation tracks the regional aggregate market rather than a construction schedule MWRD fully controls . MWRD's June 2025 TARP Status Report puts Stage 2 at roughly 72 percent excavated, and the District's own 2025 public update pushed the consent-decree completion date from December 31, 2029 to an estimated December 31, 2032, explicitly citing slowed stone demand rather than any funding or design shortfall . The federal side of Stage 2 has its own, separately noteworthy history. On January 31, 2019, USACE's Chicago District signed what both the Corps and MWRD described as a first-of-its-kind agreement: rather than USACE designing and constructing the remaining reservoir features itself (the arrangement used for Stage 1 and for Majewski Reservoir), the Corps transferred $33.8 million in federal funds directly to MWRD as Stage 2's federal cost-share, under a pilot program the Corps had never previously used, and MWRD assumed full design and construction responsibility for the remainder of the work . MWRD's 2026 capital budget shows that federal-share arrangement continuing project-by-project rather than as a single lump sum: the 2025-completed McCook Reservoir Rock Wall Stabilization and Geotechnical Instrumentation contract (17-131-4FR, $21,862,000) carried a 53 percent federal share; the still-future McCook Reservoir Stage 2 Final Reservoir Preparation contract (17-132-4F, $24,796,000) is budgeted at a 69 percent federal share; and ongoing geotechnical professional services (19-151-4C, $1,491,000) carry a 75 percent federal share — the same historical rate as the original Stages 1–2 Army Corps construction-contract package (73-161-2H, $615,937,000) . Total Phase II reservoir project cost across all three reservoirs stood at $1,525,491,000 in the 2026 budget book, essentially matching the $1.524 billion figure in MWRD's own June 2025 T

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: O'Brien WRP Battery E — MWRD's largest contract ever, and a live case study in cost growth
text: The O'Brien WRP's Battery E Activated Sludge Facility is covered in construction and regulatory detail in doc 12; this document adds the primary-source contract detail behind the headline numbers. MWRD's own bid documents (Contract 21-092-3P, "Battery E Activated Sludge Facility," Terrence J. O'Brien Water Reclamation Plant, Skokie) set a bid-opening date of Tuesday, October 14, 2025, and describe the physical scope precisely: an 8-by-8-foot cast-in-place concrete influent conduit and utility tunnel, a three-pass fermenter tank with fourteen mechanical mixers, a one-pass anoxic pass with six mixers, an influent channel with four mixers and six motorized slide gates, six two-pass aeration tanks (each with two mixers, an FRP baffle wall, an internal mixed-liquor recirculation pump, and a fine-bubble membrane disc diffuser system), and a 63-inch-diameter HDPE return-activated-sludge conduit — a new aeration battery built essentially from scratch alongside the 98-year-old plant it will partly relieve . The bid document specifies 1,460 calendar days to substantial completion after bond approval — just under four years, consistent with the budget book's separately stated January 2030 completion target . The cost story is where this project becomes a useful test case for how to read every other dollar figure in this document. MWRD's 2026 Final Budget Book — finalized before the contract was awarded — carried Battery E at an estimated construction cost of $325,005,000, with the contractor listed as "to be determined" and the award date projected for January 2026 . Trade press covering the pre-bid project described an even wider engineering-estimate range of $308–373 million . When MWRD's Board of Commissioners actually authorized the contract — at its June 18, 2026 meeting, under agenda item 26-0432, after the item had first been pulled from the June 4 agenda — the award went to Michels Construction at $409,420,079, roughly 26 percent above the budget book's own estimate and above even the high end of the pre-bid range . Independent local reporting describes this as the largest single contract in MWRD's history . (One naming quirk worth flagging: MWRD's own board observer coverage and agenda item title the June 2026 award a "sludge removal contract," which on its face reads like a narrower scope than "Battery E Activated Sludge Facility" — this document treats the two as the same underlying Contract 21-092-3P per the dollar figure and date match, but the discrepan

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
text: Deep construction and hydraulic history for this station is doc 08's subject; this entry exists only to log the current contract precisely, since doc 08 as last revised could not confirm the exact contract number, award date, or dollar value of its "2023–2026" rehabilitation phase. MWRD's 2026 capital budget resolves that gap directly: Contract 22-094-3D, "Rehabilitation of Elevated Deck and Boat Dock at North Branch Pumping Station, NSA," was awarded to Path Construction Company, Inc. in March 2024 for an estimated construction cost of $22,587,970 , with substantial completion targeted for August 2027 . The scope — replacing deteriorated exterior concrete decking, stairs, and the boat dock, plus flap-gate replacement at the adjacent Dropshaft DS-91 connecting structure — matches the contractor's own February 2026 project profile describing roughly 80 percent of exterior wall repairs complete and the old boat dock fully demolished by that date . This is a separate, later contract from the 2018 Contract 16-079-3D ($4,613,450) that repaired the station's storm-pump-eroded concrete columns, which doc 08 already documents in full . Contract 22-094-3D's own budget history is a second, smaller-scale illustration of the same cost-and-schedule drift documented above for Battery E. MWRD's 2023 Final Budget Book first priced the deck-and-boat-dock rehabilitation at an estimated $7,000,000, with a planned award of April 2023 and substantial completion by March 2024 ; the 2024 Final Budget Book more than doubled that estimate to $15,000,000, pushed the planned award to January 2024, and extended the completion target to August 2026 ; the contract's eventual actual terms — $22,587,970 awarded to Path Construction in March 2024, completion now August 2027 — came in more than three times the first budgeted estimate and roughly a year and a half later than that estimate's completion date. The work also has an earlier, distinct predecessor: MWRD's 2021 Final Budget Book shows a "Rehabilitation of North Branch Pumping Station & Other Miscellaneous Work" contract (Project 20-858-3D, estimated $5,700,000, planned award July 2021) covering combined miscellaneous repairs at both North Branch Pumping Station and the separate Webster Avenue Aeration Station — a smaller, earlier-generation project distinct from both the 2018 column repair and the 2022-numbered deck/dock contract this document otherwise tracks .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
text: The July 2026 storm cycle that filled Thornton Reservoir to a record 94 percent of capacity — covered in construction and hydrological detail in doc 10 — produced a parallel, still-unfolding capital-projects story: a slowly draining reservoir of raw combined sewage generating hydrogen-sulfide odor complaints severe enough that MWRD's existing aerator-and-sprayer systems were acknowledged by the District itself to only partially work at such fill levels . MWRD's response, announced in mid-July 2026, was two new projects — a carbon-filtration system and a misting system — together budgeted at roughly $5 million and due for completion by the end of December 2026, bringing the reservoir's decade-long cumulative odor-mitigation spending to about $8.6 million . These sit alongside the reservoir's existing, already-built odor infrastructure: a 2017 surface-aeration system and a 2020 program combining new odor-control systems with decommissioning of the temporary Thornton Transitional Reservoir, plus a $3,064,500 odor-control-system contract (17-273-4P) awarded October 2024, under construction with a substantial-completion target of May 2026 per MWRD's 2026 budget book .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: Stickney WRP — digesters, gas piping, and boilers
text: Stickney's 2009 Master Plan (12 projects, $993.6 million, covered in doc 11) is now well into its execution phase, and MWRD's 2026 capital budget shows two large, currently active Stickney contracts squarely within that plan's solids-and-energy scope. Digester Rehabilitation and Gas Piping Replacement, Phase II (Contract 18-148-3P, $53,151,662, contractor IHC Construction Companies, LLC, awarded November 2023, substantial completion March 2030) covers digesters 1 through 12 and also rehabilitates the digester gas waste-gas burners; MWRD's project justification explains the underlying mechanism as much broader than pipe replacement alone — anaerobic digesters accumulate dense inorganic solids over years of operation, reducing usable digester volume and requiring periodic draining and cleaning, and recent work by the Maintenance & Operations Department "uncovered extensive fouling of the existing digester gas piping" during that draining/cleaning cycle . The replacement piping is sized to carry the additional gas volume generated since Stickney's original Imhoff tanks were converted to primary settling tanks; the project is explicitly sequenced to allow a later, separate contract to install a final gas-mixing system inside the digesters without draining them . Boilers 3, 4, and 5 and MCC Replacement (Contract 19-155-3MR, $21,979,424, contractor Path Construction Co., awarded May 2023, substantial completion June 2027) replaces boilers that MWRD's own project justification describes as "at the end of their useful lives" with new units capable of co-firing digester gas and natural gas — maximizing use of the plant's own biogas rather than flaring or venting the surplus, plus a new deaerator for full system redundancy . MWRD's budget narrative separately notes complementary work already underway: mechanical mixers installed in Aeration Batteries A, C, and D (with Battery B in progress) to support Stickney's now-successful biological phosphorus removal, and a pilot combined-heat-and-power (CHP) biogas project begun in 2024 at Egan WRP whose findings MWRD is evaluating for wider application, including potentially at Stickney .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: Calumet WRP — an unlikely driver: the CTA Red Line extension
text: The Calumet Master Plan (2009, $507.16 million, ten projects, covered in doc 12) has one active-in-2026 project with an origin story worth flagging on its own terms. The Rebuild of Dewatering Facility, CWRP (Contract 24-268-3P, $65,000,000, awarded January 2026, substantial completion August 2028) restores an existing centrifuge building to mechanically dewater digested sludge — but MWRD's own project justification states plainly that the driver is the Chicago Transit Authority's Red Line extension, which will make the plant's existing practice of trucking digested sludge from lagoons to drying beds "no longer practical" once the transit project is built. A companion contract, Install Pipe Casing and Utilities Under Railroad Tracks to Dewatering Facility, CWRP (Contract 24-269-3P, $13,200,000, awarded May 2026, substantial completion December 2027) physically threads new utility infrastructure under the same rail corridor . Separately, TARP Mechanical Equipment Improvements, CWRP (Contract 26-822-31, $25,500,000, awarded January 2026, substantial completion December 2028) rehabilitates the original-to-1985 suction and discharge valves on the Calumet Tunnel and Reservoir Plan's main sewage pumps, whose leaking seals currently require dewatering the entire pump house to service individual units, plus unreliable medium-voltage-drive auxiliary chillers . Because the $65,000,000 Rebuild of Dewatering Facility contract runs to August 2028, MWRD's 2026 operating budget separately funds roughly $2.737 million in stopgap measures to keep biosolids moving in the meantime: tanks, pumps, and polymer for GeoPools membrane-dewatering units ($907,000), additional truck hauling to alternative disposal sites ($860,000), and the purchase of one wheel loader ($620,000) and one tractor ($350,000) — all justified in the budget book as necessary because "the Red Line CTA expansion disrupts current access to the East Drying Sites" ahead of the permanent facility's completion .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: Collection Asset Management Plan (CAMP) — interceptor rehabilitation, systemwide
text: Doc 07 already covers CAMP's 2013 origin as the successor to the 1991 Interceptor Inspection and Rehabilitation Program (IIRP); MWRD's 2026 capital budget shows the program is not a single line item but a continuously rolling set of individually contracted interceptor-segment rehabilitations, several running simultaneously. Contracts under construction or newly awarded as of 2026 include: North Shore 1 Rehabilitation, NSA (10-047-3S, $45,424,000, awarded January 2024); 39th Street Conduit Rehabilitation, Phase II, SSA (01-103-AS, $41,109,000, awarded October 2024) — a conduit MWRD's own project justification dates to roughly 110 years old, whose scope includes rehabilitating a 16-foot-by-12-foot double-barrel reinforced-concrete connecting sewer running into the Racine Avenue Pumping Station ; Salt Creek Intercepting Sewer No. 3 Rehabilitation, SSA (20-161-3S, $20,079,000, awarded November 2024); Upper Des Plaines Intercepting Sewer 14B Rehabilitation, NSA (06-360-3SR, $36,328,000, awarded June 2023); and Upper Des Plaines Intercepting Sewer 11D Rehabilitation, NSA (12-369-3S, $17,000,000, budgeted for February 2026 award) — plus the three-phase Kirie–Egan Solids Pipeline Rehabilitation (Sections 1–3, contracts 23-416-2S/23-417-3S/23-418-3S, roughly $50 million combined across the three sections, already flagged in doc 13's upgrade timeline for Sections 1–2), whose project justification cites four documented pipe breaks along the existing 18-inch solids pipeline between survey stations 0+00 and 27+05 as the specific trigger, to be repaired with cured-in-place pipe lining and carbon-fiber-reinforced-polymer manhole lining . The 11D work is itself sequential rather than a single contract: an earlier, distinct segment — Upper Des Plaines Intercepting Sewer 11D, Extension C Rehabilitation, NSA (Project 11-404-3S; pre-bid estimate $11,500,000, but awarded September 2023 to Benchmark Construction Co. at an actual $8,348,000, substantial completion October 2024) — was already contracted and largely finished by the time the $17,000,000 12-369-3S segment above entered the 2026 award queue, illustrating how granular CAMP's segment-by-segment approach runs in practice . Further interceptor rehabilitation contracts are already queued in MWRD's "Projects Under Development" list for 2027–2030 award, including Calumet Intercepting Sewer Nos. 2, 3, 4, 10, and 13; West Side Intercepting Sewer Nos. 2 and 3-D; Southwest Side Intercepting Sewer Nos. 15, 16, and 17B; Harms Roa

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: Kirie, Egan, and the suburban WRPs
text: Doc 13 already covers Kirie's February 5, 2026 board-approved $27 million contract with the Sollitt/Broadway Joint Venture for electrical-distribution and phosphorus-removal improvements; MWRD's 2026 budget book itemizes the phosphorus-removal component of that same scope as Contract 23-379-3E at an estimated $23,483,000 . Budget-book history partly explains the gap between that $23.483 million line item and the $27 million publicly reported award: MWRD's 2024 and 2025 budget books both carried Contract 23-379-3E as a "Project Under Development" under the narrower title Switchgear and Motor Control Center Replacement, KWRP , priced at just $12.0 million ; by the 2026 budget book the same contract number had been retitled Electrical Power Distribution and Phosphorus Removal Improvements, KWRP and repriced to $23,483,000 — nearly double the original estimate — reflecting a broadened scope, not merely a contingency allowance, though the further step to the $27 million board-reported figure is still not independently reconciled here (see Open questions) . The 2026 budget's phosphorus-compliance table shows this Kirie work sitting inside a coordinated, systemwide phosphorus-removal capital push spanning five WRPs and running through 2030: Kirie's chemical-addition backup system (19-375-3P, $5.4M, awarded 2025) and electrical/phosphorus contract (2026); O'Brien's Battery E (2026) and prior Battery D modifications; and a not-yet-awarded $30 million phosphorus-removal project at Egan WRP (19-415-3P) slated for 2028 — all driven by NPDES permits phasing in a 1.0 mg/L monthly-average and, later, 0.5 mg/L annual-geometric-mean total-phosphorus limit across the District's plants . MWRD's own budget-book phosphorus-permit tables give plant-specific effective dates that were previously only generalized in this document: Kirie's 1.0 mg/L monthly-average limit has been active since 2021; Calumet's 1.0 mg/L limit took effect in January 2024; both plants' 0.5 mg/L annual-geometric-mean limits are due by January 2030 . O'Brien's timeline runs on a similar but separately tracked schedule, per 2023 Monitoring & Research seminar materials: a Phosphorus Task Force formed in 2012, sequencing-batch-reactor (SBR) pilot testing began in 2019, and the 1.0 mg/L monthly-average permit limit became active in August 2021, with the 0.5 mg/L annual-geometric-mean limit targeted for 2030 — the same regulatory driver behind both the Battery D modifications already built and Battery E now und

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: City DWM — water main and sewer main replacement, current pace
text: Beyond the systemwide figures already in doc 05 and doc 06, DWM's own 2026 project list — a street-by-street guide published for residents — itemizes 75 water main replacement projects and 47 sewer main replacement project areas scheduled for calendar year 2026, spanning neighborhoods from the North Side (W. Dickens Ave., N. Artesian Ave.) to the South and Southwest Sides (S. Central Park Ave., W. 66th St.) . The City's 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Program (a newer edition than the 2022–2026 CIP already cited elsewhere in this archive) puts the five-year Water System allocation at $3.7 billion total — $1.7 billion from bond funds, $1.6 billion from state and federal loans/grants, and $322.8 million from water fees — broken into a $2.3 billion Water Distribution Program (63 percent of the total, targeting roughly 12 miles of new water main installed annually, integrated with lead-line and meter replacement), $482.9 million for the Jardine and Sawyer purification plants, $356.7 million for water engineering, $280.5 million for new meters, and $231.5 million for pumping-station operations .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: City DWM — lead service line replacement, current status
text: Doc 05 already covers the core numbers behind Chicago's lead-line problem and the "2076–77" completion-date controversy; this entry updates the funding and pace picture into 2026. The Water Distribution Program's stated aim is to reach the 10,000-replacement-per-year pace by 2027 that the U.S. EPA's proposed Lead and Copper Rule Improvements sets as the relevant benchmark for a system of Chicago's size — a pace the City had not yet reached as of 2025, when roughly 8,000 lines were replaced . EPA's WIFIA program lists a $336 million loan invitation specifically for Chicago's combined water-main-and-lead-service-line program ; separately, a March 2026 announcement confirmed the related, larger Chicago–Grand Prairie Water Commission financing (below) closed a $610 million WIFIA loan of its own — a different program serving the southwest suburbs rather than City lead-line replacement, and not to be confused with the $336 million Chicago-specific figure . In January 2026 the City announced its first four Lead Service Line Replacement Small Business Initiative (SBI) contracts, together worth just over $1.67 million, awarded to three small, minority- and women-owned local construction firms — a modest dollar figure relative to the program's multi-hundred-million-dollar scale, but notable as the City's first structured attempt to route lead-line replacement work through smaller local contractors rather than only its largest incumbent contractors .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
section: City DWM — Central Park Pumping Station and the steam-to-electric queue
text: Doc 05 documents the Central Park conversion's roughly $115 million estimated cost and could not identify the fourth station (beyond Central Park and Springfield Avenue) slated for steam-to-electric conversion. The City's 2025–2029 Capital Improvement Program resolves that gap: DWM's pumping-station-conversion program is Central Park (project 3343, essentially complete), Western Avenue (project 3342, design 2024–2025 at $2,671,876, construction 2027–2030 at a further $149,000,000, for a $151,671,876 total), and Mayfair (project 3258, design 2028–2030, construction 2030–2034, $107,874,000 total) — three stations beyond Springfield Avenue (already converted, per doc 05), one already largely built, one about to begin construction, and one not starting design work until the end of this decade . Central Park itself carries a project-book total of $113,579,827 across its full 2014–2025 design-and-construction life, funded through a mix of City bond issues and two Illinois EPA state-revolving-fund loans (0F57, $53,656,000; 0F60, $49,999,999) . Contractor project pages for the conversion (Helm Group as constructor, Aldridge Electric handling electrical/ductbank coordination) describe the work — refurbishing five existing pumps "like new," installing five new variable-frequency drives, a new medium-voltage electrical distribution system, and a small unit substation, all while keeping the century-old station operational throughout — in language consistent with a substantially finished project, though neither contractor page states an exact completion date, and this document did not independently confirm final commissioning against a DWM press release . A separate, smaller Cermak Pumping Station electrical improvements project (design complete 2024, $3,903,405; construction 2026–2028, $29,000,000, state-IEPA funded) is also underway but is described in the City's own CIP book as electrical upgrades rather than a full steam-to-electric conversion .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
text: This project — new construction rather than rehabilitation — will bring Lake Michigan water to six southwest-suburban communities (Joliet, Crest Hill, Channahon, Minooka, Shorewood, and Romeoville) currently reliant on a declining deep sandstone aquifer, replacing groundwater with the same Lake Michigan source Chicago itself draws from . Sources vary on the project's exact scale — reported variously as a 62-mile or 65-mile transmission network, and as a $1.24 billion project (implied by a $610 million WIFIA loan reported as 49 percent of total funding) or a $1.45 billion project in Engineering News-Record's framing — a discrepancy this document presents rather than resolves . Regional construction broke ground in 2024–2025; the U.S. EPA closed the Grand Prairie Water Commission's $610 million WIFIA loan in March 2026, and the first Chicago-side work package (the AWSP-01-01 Tunnel Extension) is scheduled for completion by the end of 2026, with regional member-community construction beginning in 2026 and the whole system targeted for completion around mid-2030 . On Chicago's side, the connection point is the Southwest Pumping Station (8422 S. Kedvale Ave.) already noted in doc 05, and the City's own capital-budget line item for its piece of the project — Southwest Pumping Station Tunnel Connection (project 43810) — is budgeted at $67,690,073 across design (2022–2026, $2,690,073, City-funded) and construction (2025–2026, $65,000,000, split between City bonds and City pay-as-you-go funds), consistent with the City's own program narrative describing "the Southwest Pumping Station's tunnel connection for the Grand Prairie Water Commission" as one of the three defining projects in its 2025–2029, $231.5 million Pumping Station Operations Program . Construction on the south side of Durkin Park, adjacent to the pumping station, is expected to run roughly two years starting in 2026, during which that section of the park will be closed while the rest remains open .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
text: Doc 02 already covers Brandon Road's purpose (a multi-layer Asian carp deterrent — electric barrier, acoustic deterrent, air-bubble curtain, flushing lock, and engineered channel — at the Brandon Road Lock & Dam in Joliet) and its roughly $1.2 billion estimated total cost; this entry updates construction progress into 2026. The project's first construction increment carries an estimated $274 million in federal funding plus $114 million in state funding (Illinois and Michigan) . Site preparation and channel excavation — the first construction contract — was completed in July 2025 . USACE awarded the second construction contract, valued at $113.8 million , to J.F. Brennan Company, Inc. of La Crosse, Wisconsin, on April 11, 2026 , covering the project's "leading-edge deterrents": an automated barge-clearing system, a bubble-curtain deterrent, an acoustic deterrent, leading-edge support facilities, and an upstream boat ramp . Later phases — still to be contracted — will add a flushing lock, walls, and the electric-barrier deterrent system; the Corps has described its approach as strategically layering these defenses across three separate construction increments rather than building all deterrent types simultaneously . Congressional appropriators secured $28 million in FY2026 federal funding for Brandon Road in a January 2026 spending package — described by Illinois's senators as "enough to fully fund the project for FY26" .

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
text: Doc 02 covers the Chicago Harbor Lock's basic facts (built 1936–1938, transferred to USACE operation in 1984, one of the two busiest locks in the United States) and flags that a dedicated cost figure for its rehabilitation history was hard to locate; this entry adds what surfaced in 2026-dated reporting. The lock has now undergone three consecutive winters of major structural maintenance — off-season work each year from roughly mid-November through mid-April, when the lock is closed to navigation — described by USACE's Chicago District Commander as "significant investments" rather than routine fixes, intended to keep the 85-plus-year-old structure "reliable and operable for the next 50 years" . The most recent phase (November 16, 2025 – April 14, 2026) placed new concrete and anchors into the chamber floor, reinforced the structure around the gates, replaced the gates' hydraulic operating systems with more efficient modern units, and continued replacing the lock's main generator, with pavement repairs deferred to the 2026–2027 off-season . This continues a longer rehabilitation history that includes a 2010–2011 sector-gate replacement (already in doc 02) and a 2020–2021 North Pier Stabilization project ($7 million, contractor Superior Construction) that drove new sheet-pile walls 50 feet deep, 100 anchored tie rods, and widened the pier from 35 to 70 feet after demolishing a 1938 utility tunnel . The lock's near-term maintenance funding is, as of this document's snapshot date, publicly contested. President Trump's FY2026 budget request proposed cutting the lock's operations-and-maintenance appropriation by more than 90 percent — from the roughly $3.85 million USACE itself had requested to less than $300,000 — prompting Illinois Senators Dick Durbin and Tammy Duckworth to file an amendment seeking $16.6 million (matching FY2024 levels) . USACE indicated it could cover the immediate FY2026 shortfall by reallocating funds originally earmarked for electrical rehabilitation work and drawing on roughly $1 million in leftover FY2025 operating funds, but warned this approach "creates risks in future years," since later budgets are calculated from an escalating five-year average that a severely cut FY2026 baseline would depress . Whether Congress's final FY2026 appropriation restored full funding, split the difference, or left the cut in place was not resolved in the sources reviewed for this document; see Open questions.

doc: What's Being Built Right Now (2026): The System's Active Capital Projects
text: - The exact relationship between MWRD's "Battery E Activated Sludge Facility" contract (21-092-3P, $325.0M engineer's estimate) and the June 18, 2026 board item publicly titled "sludge removal contract" ($409,420,079 to Michels Construction). This document treats them as the same contract based on matching dollar figures and dates in secondary reporting, but MWRD's own board minutes/resolution language for item 26-0432 was not directly obtained to confirm the title discrepancy is merely loose paraphrasing rather than two related-but-distinct awards. - Why the Battery E low bid came in roughly 26 percent above MWRD's own engineer's estimate — construction-cost inflation, a thin bidder pool for a $300–400M single contract, or scope growth between the 2026 budget book's estimate and the October 2025 bid documents were not distinguished in sources reviewed. - Whether Congress's final FY2026 appropriation restored, partially restored, or confirmed the proposed 90-percent cut to Chicago Harbor Lock's operations-and-maintenance budget. Sources reviewed document the Trump administration's proposal and the Illinois delegation's amendment attempt but not a confirmed final outcome as of this document's July 2026 snapshot date. - Reconciling the Chicago–Grand Prairie Water Commission pipeline's reported scale — 62 vs. 65 route-miles, and a $1.24B implied total (from the $610M/49% WIFIA-loan framing) vs. a directly reported $1.45B figure in Engineering News-Record — was not possible from the sources located; both are presented above rather than silently reconciled. - A confirmed commissioning/completion date for the Central Park Pumping Station's steam-to-electric conversion. text: --- title: "System overview — the complete picture" --- Chicago's Water System: The Complete Picture Synthesis of the detail documents in docs/ (now 15 — docs/15 on the Calumet-region lakes, incl. vanished Hyde Lake, was added after this synthesis). Compiled July 2026. Every figure below is traceable to a cited source in the detail docs; citations here point to the doc (and where useful, its numbered sources) rather than re-numbering the bibliography. ---

doc: System overview — the complete picture
text: Chicago drinks from Lake Michigan and flushes to the Mississippi, and the whole system exists to keep those two facts from colliding. Raw lake water enters through two offshore intake cribs, falls down shafts, and flows by gravity through tunnels under the lakebed to two purification plants — Jardine, the world's largest conventional filtration plant, and Sawyer — which together are rated at about 2.1 billion gallons a day and actually deliver roughly 750 million to a billion gallons daily to 2.7 million Chicagoans and about 120 suburbs (docs/03, 04). Twelve city pumping stations push that water through some 4,300–4,400 miles of mains to the tap (docs/05). What goes down the drain enters a different system entirely: the city's ~4,400 miles of combined sewers — one pipe for sewage and stormwater alike, a design choice made in 1855 — drain into 560 miles of interceptor sewers owned by a separate government, the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District, which carries the flow to seven treatment plants anchored by Stickney, the largest wastewater plant on Earth (docs/06, 07, 11). Treated effluent goes into the Chicago River system, which since January 2, 1900 has flowed backwards — away from the lake, down the 28-mile Sanitary & Ship Canal, over a 40-foot drop at Lockport, and on via the Des Plaines and Illinois Rivers to the Mississippi (docs/02). When storms overload the combined sewers, 110 miles of deep rock tunnels and three giant reservoirs — the $3.9-billion Tunnel and Reservoir Plan, "Deep Tunnel" — catch up to 17.5 billion gallons of overflow for later treatment; only when that fills do the outfalls spill to the river, and only in the worst storms do the lakefront gates open and send the river, briefly, back into the lake (docs/09, 10, 14). ---

The two halves of the system are strikingly mismatched in age and symmetry. The drinking-water side runs far below its engineering ceiling: 2.1 BGD of treatment capacity against ~750 MGD of average demand, headroom deliberately built in for heat waves and fire flow (the all-time peak day, 1.51 billion gallons, was set July 27, 1955 — before Jardine even existed) (docs/04). The wastewater side runs the other way: Stickney alone can treat more than the entire drinking-water system typically delivers, because it must swallow not just sewage but the rain that falls on 300 square miles of pavement. And the storm side dwarfs both: North Branch and Racine Avenue together can pump ~3 billion gallons a day of combined sewage into the river — roughly triple what all twelve drinking-water stations deliver to taps on an average day — which is the arithmetic of a combined-sewer city in one sentence (docs/05, 07, 08). ---

doc: System overview — the complete picture
text: Start two and a half miles off North Avenue Beach, thirty-odd feet below the surface, where a drop of Lake Michigan slips through a screened port in the William E. Dever Crib . It falls down the crib's central shaft and rides gravity — no pumps yet — through the 16-foot-diameter Chicago Avenue tunnel, bored through limestone as much as 200 feet beneath the lakebed, 11.5 miles to the shore intake basin at the Jardine Water Purification Plant on its man-made peninsula north of Navy Pier (docs/03, 04). Low-lift pumps raise it about 25 feet into the treatment train: traveling screens, alum coagulation, flocculation, settling that strips ~90% of remaining particulates, then a polish through one of 96 swimming-pool-sized sand filters, then chlorine, fluoride (0.7 mg/L), and a phosphate blend that coats the inside of old pipes to hold lead in place. About eight hours after it left the lake, the drop leaves the plant (docs/04). Gravity — Chicago keeps essentially no elevated storage — carries it through the finished-water tunnels to a district pumping station; on the North Side that might be the 1928 Thomas Jefferson Pumping Station in Lincoln Square, whose motors push it into the grid at 38–42 psi (docs/05). It travels a few miles of the city's ~4,400 miles of main, up a service line (with luck, not one of the ~400,000 that are still lead), and out a kitchen tap in Ravenswood (docs/05). Down the drain, it changes governments. The house lateral drops it into a City of Chicago combined sewer under the street — the same pipe that takes the gutter's rainwater — where it flows by gravity to a connection with the MWRD's North Side Intercepting Sewer (docs/06). At the riverbank at Lawrence Avenue the interceptor delivers it to the Art Deco North Branch Pumping Station , which in dry weather quietly lifts sanitary flow onward so it can run downhill again to the O'Brien Water Reclamation Plant in Skokie (docs/08). O'Brien takes about twelve hours to do its work: screens, grit removal, primary settling, activated-sludge aeration, final settling, and then a pass under 896 ultraviolet lamps — the largest UV disinfection installation in the world (docs/12). (O'Brien keeps none of its sludge; solids are pumped to Stickney for digestion, joining a solids-consolidation chain that also drains Kirie via Egan and Lemont — docs/11, 13.) doc: System overview — the complete picture
text: Rain is forecast, so MWRD acts first: it opens up the Lockport gates and holds the lakefront gates shut, drawing the canals down to create roughly five billion gallons of empty channel — the cheapest storage it owns (docs/14, sources 14, 39). The storm arrives. Street grates and 200,000-plus catch basins feed the city's combined sewers, which were sized generations ago for about two inches of rain in a day (docs/06). Within the first hour of hard rain the interceptors are full, and at hundreds of MWRD connecting structures the regulating weirs begin diverting excess down the drop shafts — vertical plunge shafts, air vented up one side, water spiraling down the other — into the TARP tunnels 150–300 feet below the rivers (docs/09). The tunnels' 2.32 billion gallons fill fast; gates open and flow passes onward to the reservoirs: Mainstream and Des Plaines tunnel water to McCook , Calumet water to Thornton , O'Hare-area water to Majewski (docs/10). If the storm keeps building, the big relief pumps come on — before TARP is full, which surprises people. In the July 2, 2023 storm, McCook was only 17% full at 2 a.m., yet CSOs began at 8:19 a.m. and the North Branch Pumping Station started pushing combined sewage into the river at 9:44 a.m. MWRD's own explanation: when TARP is filling rapidly, North Branch and Racine Avenue — 1,500 and 3,125 cfs respectively — are activated deliberately to pull sewer levels down and prevent a pressure surge through the neighborhood pipes the moment the tunnels top out. Most of the region's ~370 CSO outfalls spill passively over fixed weirs; these two pump on purpose (docs/08, source 6; docs/07, 14). As tunnels and reservoirs saturate, gravity CSOs open along every reach — the Chicago River, the Cal-Sag, the Des Plaines — through the City's 184 permitted outfalls and the suburbs' remainder (docs/06, 14). The last resort comes when the waterway itself threatens to overtop into basements and streets: canal levels rise above the lake's, and MWRD opens the Wilmette sluice gates , then the Chicago River Controlling Works , then the O'Brien Lock — reversing the reversal and sending diluted sewage into Lake Michigan. The record release was 11.05 billion gallons over September 13–16, 2008 (remnants of Hurricane Ike); April 2013 came second at 10.7 billion (docs/14, source 12). In July 2023 all three points opened, and over 1.1 billion gallons reached the lake (docs/09). Then the slow unwinding. The Mainstream Pumping Station lifts McCook's

section: 4. How it was built — seven eras
text: 1855–1889: Chesbrough's sewers and the raised city. After cholera killed 1,424 Chicagoans in 1854 (a 53.9-per-1,000 death rate), the legislature created a Board of Sewerage Commissioners on February 14, 1855; its engineer, Ellis S. Chesbrough, delivered the first comprehensive sewer plan for any American city that December. Because the city sat barely above lake level, the sewers went in nearly at the surface and the streets were raised four to fourteen feet around them — buildings jacked up on thousands of jackscrews, hotels operating mid-lift. Fifty-four miles were in service by 1861 (death rate: down to 18.8), 888 miles by 1892 — all draining, fatally, to the river and thence the lake that supplied the drinking water. Chesbrough's answer was to chase clean water offshore: the Two-Mile Crib and lake tunnel of 1865–67 ($380,784), a template repeated eight times through 1935. The 1871 I&M Canal "deep cut" reversed the river's flow briefly and feebly; the storm of August 1885 pushed the sewage field out to the crib and made a permanent fix politically unstoppable (docs/01, 03). 1889–1910: the Sanitary District and the reversal. The Sanitary District of Chicago was chartered May 29, 1889 and ratified by referendum that November, 70,958 to 242. From "Shovel Day" (September 3, 1892) some 8,500 men dug the 28-mile Main Channel — 41 million cubic yards of earth and rock, $23.7 million, machinery so novel the trade press called it the "Chicago school of earth moving," later exported to Panama. On January 2, 1900, ahead of a Missouri injunction, trustees quietly cut the dam and the Chicago River began flowing backwards for good. The Lockport Controlling Works (1899) and Powerhouse (1907) fixed the downstream end; the North Shore Channel (1910) extended dilution to the northern lakefront (docs/02, 01). 1900–1930: interceptors and the first treatment plants. Dilution alone couldn't protect the lakefront north of the river mouth, so from 1906 the District built intercepting sewers along the shore, feeding a pumping station at Lawrence Avenue (1907; rebuilt 1928–30 as the present North Branch Pumping Station ). The Cal-Sag Channel (1922) reversed the Calumet rivers, and the Supreme Court's diversion rulings of 1925–1930 — capping the lake water available for dilution — forced actual treatment: Calumet (1922, the first plant), North Side/O'Brien (1928), with the 95th Street PS (1925) lifting Calumet-area flow. On the drinking-water side, chlorination began experimental

text: The single most confused fact about this system: two separate governments run it , with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers as the third player on the waterway. DWM is a city department funded by water/sewer rates; MWRD is an independent district with its own elected board covering nearly all of Cook County. The boundary runs, literally, at the pipe flange where a city sewer meets a District interceptor (docs/06). doc: System overview — the complete picture
text: The ten most interesting unresolved figures and disputes, distilled from the detail docs' Open Questions sections: 1. Who conceived Deep Tunnel? Vinton Bacon (MSD superintendent 1962–70) is credited as originator in several accounts; Frank Dalton's own MWRD obituary says he "conceived and led" it. The 1968 Harza-Bauer report is the earliest concrete design. Genuinely contested, not just under-researched (docs/09). 2. What did TARP really cost? $1.223B (1972 estimate) vs. $2.33B (Phase I contracts, nominal) vs. $3.86B (MWRD's cumulative ledger through 2025) vs. GAO's $10.2–12.5B full-program projections of 1981–83. The figures bundle financing, inflation, and Phase II differently and no source reconciles them (docs/09). 3. How many times has the river reversed into the lake? MWRD's own 1985–2021 table counts 22 years with events totaling ~51.3 billion gallons; press accounts attribute "27 reversals / 44 billion gallons" and "about 50 events" to the same agency. Counting rules (gates vs. locks, dates vs. events) are undocumented, and no post-2021 ledger exists (docs/14). 4. When was the Dunne Crib built — 1909 or 1911? Wikipedia and most histories say 1909; the City's own 1933 and 1956 engineering reports say 1911. The archive carries both (docs/03, 04). 5. How many died in the 1909 crib fire? Estimates run 47 (early count) to ~60 (coroner's jury) to 67–70 (press); the contractor never fully documented its temporary laborers, so Chicago's worst forgotten disaster has no fixed toll (docs/03). 6. When was Jardine finished — 1964 or 1968? Trade press and the Encyclopedia of Chicago say full operation fall 1964; ENR, Structurae, Wikipedia and Gizmodo all say 1968 ("13 years, $105 million"). Probably online-1964/complete-1968, but no dedication record confirms it (docs/04). 7. Racine Avenue Pumping Station: 1939 or 1964? MWRD's own press release says built 1939; South Side Weekly describes a 1964 station that "replaced" a smaller one. Rebuild, expansion, or error — unresolved (docs/07, 11). 8. When did North Branch PS get its five 300-cfs pumps? The 1970s demonstration project installed only ~48 cfs of pumps at the site; the 1,500-cfs bank first appears, as pre-existing, in a 2018 rehab contract. No document dates the expansion — the TARP era is only a hypothesis (docs/08). 9. What can the Mainstream Pumping Station actually pump? text: The detail docs ( docs/ ) — each with a facilities table, construction narrative, upgrade timeline, current operations, incidents, open questions, and full citations: - docs/01 — Early sewerage history : Chesbrough's 1855 plan, raising the city on jackscrews, the I&M Canal reversals, the debunked 1885 epidemic myth, and the politics that created the Sanitary District. - docs/02 — River reversal & canals : the Main Channel dig, the stealth opening of January 1900, Lockport's powerhouse and gates, the Supreme Court diversion wars, and the carp barriers. - docs/03 — Intake cribs : all nine cribs ever built, crib-tender life, the 1909 fire, the 1998 Harrison tunnel collapse, and which two still work. - docs/04 — Water purification plants : Jardine and Sawyer end to end, from 1912 chlorination and 1956 fluoridation to PFAS monitoring and the orthophosphate switch. - docs/05 — Water pumping & distribution : the twelve pumping stations from "Old Sally" (1854) to Lexington (1992), steam-to-electric conversions, and the lead-service-line problem in full. - docs/10 — TARP reservoirs : Majewski, Thornton, and McCook — quarry economics, grout curtains, the consent decree, and the July 2026 record fills and odor crisis. - docs/11 — Stickney WRP : the world's largest treatment plant, Imhoff tanks to Ostara phosphorus reactors, the biosolids empire, and the disinfection equity fight on the canal. -
